metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for Photochemical, spectroscopic and electrochemical properties of new phenothiazine photosensitizer
metadata.publisher: Cherkasy National University

additional_descriptions.description: Dataset belongs to the characterization of the photochemical, spectroscopic and electrochemical properties of newly synthesized phenothiazinium photosensitizer. It includes characterization of all synthesized compounds using HPLC, NMR, UV-Vis spectroscopy, MS/MS, elemental analysis. The electrochemical properties were investigated by means of cyclic voltammetry on platinum working electrode in bulk and also in thin-layer spectroelectrochemical cell accompanied with semitransparent platinum electrode. Absorption spectra in UV-Vis range and emission spectra (fluorescence) of compounds and their reduction products were recorded using in-situ UV-Vis-FL-spectroelectrochemistry. Theoretical calculations involved the density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent (TD) DFT calculations implemented within the Gaussian 09 program. The B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) method was used for geometry optimization and electronic structure determination. The Solvation Model Density was employed in TDDFT/B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) calculations of absorption spectra to simulate the methanol and acetonitrile solvents effect.

Characterization of compounds: by chromatography HPLC, NMR, UV-Vis spectroscopy, MS/MS, elemental analysis. The electrochemical properties: cyclic voltammetry, spectroelectrochemistry
Properties of molecules at different redox state: in-situ UV-Vis-FL-spectroelectrochemistry (absorption spectra in UV-Vis range and emission spectra -fluorescence).
Theoretical calculations: the density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent (TD) DFT calculations.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Green Valorisation of Bio-wastes – Electrochemical "One-pot" Reductive Amination of Furfural on Graphite Electrode in Water - experimental dataset
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains primary experimental data for the article such as NMR spectra. New way of reductive amination of furfural (extracted from lignocellulosic bio-wastes) is presented, using electrochemical approach, where in situ formed imines are directly reduced on carbon electrode in basic buffers to (furyl)-substituted vicinal diamines identified by NMR spectroscopy. "One-pot" process, avoiding isolation of intermediates, without organic solvents and metal catalysts.
NMR spectra recorded on a Bruker Avance Neo 500 spectrometer in CDCl3 solutions. For details see the article and its supporting information file.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for article - Monomeric human CD4 clusters in the immunological synapse
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset contains the original dataset for the article "Monomeric human CD4 clusters in the immunological synapse".

CD4 enhances the sensitivity of T cells to rare and weak antigens, but it has remained unclear whether it can form dimers before or during T-cell activation. In this study, we employed fluorescence anisotropy to investigate the oligomerisation state of CD4 in both non-stimulated and stimulated Jurkat cells. We show that CD4 predominantly exists as a monomer in non-stimulated cells. Native protein displayed weak assembly into higher-order structures, whereas the variant lacking the extracellular domain did not. Palmitoylation of CD4 was found to prevent tight aggregation of the protein. Upon antigen stimulation, we observed increased ordering of CD4 within, but not outside, the immunological synapse and this process was dependent on palmitoylation. The accumulation of CD4 in defined membrane regions affected its mobility, suggesting that surface membrane morphology regulates the organisation of signalling receptors and, consequently, their function.

Jurkat cells were transiently transfected and where applicable, conjugated to Raji B cells, attached to coverslip and imaged on confocal scanning microscop by FRET and FRAP methods. FRET was measured using TSCPC module from PicoQuant (PicoHarp 300, Microtime 200). FRAP was measured on TCS SP8 Leica microscop with LAS X software.

FRAP_usedData_origTif.zip contains original time series of FRAP measurements, extracted from the native .lif format by home-made scripts in Fiji macro. The .tif series can be opened with an open-source software Fiji or ImageJ.

 

FRET.zip contains
- Directory with original .pt3 files, which can be opened using commercial PicoQuant software.

- List of G-values recorded for each measurement.

- Directory with references: decay of diluted Atto488 for G-factor computation and IRF.

- Directory with extracted .dat files of parallel (HH) and perpendicular (HV) components of GFP fluorescence decay.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Experimental data for electron-induced dissociation of iron tetracarbonyl acrolein
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset contains experimental data on the electron beam–induced dissociation of the iron tetracarbonyl acrolein molecule, which is a potential focused electron beam–induced deposition (FEBID) precursor. The experiments were performed using a trochoidal electron monochromator coupled to a quadrupole mass spectrometer (TEM–QMS) setup and the Cluster Beam (CLUB) apparatus.

The TEM–QMS setup was primarily used to study the dissociative electron attachment (DEA) process with higher electron beam energy resolution and to record anionic mass spectra. In contrast, the CLUB setup was used to study dissociative ionisation and to record cationic mass spectra. In addition, high-resolution anionic mass spectra were measured using the CLUB setup.

The dataset contains ion-yield curves for anionic species with masses of 84, 112, 140, 168, and 196 Da measured using the TEM–QMS setup. It also includes the complete dataset of anions produced in the 0–12 eV incident electron energy range and cationic fragments measured in the 5–80 eV range using the CLUB setup.

Two different experimental setups were used to perform the experiments. An experimental setup with a trochoidal electron monochromator with a quadrupole mass spectrometer (TEM-QMS) was used to study the anionic fragment formation as a function of the incident electron energy between 0 and 12 eV due to the dissociative electron attachment (DEA) process. An effusive molecular beam of the sample was made to interact with the incoming electron beam, and the produced anions were mass analysed as a function of the incident electron beam energy using a quadrupole mass spectrometer. Whereas for the CLUB set-up, the molecular sample was introduced as a background gas and interacted with the electron beam of controlled energy. Both of the produced anionic and cationic mass fragments were analysed using a high-resolution reflectron time of flight (RTOF) mass spectrometer.

This dataset contains three folders, each of which contains data in ASCII format with '.txt' file extension. Two folders correspond to the CLUB experimental set-up and one corresponds to the TEM-QMS experimental set-up.

 

The folder 'RAW_Data_CLUB_setup' contains two datasets, each of them are data matrix where the columns correspond to the incident electron energies and rows correspond to the mass of the fragments. The file 'Mass_spectra_negative_ion_matrix_0_12eV.txt' contains the information (mass and energy) of the negative ion mass fragments in the energy range 0 to 12 eV, whereas file 'Mass_spectra_positive_ion_matrix_5_80eV.txt' consists of the information about positive ion mass fragments in the energy range 5 to 80 eV. All data related to the CLUB experiment can, in principle, be obtained from these two datasets.

 

In addition, another folder called 'CLUB_data' contains data for cationic mass in the region of interest, energy and mass range. The file 'cationic_mass_spectra_70eV.txt' is the cationic mass spectra recorded at 70 eV constant incident electron energy and 40 to 230 mass to charge (m/z) range. And the file 'Energy_integrated_cationic_mass_spectra_69-71eV.txt' contains data for energy integrated mass spectra from 69 to 71 eV energy and 53.5 to 58.5 m/z range.

 In the folder 'TEM-QMS_data', there are five datasets, each containing the data for negative-ion yield in the energy range 0 – 12 eV for mass to charge ratios 84, 112, 140, 168 and 196, respectively.

metadata.title: Single-cell RNA sequencing atlas of Alzheimer’s disease cerebral organoid development
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset constitutes a single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) atlas of human cerebral organoid development designed to characterize transcriptional changes associated with Alzheimer's disease (AD). The atlas comprises organoids derived from nine independent cell lines, including three wild-type (healthy control) lines and six lines derived from Alzheimer's disease patients or AD-associated cell lines.

Organoids were profiled across eight developmental time points (day 0, day 6, day 12, day 18, day 30, day 60, day 90, and day 120) to capture longitudinal changes in cellular composition, gene expression programs, and disease-associated trajectories. Single-cell libraries were prepared using a combinatorial indexing–based Evercode Whole Transcriptome (v3 Mega) protocol from Parse Biosciences. The dataset provides a comprehensive resource for studying human neurodevelopment, Alzheimer's disease progression, and genotype-dependent transcriptional dynamics at single-cell resolution.

Human cerebral organoids were generated from nine independent cell lines, including three wild-type control lines and six lines derived from Alzheimer's disease patients or AD-associated sources. Organoids were collected at eight defined developmental stages (day 0, day 6, day 12, day 18, day 30, day 60, day 90, and day 120). Single-cell suspensions were prepared from each line and time point to construct a comprehensive single-cell atlas describing developmental and disease-associated transcriptional changes in cerebral organoids.

Single-cell RNA sequencing libraries were prepared using a combinatorial indexing–based Evercode Whole Transcriptome (v3 Mega) protocol from Parse Biosciences. Libraries were sequenced on an Illumina sequencing platform using paired-end reads. Raw sequencing reads were processed using standard scRNA-seq workflows: reads were aligned to the human reference genome (GRCh38), unique molecular identifiers (UMIs) were used to generate gene–cell count matrices, and standard quality control filtering was applied. Processed data are provided in in the form of a raw, unfiltered expression matrix.

metadata.title: Single-cell RNA sequencing of isogenic cerebral organoid models of early Alzheimer’s disease development
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset comprises single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data generated from human cerebral organoids derived from three isogenic cell lines that differ only by disease-relevant genetic variants. The experimental design includes a wild-type control line, a line carrying a mutant APOE4 allele associated with sporadic Alzheimer's disease, and a line carrying a mutant PSEN1 allele associated with familial Alzheimer's disease.

Organoids were profiled at multiple early developmental time points (day 0, day 6, day 9, and day 12) to capture transcriptional changes associated with the earliest stages of Alzheimer's disease–related phenotypes. Single-cell libraries were prepared using a combinatorial indexing–based Evercode Whole Transcriptome (v2 Mini) protocol from Parse Biosciences. The dataset includes raw unprocessed gene–cell expression matrices suitable for analyses of early neurodevelopment, genotype-specific transcriptional effects, and comparative single-cell studies of Alzheimer's disease models.

Human cerebral organoids were generated from three isogenic cell lines: a wild-type control, an APOE4 mutant line modeling sporadic Alzheimer's disease risk, and a PSEN1 mutant line modeling familial Alzheimer's disease. Organoids were harvested at defined early developmental stages (day 0, day 6, day 9, and day 12). Single-cell suspensions were prepared from each genotype and time point to investigate genotype-dependent transcriptional changes during early organoid development.

Single-cell RNA sequencing libraries were prepared using a combinatorial indexing–based Evercode Whole Transcriptome (v2 Mini) protocol from Parse Biosciences. Libraries were sequenced on an Illumina sequencing platform using paired-end reads. Raw sequencing data were processed using standard scRNA-seq workflows: reads were aligned to the human reference genome, unique molecular identifiers (UMIs) were used to generate gene–cell count matrices, and standard quality control filtering was applied to remove low-quality cells and lowly expressed genes. Processed data are provided as a raw, unprocessed expression matrix.

metadata.title: Single-cell RNA sequencing of cerebral organoid–glioblastoma co-cultivation models
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset comprises single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data generated from an in vitro co-cultivation model combining human cerebral organoids (CO) and glioblastoma (GBM) cells. The experimental design includes control samples of separately cultivated cerebral organoid cultures and separately cultivated glioblastoma spheroids, as well as co-cultivation samples generated using three distinct GBM cell lines (U3013, U3047, and U3118).

Single-cell libraries were prepared using a combinatorial indexing–based Evercode Whole Transcriptome v3 (100,000-cell scale) protocol from Parse Biosciences, enabling transcriptomic profiling of large numbers of cells without physical compartmentalization. The dataset is intended to capture cellular composition and transcriptional states arising from interactions between GBM cells and cerebral organoid–derived neural cells at single-cell resolution. It includes raw unfiltered gene–cell expression matrices suitable for downstream analyses such as cell type annotation, tumor–neural interaction studies, and comparative transcriptomic analyses across experimental conditions.

Human cerebral organoids were co-cultivated with glioblastoma cells from three distinct Uppsala-derived GBM lines (U3013, U3047, U3118). Control samples included separately cultured cerebral organoids and separately cultured GBM spheroids. Single-cell suspensions were generated from each condition to profile cellular heterogeneity and interactions at single-cell resolution.

Single-cell RNA-seq libraries were prepared using a combinatorial indexing–based Evercode Whole Transcriptome v3 (100,000-cell scale) protocol from Parse Biosciences. Libraries were sequenced on an Illumina platform with paired-end reads. Raw reads were processed using standard scRNA-seq pipelines: reads were aligned to the human reference genome (GRCh38), UMIs were collapsed to generate gene–cell count matrices, and standard QC filtering was applied to remove low-quality cells.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for our contribution to COLUMBUS -An Efficient and General Program Package for Ground and Excited State Computations Including Spin−Orbit Couplings and Dynamics
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The dataset contains inputs and outputs of calculations performed by us for a contribution to the paper
on the Columbus program system. 
In particular, CASSCF, MRCI and MRAQCC results for silaethylene in its low lying states are covered.
The COLUMBUS program system provides the
tools for performing high-level multireference (MR) computations,
including the multireference configuration interaction (MRCI)
method and its multireference averaged quadratic coupled cluster
(MR-AQCC) extension, allowing computations on a wide range of
fascinating atomic and molecular systems, including the treatment
of open-shells and complicated excited state phenomena. The
inclusion of spin−orbit coupling (SOC) directly within the MRCI
step enables the description of systems containing heavy elements,
such as lanthanides and actinides, whose properties are strongly
influenced by SOC.
Quantum chemical calculations - CASSCF, MRCI using the Columbus program and nonadiabatic MD simulations using Newton-X and Columbus
Human readable inputs and outputs of Columbus and Newton-X programs.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Quantum Chemical and Trajectory Surface Hopping Molecular Dynamics Study of Iodine-Based BODIPY Photosensitizer
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The dataset contains underlying data for a computational study of I-­BODIPY (2- ­ethyl- ­4,4- ­difluoro- ­6 ,7- ­diiodo-­1,3- ­dimethyl- ­4 -­bora-­3a,4a- ­diaza-­s-­indacene), 
which has been carried out to investigate its key photophysical properties as a potential triplet photosensitizer capable of generating singlet
oxygen. Trajectories for a nuclear ensemble approach absorption spectra simulations and mixed quantum-­classical trajec-
tory surface hopping (TSH) molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are included in the dataset. Two bright states in the visible spectrum
of I­ -­BODIPY have been found, exhibiting a redshift of the main peak with respect to unsubstituted BODIPY caused by the
iodine substituents. Excited-­state MD simulations including both non-­adiabatic effects and SOCs have been performed to
investigate the relaxation processes in I-­BODIPY after its photoexcitation to the S1 state. The TSH MD simulations revealed
that intersystem crossings occur on a time scale comparable to internal conversions and that after an initial phase of triplet
population growth a "saturation" is reached where the ratio of the net triplet to singlet populations is about 4:1. The calculated
triplet quantum yield of 0.85 is in qualitative agreement with the previously reported experimental singlet oxygen generation
yield of 0.99 ± 0.06.
TDDFT calculations and non-adiabatic  molecular dynamics simulations
Dataset contains human-readable inputs and outputs of quantum chemical programs Turbomole and Newton-X

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for A Descriptor Is All You Need: Accurate Machine Learning of Nonadiabatic Coupling Vectors
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Nonadiabatic couplings (NACs) play a crucial role in modeling photochemical and photophysical processes with methods such as the widely used fewest-switches surface hopping (FSSH). There is, therefore, a strong incentive to machine learn NACs for accelerating simulations. However, this is challenging due to NACs' vectorial, double-valued character and the singularity near a conical intersection seam. For the first time, we design NAC-specific descriptors based on our domain expertise and show that they allow learning NACs with never-before-reported accuracy of R2 exceeding 0.99. The key to success is also our new ML phase-correction procedure. We demonstrate the efficiency and robustness of our approach on a prototypical example of fully ML-driven FSSH simulations of fulvene targeting the SA-2-CASSCF(6,6) electronic structure level. This ML-FSSH dynamics leads to an accurate description of S1 decay while reducing error bars by allowing the execution of a large ensemble of trajectories. Our approach is generalizable to more states as we demonstrate for a three-state ML-FSSH simulation of methylenimmonium cation. Our implementations are available in open-source MLatom. Here, we provide all the trained models and data used within this study.
The methodology is based on state-of-the-art machine learning models and the standard workflow of a nonadiabatic molecular dynamics study.
Data set of machine learning models, trajectories and supporting information for: A Descriptor Is All You Need: Accurate Machine Learning of Nonadiabatic Coupling Vectors

metadata.title: Supporting data for "Verification and experimental validation of neutral atom beam source produced by L-PBF"
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: This archive contains the supporting data files, images, documented videos and scripts for the manuscript titled "Verification and experimental validation of neutral atom beam source produced by L-PBF" by Vineet Kumar, Niklas Vilhelm Lausti, Peter Kúš, Adam Jelínek, Ivan Hudák, David Motyčka, Petr Dohnal, Radek Plašil, Jiří Hajnyš (Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, VŠB–Technical University of Ostrava, Ostrava, Czech Republic), and Michal Hejduk (Charles University, Faculty of Mathematics and Physics, Department of Surface and Plasma Science, Prague 8, Czech Republic). This work supports the development of the experimental setup for the electron–ion trapping experiment (EiTEx) described in https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.06537, with the associated data available through the Národní Repozitář: https://data.narodni-repozitar.cz/general/datasets/74f00-v0b78.

The data presented in this repository were generated during the experimental characterization of a 3D-printed oven assembly, including measurements of oven tube current–dependent temperature profile for atomic-beam production and atomic-fluorescence characterization. The repository also contains vacuum chamber-pressure data recorded during oven operation (supplementary material), as well as the COMSOL Multiphysics 6.1 simulation data referenced in the associated article. In addition, it includes scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDS) data for the morphological and compositional analysis of the 3D-printed surface. CAD schematics and photographs of the setup are also provided. The repository further contains video files showing atomic-fluorescence confirmation at different positions inside the vacuum chamber, namely at the source side (oven-aperture region) and at the trapping-region side. All measurements were performed using the equipment and infrastructure available in Optická laboratoř, Katedra fyziky povrchů a plazmatu, Skupina fyziky plazmatu a modelování, MFF UK, where the complete experimental setup is installed. Given the developing laboratory environment, the experiment was iteratively assembled and optimized, which is reflected in the range of operational regimes captured in the dataset. The accompanying processing code is provided together with this repository. Data were recorded using the laboratory acquisition hardware and subsequently processed both online and offline using custom Python scripts executed primarily via Google Colab. The ARTIQ controller was used for real-time monitoring—particularly wavelength scanning—while Grafana supported visual monitoring and InfluxDB v2 handled data management and storage. Details of the ARTIQ control code implemented in this experiment can be found in the publicly accessible GitLab repository (https://gitlab.mff.cuni.cz/kfpp/uplp-public/atomic-oven). The recorded video files included here have also been edited together with supplementary images and made publicly accessible on YouTube for broader outreach (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e7vYzzxTerw, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_MnCSATBPsA). All scripts are included to ensure that figures and processed data products can be fully reproduced from the raw measurement files. Ethical Considerations: No animal or human subjects were involved in this research, and no personal or private data were collected or used. All computational and experimental procedures follow standard practices in the field to ensure reliability.

The archive is structured into five primary folders, along with an additional supplementary folder containing extra data files. Although the supplementary folder is not used in the present paper, it may be utilized in a future publication, and the entire archive can therefore be cited accordingly. Each folder contains the corresponding data files together with figures illustrating plots or images of the experiment. The folders are named according to the figure numbering used in the associated article: Fig 1 – This contains images and schematic views of the experimental setup, including the CAD model. Fig 2 – Contains the SEM image data files (.hdr format) and accompanying processed files, with code about edge detection algorithm for crack density analysis. Fig 3 – This folder contains the energy-versus-count files and the corresponding spectral profiles of the detected elements, as well as the files reporting the percentage composition. Accompanying code is provided for processing these datasets. Fig 4 – This folder contains the COMSOL heat-simulation file (in .mph format), together with the generated data files representing the heat-distribution map. Accompanying code is provided for processing and analyzing the simulation results. In another sub-folder 'Experimental' – it contains oven temperature as a function of current for different holding times. Fig 5 – This contains images, and video files related to the neutral atom fluorescence. Supplementary material – This folder contains the atomic-source characterization data, including vacuum-chamber pressure measurements as a function of oven current (temperature), along with the corresponding processed figures and analysis scripts. It also includes datasets and plots showing the temporal evolution of chamber pressure during different oven-heating sequences. In addition, the folder includes the SEM image dataset of the oven-assembly heat-shield aperture (in .hdr format), the processed figure demonstrating the four-step circularity evaluation, and the code used to perform this analysis.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Raw data for the manuscript entitled  "Spectroelectrochemical sensing of electrogenerated chlorine dioxide"
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset provides raw data on the manuscript entitled  "Spectroelectrochemical sensing of electrogenerated chlorine dioxide". The dataset involves cyclic voltammetric data and associated in-situ UV-Vis-NIR aborption spectroscopic data acquired for charge transfer reactions of ferrocenemethanol used as the standard redox-active probe and chlorine dioxide as the target analyte electrogenerated from sodium chlorite.

Cyclic voltammetric measurements were performed employing potentiostat  Autolab PGSTAT30, Metrohm.
Absorption UV-Vis-NIR spectra were recorded by diode array detector spectrometer Agilent 8453.

Cyclic voltammetric measurements were performed in the staircase regime at the polarization rate of 3 mV/s and 1 mV resolution. 
Absorption UV-Vis-NIR spectra were recorded in 190 nm to 1100 nm range with 1 nm resolution.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for the article: Spontaneous Room-Temperature Solid-State Reaction at the MoS2/Ti Interface: Implications for Contact Engineering
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset supports the article "Spontaneous Room-Temperature Solid-State Reaction at the MoS2/Ti Interface: Implications for Contact Engineering." Direct exfoliation of layered materials onto clean metal surfaces under ultra-high vacuum (UHV) provides pristine, oxide-free interfaces while avoiding the disorder introduced by high-energy metal deposition. Although metal-assisted exfoliation is well established for noble metals such as gold, its applicability to more reactive metals remains insufficiently explored.
To address this gap, we exfoliated large-area MoS2 onto titanium under UHV and characterized the resulting heterostructures using photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The dataset includes all experimental and computational results reported in the publication, including stitched in-situ optical images, photoelectron spectra, Raman maps, AFM and KPFM data, and DFT outputs.
These combined analyses reveal pronounced room-temperature chemical reactivity at the MoS2/Ti interface: the first MoS2 monolayer decomposes to metallic Mo, while non-stoichiometric TiSx forms spontaneously upon physical contact. Subsequent air exposure leads to oxidation of metallic Mo and interfacial Ti, disintegration of TiSx, and partial reformation of MoS2. In contrast, bilayer MoS2 suppresses oxidation, and trilayer and thicker flakes effectively protect the interface.
Raman spectroscopy, optical microscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM, KPFM), density functional theory simulations, photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS)
The data are provided in .png, .text, .dat, .xlsx, or .gwy files. All the datasets are located in folders named after the figures (eg, Figure 1, Figure 2, etc.).

metadata.title: Data k funkčnímu vzorku oběžného kola čerpadla vyrobené metodou DED-LB/W
metadata.publisher: COMTES FHT

additional_descriptions.description: Data postihují podklady k funkčnímu vzorku oběžného kola čerpadla vyrobeného aditivní metodou DED-LB/W. Komponenta byla zhotovena z niklové slitiny MoNiCr a její účel spočívá v ověření možnosti výroby dílů s požadovanou geometrií a materiálovou integritou pro aplikace s výrazným tepelným zatížením. Funkční vzorek slouží jako demonstrátor technologické proveditelnosti výroby tvarově náročných komponent pro tepelně namáhané aplikace pomocí aditivních technologií, se zaměřením na vnitřní homogenitu struktury a stabilitu procesních parametrů. K dispozici je datový soubor, který obsahuje originální metalografické snímky (jpg), výsledky z měření tvrdosti (xlsx) a popis funkčního vzorku (pdf).

Komponenta byla vyrobena metodou aditivní přímé depozice DED-LB/W na zařízení Meltio s robotickým systémem Fanuc s polotovarem v podobě drátu. Chemické složení bylo provedeno pomocí optického emisního spektrometru Q8 Magellan. Tvrdost byla změřena laboratorním tvrdoměrem Struers Durascan 50 podle normy ČSN EN ISO 6507-1 (420374) Kovové materiály - Zkouška tvrdosti podle Vickerse při zatížení HV10. Metalografická analýza byla provedena pomocí světelného mikroskopu NIKON ECLIPSE MA200. Mikroskop je vybaven softwarem pro digitální zpracování a analýzu obrazu NIS Elements 5.2.
Data jsou v komprimované složce s názvem Data_MoNiCr obezne kolo.zip. Velikost je 27 154 kB.

metadata.title: Raw nanopore long-read sequencing data for 61 Klebsiella pneumoniae isolates – PromethION run 240216-FNB-P2S
metadata.publisher: Vysoké učení technické v Brně

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains raw nanopore long-read sequencing data generated using the Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) PromethION platform (run 240216-FNB-P2S). It includes 61 clinical isolates of Klebsiella pneumoniae obtained from the University Hospital Brno (Brno, Czechia). The data are provided in pod5 format exactly as exported from the instrument and include both pass and fail reads.

A total of 61 Klebsiella pneumoniae isolates were collected between April 2018 and November 2023 from multiple departments of the University Hospital Brno (Brno, Czechia). Genomic DNA was extracted using the DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit (Qiagen). DNA purity and concentration were assessed using a NanoDrop spectrophotometer and a Qubit 3.0 Fluorometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Sequencing libraries were prepared using the Rapid Barcoding Kit 96 V14 (Oxford Nanopore Technologies). All samples were sequenced on an Oxford Nanopore Technologies PromethION 2 Solo instrument using R10.4.1 flow cells as part of run 240216-FNB-P2S. Data were generated natively in POD5 format and exported directly from the run directory. During acquisition, the instrument performed real-time basecalling and automatically classified reads into pass (reads with a minimum quality score of 10) and fail categories. Both pass and fail POD5 files are included in this dataset.

Sequencing platform: Oxford Nanopore Technologies PromethION 2 Solo. Flow cell: R10.4.1. Acquisition software: MinKNOW v23.11.5. Data format: POD5. Dataset structure: each isolate contains 2 directories: pod5_pass (reads with a minimum quality score of 10) and pod5_fail (reads with quality scores below 10).

metadata.title: Supporting Data for "3D-printed components for electron–ion trapping: Pre-experimental tests of functionality and ultra-high vacuum compatibility"
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: This archive contains supporting data and scripts for the manuscript titled "3D-printed components for electron–ion trapping: Pre-experimental tests of functionality and ultra-high vacuum compatibility" by authors Vineet Kumar, Niklas Vilhelm Lausti, Jiří Hajnyš (Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, VŠB - Technical University of Ostrava, Ostrava, Czech Republic), Ivan Hudák, David Motyčka, Adam Jelínek and Michal Hejduk (Charles University, Faculty of Mathematics and Physics, Dept. of Surface and Plasma Science, Prague 8, Czech Republic).

The data presented in this repository were generated during the experimental work on 3D-printed components for the EiTEx electron–ion trapping apparatus described in the associated article. All measurements were performed using the equipment and infrastructure available in Optická laboratoř, Katedra fyziky povrchů a plazmatu, Skupina fyziky plazmatu a modelování, MFF UK, where the complete experimental setup is installed. Given the developing laboratory environment, several components of the apparatus—including diagnostic electronics, pumping configuration, and mechanical fixtures—were iteratively assembled, tested, and optimized throughout the measurement period, which is reflected in the range of operational regimes captured in the dataset. The accompanying processing code is provided together with this repository. Data were recorded using the laboratory acquisition hardware and subsequently processed both online and offline using custom Python scripts executed primarily via Google Colab. LabVIEW was employed for real-time monitoring and data logging, while Grafana was used for visual monitoring and InfluxDB v2 for data management and storage. All scripts are included to ensure that figures and processed data products can be fully reproduced from the raw measurement files. Ethical Considerations: No animal or human subjects were involved in this research, and no personal or private data were collected or used. All computational and experimental procedures follow standard practices in the field to ensure reliability.

The archive is organized into six main folders. Each folder contains the corresponding data files together with PDF figures illustrating plots or images of the experimental setup. The folders are named according to the figure numbering used in the associated article: Fig 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 8 – These folders contain images and schematic views of the experimental setup and its 3D-printed components, including the trap, oven assembly, vacuum chamber layout, and other parts of the apparatus. Fig 6 (UHV – Pressure vs. time) – Contains the data and accompanying plot showing the pressure vs. time progression during the pump-down sequence, illustrating the stages leading to ultra-high-vacuum (UHV) conditions suitable for electron trapping. Fig 7 (Pressure – Long-term evolution) – Includes the dataset and figure showing the long-term evolution of chamber pressure in the ion-sputter/NEG pumping regime over approximately one month. Fig 9 (Oven data – Temp. & Press.) – Contains the time-resolved oven temperature data along with corresponding chamber pressure measurements recorded during controlled heating and evaporation sequences of the calcium oven. Fig 10 (Oven vs. chamber pressure) – Provides the data and plots illustrating the temporal evolution of chamber pressure during various oven heating sequences performed under UHV conditions. Fig 11 (Resonance data) – Contains the measured microwave power spectrum of the 3D-printed coaxial resonator (red trace) recorded with the trap installed inside the vacuum chamber, as a function of frequency. Each folder includes all raw and processed data files necessary for reproducing the plots presented in the article. To run the figure generation scripts, Python v3 is required.

Data on 'SIFT-MS Analysis of Amines: Unusually efficient O2 Addition to the Radical Cation Product'
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset includes experimentally measured reaction rate coefficients and observed product ion ratios for the reactions of H3O+, NO+, and O2+ with the four amines n-propylamine, n-butylamine, dipropylamine, and dibutylamine from Selected Ion Flow-Drift Tube organized in csv tables. The dataset also contains output files from the ORCA software with optimized geometry and thermodynamic properties of the relevant ions and the neutral molecules as well as their polarizability and dipole moment.

metadata.title: Data for : Enzymatic Synthesis of Modified RNA Containing 5-Methyl- or 5-Ethylpyrimidines or  Substituted 7-Deazapurines and Influence of the Modifications on Stability, Translation and CRISPR-Cas9 Cleavage
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: A set of modified 5-methyl- and 5-ethylpyrimidine (uracil and cytosine) and 7-methyl-, 7-ethyl- and 7-unsubstituted 7-deazapurine (deazaadenine and deazaguanine) ribonucleoside triphosphates was synthesized and used for enzymatic synthesis of base-modified RNA using in vitro transcription (IVT). They all were good substrates for T7 RNA polymerase in the IVT synthesis of model 70mer RNA, mRNA encoding Renilla luciferase, as well as 99-mer single-guide RNA (sgRNA). The effect of modifications in the particular RNA on the stability and efficiency in in vitro and in cellulo translation, as well as in CRISPR-Cas9 gene cleavage was quantified. In the in vitro translation assay, we observed moderately enhance luciferase production with 5-methyluracil and -cytosine, while any 7-deazaadenines completely inhibited the translation. Surprisingly, in cellulo experiments showed a significant enhancement of translation with mRNA containing 7-deazaguanine and moderate enhancement with 5-methyl- or 5-ethylcytosine. Most of the modifications had minimal effect on the efficiency of the gene cleavage in CRISPR-Cas9 except for 7-alkyl-7-deazaadenines that completely inhibited the cleavage. The results are important for further design of potential base-modified RNA therapeutics.
In vitro transcription, in vitro translation, CRISPR-Cas9

metadata.title: Podkladová data k článku "Roadmap to planar electron-ion point Paul trap"
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_titles.title: Supporting Data for "Roadmap to planar electron-ion point Paul trap"

additional_descriptions.description: This archive contains supporting data and scripts for the manuscript titled "Roadmap to planar electron-ion point Paul trap" by authors Niklas Vilhelm Lausti, Vineet Kumar, Ivan Hudák, Michal Hejduk (Charles University, Faculty of Mathematics and Physics, Dept. of Surface and Plasma Science, Prague 8, Czech Republic) and Michal Tarana (J. Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry of the Czech Academy of Sciences, Dolejškova 2155/3, 182 23 Prague 8, Czech Republic).

The data presented in this archive were generated solely through computational methods and simulations, with no involvement of animal subjects, human participants, or personal information.

Data Generation Process

  1. Simulation Software: The data was generated using Comsol Multiphysics 6.0 for electromagnetic simulations and Gnuplot for visualizing energy levels and trapping potentials.
  2. Computational Models:
    • Electromagnetic Propagation: Simulations of electromagnetic propagation in the planar trap circuitry were performed using Comsol. The results include graphical outputs but do not contain numerical data, as these can be regenerated from the project files.
    • Quantum System Energy Levels: Gnuplot scripts were used to produce figures related to energy levels of the Ca²⁺-electron-trap quantum system. These scripts utilize data derived from numerical solutions of the Schrödinger equation for s-states in a spherically symmetric field.
    • Trapping Potentials: Additional Gnuplot scripts generate visualizations of both ideal spherically symmetric trapping potentials and those generated by the planar trap.

Ethical Considerations

  • No Animal or Human Subjects: This research did not involve any animal subjects or human participants.
  • No Personal Data: No personal or private data were used in the generation of this dataset.
  • Compliance with Standards: All computational methods adhere to standard practices in the field, ensuring reproducibility and reliability.

Data Structure

The archive is organized into three folders:

  1. Planar trap files (no solutions): Contains Comsol project files (.mph) and graphical outputs.
  2. energylevels: Includes Gnuplot scripts (.gp) and data files (.dat) for energy levels.
  3. potentialplot: Contains Gnuplot scripts (.gp) and data files (.dat) for trapping potentials.

Usage Instructions

  • Comsol Files:

    1. Open Comsol Multiphysics 6.0.
    2. Load the project file (.mph).
    3. Run the simulation to generate numerical results and graphical outputs.

  • Gnuplot Scripts:

    1. Install Gnuplot if you haven't already.
    2. Navigate to the folder containing the script files.
    3. Run the scripts using the command gnuplot script_name.gp.

metadata.title: Charles University, DataHub Community
Supporting materials for Adjective-based qualitative comparative analysis: Relating immobility situations across contexts
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset is supporting material that accompanies the paper introducing an adjective-based qualitative comparative method for analysing complex lived experiences across diverse contexts. By identifying, harmonising, and relationally comparing salient descriptive adjectives derived from qualitative data, the approach enables structured yet interpretively rich cross-case comparison. Demonstrated through a study of immobility among four non-migrating groups, the method reveals shared and context-specific patterns without reducing complexity.

The supporting material coincides with the method introduced in the paper and includes: a master list of adjectives used in the methodology; brief explanations of their meanings; notes on the relevance of each adjective to individual case studies; and illustrative questions for the individual studies.

metadata.title: Data set for Potent Competitive Inhibitors of Ecto-5´-Nucleotidase (CD73) based on 6-(Het)aryl-7-Deazapurine Ribonucleoside 5’-O-Bisphosphonates
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: CD73 generates immunosuppressive adenosine in the tumor microenvironment and is a promising target for cancer immunotherapy. We have designed and systematically studied diverse 2-substituted 7-deazapurine ribonucleoside 5′-O-bisphosphonates bearing a variety of (het)aryl groups at position 6 and discovered their highly potent and selective CD73 inhibition activity. The most active compounds (with single digit picomolar Ki) contained bicyclic (het)aryl groups at position 6 in combination with chlorine at position 2. Further optimization of pharmacokinetic properties identified inhibitors with low clearance, long half-life, high solubility, and excellent selectivity over CD39 and NTPDase3. They effectively suppressed adenosine formation in MDA-MB-231 cells, rescued CD8+ T cell activation, and were non-toxic to human fibroblasts. Overall, their profile compares favorably with AB680, a clinical CD73 inhibitor currently in phase I/II trials.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for the paper 'The minimal membrane requirements for BAX- induced pore opening upon exposure to oxidative stress'
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset presents the kinetics of calcein leakage from large unilamellar vesicles (POPC/POPE/TOCL 43/36/21) subjected to oxidative stress in the presence of the protein BAX and its activator cBid. Oxidative stress was mimicked by substituting 10 or 20 mol% of POPC with its oxidized derivatives PazePC or PoxnoPC. The BAX concentration ranged from 9 nM to 4.4 µM, while the BAX/cBid ratio was kept constant at 5:1.

The leakage experiments were performed on a 96-well plate, using a 
multi-well plate reader Tecan Pro (Tecan, Mannedorf, Switzerland).

LUVs were prepared by extrusion (see above) from dried lipid films resuspended in 500 mL buffer (20 mMHEPES, 100 mM KCl, 0.1 mM EDTA [pH
7.4], the total osmolality of 480 mOsm/kg) containing 80 mM calcein. Nonencapsulated calcein was removed by size-exclusion chromatography. The wells were prepared by twofold serial dilution of protein/s in HEPES buffer. Ten solutions with different protein concentrations were added into the plate for each lipid composition tested, including a control sample without any protein (background correction) or the sample containing Triton-X100 (assuring for full leakage). The plate was preheated to 37 °C and loaded with liposomes to reach the final lipid concentration of 100 mM. During the measurement, the sample was excited at 492 nm and the fluorescence emission was recorded at 520 nm every 5 min for a duration of 120 min. At the end of each measurement, Triton-X was added to each well (0.05% [w/v]), disrupting the vesicles and ensuring the full release of calcein. The results are expressed as a fraction of the total leakage induced by Triton X-100 after subtracting the background leakage observed in the absence of any protein.

metadata.title: Dataset for Versatile enzymatic approach for truncation, labeling, and mutation of nucleobase-modified aptamers demonstrated on indole-modified β-conglutin aptamer
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: A new enzymatic method for truncation of aptamers from 5'-end has been developed and demonstrated on a newly selected indole-modified β-conglutin aptamer. This method relies on extension of a primer containing ribonucleotides, which can be specifically hydrolyzed by RNase, thereby removing the whole or 5'-part of the primer. This approach enables flexible synthesis of modified aptamers truncated from the 5' end, 3' end, or both ends - a capability previously achievable only through chemical oligonucleotide synthesis. Furthermore, by employing doubly labeled primers, diverse labeled or modified nucleotides can be introduced to prepare 5'-labeled aptamers suitable for fluorescence- or immobilization-based assays. The different variations of this method also enable the synthesis of mutated sequences to study the effect of modifications and their positions. Overall, this enzymatic method provides a valuable alternative for the truncation or labeling of (hyper)modified and functional oligonucleotides avoiding the need for chemical synthesis.

metadata.title: Edukace  vedené zdravotními sestrami -  průřezová studie
metadata.publisher: Jihočeská univerzita v Českých Budějovicích

additional_titles.title: Nurse-Led Education in Prevention of Modifiable Risk Factors of Ischemic Heart Disease from the Point of View of General Public - a cross-sectional study
Nurse-led education - a cross-sectional study

additional_descriptions.description: Introduction:Ischemic heart disease is one of heart and vascular  diseases caused by atherosclerosis. Healthy lifestyle remains to be the main preventive intervention.It needs to be reminded that the complex process of patient education develops the patient`s knowledge, skills and attitudes.Objective:to describe the state of education in the prevention of modifiable  risk factors of cardiovascular diseases (further CVD) from the point of view of patients.Methods: A non-standardized questionnaire relating to the ischemic heart disease was used for the data collection. The sample of respondents was chosen using a quota selection corresponding with the composition of all CR citizens with in terms of regions, sex and age. The total sample consisted of 1,391 respondents. Results:The results showed a significant correlation between gender and the education on exercise (p=0.001). Furthermore, a significant correlation was identified between sex and the education on smoking (p=0.001).A significant correlation was identified between sex alcohol abusing (p=0.001).In respect of the age, nurses most participate in the education on nutrition, (p=0.001), cessation of smoking (p=0.001). Furthermore, the study proved that the motivation elements in the education on modifiable risk factors were not sufficiently used. 47.5% of respondents answered that nurses used open-ended questions in the education, 37.3% of respondents meant that nurses always and often used reflection. Conclusion:Shortcomings were identified relating to the low use of motivation elements. Therefore, it can be judged that in practice, the education has just an informative character.(en),

A descriptive and cross-sectional design was used for this study. A representative sociological survey was conducted.A combination of non-standardized questionnaires on CHD prevention was used to collect data.The final version of the questionnaire was based on a pilot study. Content and construct validity were confirmed by a preliminary study conducted on a selected sample of 186 respondents, evaluating not only the wording and comprehensibility of the questions, but also areas of health literacy and prevention of CHD risk factors and their relevance.In addition, construct validity was verified by a parallelism test of data obtained from the pilot study, which aimed to identify expected correlations between selected variables.Reliability measurements for each group of questions were performed using Cronbach's alpha test. The internal consistency values for each group of questions ranged from 0.796 to 0.950, indicating a high level of internal consistency.The questionnaire contained 80 questions and represented the result of the operationalization of the objectives and hypotheses that were established on the basis of theoretical and empirical analysis of the problem under study.The field survey itself was conducted in the Czech Republic between March 11, 2024, and March 26, 2024. The study was approved by the Ethics Committee.
We processed the data in SPSS 24.

metadata.title: Brain Drain - dataset
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Jana Evangelisty Purkyně v Ústí nad Labem

additional_titles.title: Prediction of Talent Mobility

additional_descriptions.description: The research aimed to identify the factors that influence young people's decisions about where they want to live and subsequently predict their level of motivation to move. As part of the qualitative survey (focus groups), individual items were first identified and then grouped into nine categories. The classification of items into categories was verified using Cronbach's alpha. The dataset contains data from a subsequent quantitative survey, in which these categories were quantitatively verified. The theoretical basis for the mobility forecast was Vroom's Expectancy Theory (VET). Both the shortened version (the product of expectancy and valence) and the full version (the product of expectancy, valence, and instrumentality) were used for the calculations. The respondents' self-reported level of mobility was also surveyed and subsequently evaluated. The research sample consisted of 1,017 university students under the age of 35 who are permanent residents of a structurally disadvantaged region. This region was deliberately chosen because greater mobility of young and talented people (brain drain) is expected there.

The dataset contains data from a written questionnaire (N = 1017). The content questions in the questionnaire were formulated to allow for the subsequent use of both the shortened and complete versions of Vroom's expectancy theory. The main variables were therefore:
(1) Valence. In general, valence expresses value or importance. Question wording: "How important are the following factors for your satisfaction with life?" Nine categories were then presented, which respondents rated on a Likert scale from 1 (completely unimportant factor) to 10 (essential factor).
(2) Expectations. This variable expresses the expectation that a result will be achieved. Question wording: "If you continue to live where you live TODAY, to what extent can you expect to be satisfied in the following areas?" The same nine categories were then presented, and respondents again rated them on a Likert scale from 1 (completely dissatisfied) to 10 (maximally satisfied).
Note: To make the questionnaire user-friendly for respondents, the scales were always constructed so that 1 meant a negative response (very dissatisfied, definitely unimportant) and 10 meant a positive response (very satisfied, very important). However, in the calculation, the expectation scale was mirror-reversed because dissatisfaction affects the strength of motivation. This means that the higher the valence (value) and the higher the dissatisfaction (expectation), the higher the level of motivation for mobility.
(3) Instrumentality. The third variable expresses whether an individual will receive the expected reward. The question was formulated as follows: "If you moved to another region, what do you think you would gain that would be better than now?" Subsequently, 28 items were presented. Respondents again answered on a Likert scale from 1 to 10, but 1 meant that the item in question would definitely be worse after moving (i.e., moving would not improve their situation), and 10 meant that the item in question would definitely be better after moving (i.e., moving would significantly improve their situation).
(4) The subjective degree of mobility was also assessed, with respondents answering on a Likert scale of 1 to 10 how likely they thought it was that they would move outside the region. 1 meant definitely not moving, and 10 meant definitely moving.

Dataset - column labels:
q3_1 to q3_32 - these columns label individual items - questions related to Instrumentality
Q3_1 to Q3_9 - these columns label individual categories that were created from the items
q1_new1 to q1_new9 - these columns represent questions related to valence
q2_new1 to q2_new9 - these columns represent questions related to expectancy
s5 - this column represents the subjectively declared degree of mobility

metadata.title: Red mark syndrom disease video data
metadata.publisher: Jihočeská univerzita v Českých Budějovicích

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset consists of the video records of the rainbow trout in three cultivation units infected by Red mark syndrome disease (RMS). The videos were recorded by the digital camera placed in the cultivation tank's waterproof box. Each video is 30 minutes long. The records were taken during 3 months. Two tanks, TANK_01 and TANK_03 contain infected fish. TANK_20 contains control non-infected fish. The dataset documents the progress of the RMS visible signs on the fish skin from early to late stages. The dataset was used to develop a remote early warning system for automatic disease detection in aquaculture production. It can be used for the studies in the filed of automation of aquaculture: fish individual identification, disease analysis, fish behavior analysis, or fish detection.

Experimental animals

The rainbow trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) used in the study were hatched at DTU and raised under pathogen-free conditions. At a size of around 50 g, they were individually tagged with a passive integrated transponder (PIT) tag in the dorsal musculature under anaesthesia. Before experimentation, they were moved to the infection unit at DTU. The infection was caused by cohabitation with RMS-affected seeder rainbow trout. The size of the 29 seeders used for the experiment was 203±26 g, 25±1.2 cm. A total of 180 healthy rainbow trout were used (160±38 g,  22.9±1.7 cm at the start of the experiment and 348±85 g, 29±1.8 cm at the end on day 83). One hundred twenty were infected by cohabitation, and 60 were non-infected controls. The seeders were produced 61 days before the cohabitation with the experimental fish. The cohabitation of the 29 seeders and 120 cohabitants was from 20.9.2023 to 4.10.2023 when the seeders were removed. The 120 cohabitants were subsequently divided into two experimental tanks (tank TANK_01 and TANK_03). A third similar tank was used for 60 uninfected fish as a control group (tank TANK_20). See the detailed schedule in Figure 1. [PC1] All fish were kept in flow-through 1m3 tanks supplied with a flow of fresh water at a rate of ~130 L/h. Water temperature was 12.0+/-0.5 °C, the light regime was 12:12 h light: dark, and the fish were fed commercial 3mm pellets at a rate of 1% total body weight daily using clock feeders. One fish per tank was humanely euthanised at two-week intervals, and skin lesions were sampled for histological confirmation of RMS. The experiment was terminated on 12.12.2023. The experiment was conducted under the animal experimentation license 2019-15-0201-00159-C2 issued by the Danish Animal Experiments Inspectorate. The fish was sacrificed at the end of the experiment.

 

Figure 1. Experimental schedule.

RMS symptoms

RMS is a skin disease, and the primary clinical manifestation is characteristic changes in the mucosal surface of the fish. The initial symptoms are often subtle and may appear as lighter patches of skin. These changes usually have a dorsoventrally elongated shape if found on the flanks. They gradually develop a pinkish hue and turn bright red in later stages (see Figure 2). Lesions are typically from 2 to 10 cm in diameter. Apart from the colour change (due to hyperemia/haemorrhaging), hallmark pathological signs of RMS are oedema (raised skin) and scale loss (due to resorption) at more advanced stages of lesion development. Ulcers can develop in severe cases, often as a result of secondary bacterial infections. This complicates the course of the disease and potentially results in fish mortality if therapeutic measures are not initiated in time (Galeotti et al., 2021), but it also jeopardises a correct diagnosis. However, in the semi-controlled model at DTU, ulceration is rare.

 

Figure 2. Examples of RMS visual symptoms. Left – early symptom (6 weeks after cohabitation [PC2] ), light spot on the body, Middle – the pinkish spot (8 weeks after cohabitation), Right – late symptom, dark red large lesion with missing scales (10 weeks after cohabitation).

Fish video records

The video records simulated the real fish cultivation conditions similar to those in fish production. Videos were recorded at regular intervals (every few days, 2-6 times per week) using an RGB digital camera (See3CAM_CU27) submerged in the housing in the fish tank, ensuring consistent monitoring of symptom progression throughout the experiment, see Figure 1. The HD resolution of 1280  × 720 pixels and a frame rate of 80 fps were used for video recording. The camera in housing was placed on a plastic tube holder; see Figure 3. The camera was placed in a waterproof transparent plastic box connected by an optical USB cable to the recording computer. The secure holder enables removing the camera from the tank during tank maintenance. The holder was equipped with a water-resistant LED light (20W, 5500K). Lighting was used to improve visibility in the tank, maximising overall image quality and increasing camera framerate. Both the light and the camera were remotely controlled from the computer. The light was switched on only during the recording period.

 

 

Figure 3. Left – The camera system consists of a plastic holder, an LED light, and the camera enclosed in a waterproof transparent box. Right – the top view of the tank with the fish.

All three tanks (two with infected fish and one control tank) were equipped with the camera system. The video recording started on October 9, five days after the seeders were removed. The earlier period was not recorded because no RMS symptoms were expected (based on the knowledge of RMS development). The thirty-minute video records were taken during the whole experiment for all tanks. Four video records were taken for the control tank, and forty video records were taken for two tanks with infected fish. The last video records were captured on December 10, two days before the last evaluation and experiment termination. In total, 44 video records (22 hours) of fish swimming were recorded. The fish freely moved in front of the camera. The cameras were focused at a distance greater than 15 cm to optimise the field of view and maintain focus on the majority of the fish swimming in the tank, Figure 4.

Figure 4. Examples of underwater video records from the camera system. The two images demonstrate the difference in the illumination conditions.

Data preprocessing and annotation

Video recordings were preprocessed and manually annotated to prepare data for training the CNN-based detector. Providing ground-truth data enabled reliable localisation and identification of the progress of visual signs on the fish skin at different stages. This process generated Video Image Dataset (VID).

The VID images dataset was extracted from videos to create a dataset for model training from video records. All video records were manually checked in the VirtualDub software, and the frame with visible signs on the fish was determined. When the sign was visible, the short track was used to extract every 10th frame. These frames represent the fish with visible signs at different positions, orientations, and rotations and with light reflections on the fish's body. These parameters change during the fish's swimming, and the training dataset should consist of the images under the different conditions to generalise well. The same five classes used for still image annotation were used for video image annotation. The total number of annotated images was 1991. The fish bodies were also annotated for the VID dataset to enable a more detailed analysis of the video record. The whole fish body rectangle, fish eye rectangle and dorsal fin rectangle were annotated for the fish with annotated signs of early or late RMS, see Figure 5. This triplet was annotated only if all three parts were visible. In total, 1500 fish were annotated. The number of early RMS was 1426, and the number of late RMS was 688.

Figure 5. Annotated images from short video sequence of early RMS symptoms on one fish. The early RMS (green), fish (pink), eye (red), dorsal fin (orange) and snout/lower jaw (dark green) was annotated on all images.

The dataset is stored in four ZIP files.

TANK_01.zip

TANK_03.zip

TANK_20.zip

Images from videos.zip

Images and annotations.zip

 

 

TANK_01.zip - contains 17 video (mp4 files). The name of the file represents the date and time of the recording. Each file is 30 minutes long with the resolution of 1270*720, 80fps, H264 codec. 

        - The file 20231129-190001.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 1920*1080, 60fps

        - The file 20231130-220508.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 1920*1080, 60fps

        - The file 20231205-185541.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 55 minutes long

        - The file 20231206-194522.mp4 need not to be read properly



 

TANK_03.zip - contains 23 video (mp4 files). The name of the file represents the date and time of the recording. Each file is 30 minutes long with the resolution of 1270*720, 80fps, H264 codec. 

        - The file 20231127-161610.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 1920*1080, 60fps

        - The file 20231204-192209.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 47 minutes long

        - The file 20231107-174838.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 46 minutes long

 

 

TANK_20.zip - contains 4 video (mp4 files). The name of the file represents the date and time of the recording. Each file is 30 minutes long with the resolution of 1270*720, 80fps, H264 codec. 

        - The file 20231024-171335.mp4 was recorded by different settings - 59 minutes long

 

Images from videos.zip – contains three subdirectories: TANK_01, TANK_03, andTANK_20. Each subdirectory contains subdirectories with the images selected from the particular video file of the tank. The name of subdirectory corresponds to the name of video file the images were selected from. The sub sub directory contain the images in png image format. The description of image selection is provided in Metodologie part of the dataset description.

 

Images and annotations.zip – it contains two folders:

-          RMS only

-          RMS+fish

 

-          Each folder contains images subfolder, labels subfolder and classes.txt file. These files contain the all images selected form the video files and manually annotated by 6 or 9 classes. The format of annotation is YOLO bounding boxes. The description of image annotation is provided in Metodologie part of the dataset description.

Data for Interactions at the graphene/polyaniline interface: electron donation from graphene to polyaniline and stabilization of polarons
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset supports the published article "Interactions at the graphene/polyaniline interface: electron donation from graphene to polyaniline and stabilization of polarons", DOI: 10.1007/s42114-025-01432-9

The graphene/polyaniline interface (heterostructure) is prepared by oxidative polymerization of aniline hydrochloride on the surface of CVD-graphene functionalized by sulfonic groups. 

The graphene/polyaniline interface is characterized by a range of methods: The morphology is investigated using SEM and AFM. The thickness by XPS and AFM. The chemical structure and the oxidation state and nature of charge carriers in polyaniline by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectroelectrochemistry is employed to obtain information on the reversibility of redox transitions in polyaniline and charge-carrier injection into graphene. A specially prepared field-effect transistor (FET) device is used to get information on the direction of charge transfer between graphene and polyaniline. Similar device is used under intermittent illumination of the 500-nm laser line to understand the photocurrent response and the light-induced formation and depletion of charge carriers.

The data is provided in text files and/or Excel spreadsheets. Some datasets are provided in their respective software formats, which cannot be exported to text files directly. All datasets are located in folders named after the figures (e.g., Fig1_SEM, Fig2_Raman, etc.).

metadata.title: Datová sada pro Zdravotní gramotnost české populace v oblasti prevence nádorových onemocnění
metadata.publisher: Jihočeská univerzita v Českých Budějovicích

additional_titles.title: Data set for Health literacy of the Czech population in the field of cancer prevention

additional_descriptions.description: Nádorová onemocnění představují druhou nejčastější příčinu úmrtí v České republice. Na tomto stavu se významně podílí řada rizikových faktorů a neuspokojivá míra zdravotní gramotnosti v této oblasti. Cílem příspěvku je poukázat na současný stav informovanosti a zájmu o preventivní činnosti v souvislosti s nádorovými chorobami mezi českou populací ve věku 18+ a poukázat na úroveň znalostí o prevenci nádorových onemocnění. V rámci empirického šetření bylo využito kvantitativního šetření za pomoci elektronického dotazníku vlastní konstrukce, který čítal 35 otázek. Dotazníkové šetření probíhalo od 16.1. do 14.2. 2025. Výzkumný soubor čítal 180 osob ve věku 18+. Získaná data byla zpracována v programu MS Office Excel. Výsledky poukazují na to, že znalosti české populace ve věku 18+ jsou v oblasti prevence nádorových onemocnění nevyvážené. S rostoucí úrovní dosaženého vzdělání roste i úroveň znalostí. Vyšší zájem o prevenci v této oblasti vykazují ženy. S rostoucím věkem respondentů pak klesá zájem o prevenci. Výraznější nedostatky ve znalostech se objevují v oblasti screeningových programů a ve znalostech spojených s frekvencí preventivních vyšetření. Naše zjištění poukazují na nezbytnost opakované edukace a rozvoje vzdělávacích aktivit, které pomohou zvýšit povědomí o důležitosti prevence nádorových onemocnění. Zároveň poukazují na nezbytnost efektivní a opakované edukace i ve vyšších věkových kategoriích, kde může být riziko vzniku některých nádorových onemocnění výraznější.

Cancer is the second most common cause of death in the Czech Republic. A number of risk factors and an unsatisfactory level of health literacy in this area significantly contribute to this situation. The aim of the contribution is to point out the current state of awareness and interest in preventive activities in connection with cancer among the Czech population aged 18+ and to point out the level of knowledge about cancer prevention. As part of the empirical investigation, a quantitative survey was used using an electronic questionnaire of our own design, which included 35 questions. The questionnaire survey was conducted from January 16 to February 14, 2025. The research group included 180 people aged 18+. The obtained data were processed in MS Office Excel. The results indicate that the knowledge of the Czech population aged 18+ is unbalanced in the field of cancer prevention. With an increasing level of education, the level of knowledge also increases. Women show a higher interest in prevention in this area. As the respondents age, interest in prevention decreases. More significant gaps in knowledge appear in the area of screening programs and in knowledge related to the frequency of preventive examinations. Our findings point to the necessity of repeated education and the development of educational activities that will help increase awareness of the importance of cancer prevention. At the same time, they point to the necessity of effective and repeated education also in older age categories, where the risk of developing some cancers may be more significant.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for the article: Hybridization Directionality Governs the Interaction Strength between MoS$_2$ and Metals
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This is a dataset supporting the published article on the mechanisms of 
metal-assisted exfoliation of large-area monolayer MoS2. Metal-assisted 
exfoliation has emerged as an effective approach for producing 
large-area monolayers of two-dimensional materials, with gold-assisted 
exfoliation being one of the most successful examples. While other 
metals also show promise for enabling large-area exfoliation, their 
wider application is often limited by oxidation in air. In this study, 
monolayer MoS2 heterostructures were fabricated on polycrystalline gold, 
silver, copper, palladium, cobalt, and nickel via direct mechanical 
exfoliation of bulk molybdenite under controlled atmospheric conditions. 
The dataset contains the complete set of results used in the 
publication, including density functional theory (DFT) calculations, 
Raman spectroscopy, photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS, UPS), and atomic 
force microscopy (AFM) maps. These combined experimental and 
computational results reveal that the exfoliation efficiency depends 
strongly on the choice of metal substrate. In particular, the 
hybridization directionality and the asymmetry between the bottom and 
top sulfur atoms in MoS2 are identified as key factors that weaken 
MoS2–MoS2 van der Waals interactions. This weakening at the first 
interface enables selective monolayer exfoliation, providing new 
insights into the design of substrate–material systems for scalable 
production of high-quality two-dimensional crystals.
Raman spectroscopy, optical microscopy, atomic force microscopy, density functional theory simulations, photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, XPS)
The data are provided in .png, .text, .dat, .xlsx, or .gwy files. All the datasets are located in folders named after the figures (eg, Figure 1, Figure 2, etc.).

metadata.title: Data set: Participatory Budgeting and Electoral Engagement: Unveiling the Impact on Election Turnout
metadata.publisher: Mendelova univerzita v Brně

additional_descriptions.description: This paper examines the impact of PB on voter turnout in local, regional, and parliamentary elections in the Czech Republic. Using panel data from over 6,200 municipalities between 2009-2022, we employ ordinary least squares regression with fixed effects and an array of control variables to analyze the relationship between PB initiatives and electoral participation. Our findings reveal that PB has a significant and positive effect on voter turnout in local and regional elections, but we found no effect in parliamentary elections. Specifically, PB implementation is associated with a 2.8-3.9 percentage point increase in local election turnout and a 1.5-3.9 percentage point increase in regional election turnout when conducted within two years prior to elections. These effects are most pronounced when PB occurs one year before elections, the effect size and significance decrease with a longer time interval from the election. Our robustness check based on machine learning methods confirms these effects with generally similar magnitudes and significance. The lack of impact on parliamentary elections suggests that PB's influence is primarily localized, while local elections turnout may be fostered by the implementation of PB initiatives to enhance voter participation in local and regional democratic processes.

The study uses panel data from 6,244 Czech municipalities (2009–2022) to estimate the effect of participatory budgeting (PB) on voter turnout in local, regional, and parliamentary elections. The main analysis applies ordinary least squares (OLS) panel regressions with time and spatial fixed effects, controlling for demographic, economic, and urban factors drawn from census and regional statistics. PB implementation is measured for up to three years before each election type, with turnout defined as the share of registered voters casting ballots. PB data were compiled from municipal sources, NGO databases, media, and direct inquiries, then matched with official electoral data. To address unobserved heterogeneity and validate findings, a robustness check employed a machine-learning counterfactual approach, comparing predicted and actual turnout in PB municipalities. This dual strategy isolates the short-term electoral effects of PB while accounting for local and temporal specificities.

The compressed file contains the full data set for the paper and the dofile for Stata software to run the analyses. At least Stata 16 is required to run all the analyses (Stata/Python integration is required to run one of the modules).

metadata.title: Výkon trestu očima odsouzených: anonymizované přepisy 15 rozhovorů (2018–2019), verze v3
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: Datový balíček obsahuje anonymizované přepisy 15 polo-strukturovaných rozhovorů s odsouzenými muži ve věznici s ostrahou v ČR, nasbírané v letech 2018–2019. Transkripty vznikly z audiozáznamů, prošly jazykovou normalizací, pseudonymizací (v1) a úplnou anonymizací (v3; k-anonymita ≥ 5). Součástí jsou metadata (codebook, DMP, etický protokol) a popis anonymizačního schématu (přezdívky, zobecnění míst a institucí). Formáty: .rtf (transkripty), .csv/.pdf (metadata). Témata rozhovorů pokrývají každodenní režim, práci a samosprávu na oddílech, vztahy mezi vězni a zaměstnanci, drogovou scénu, kázeňská opatření a prožívání trestu. Z důvodu plánu doktorského studia, které počítá s dokončením disertační práce a dvěma souběžnými články v roce 2027 si vyhrazuji právo data prozatím nezveřejňovat. Po skončení embarga budou data dostupná pro sekundární kvalitativní analýzy; veřejná verze neobsahuje osobní údaje.

Terénní výzkum v jedné věznici s ostrahou uskutečněný z pozice semi-insidera v letech 2018–2019. Bylo provedeno 15 polo-strukturovaných interview s odsouzenými muži (purposive sampling na základě vzájemné důvěry s přihlédnutím k variabilitě souboru) o délce 45–90 min (M = 70 min). Rozhovory byly s informovaným souhlasem nahrány, přepsány a dále zpracovány pro účely analýzy. Data byla primárně analyzována metodou reflexivní tematické analýzy (Braun & Clarke, 2022), počítá se s jejich sekundární analýzou.

Data tvoří přepisy ve formátu .rtf. Součástí jsou i metadata (code-book, code-map) ve formátech .csv, .pdf a .png. Dále jsou v balíčku doprovodné informace (data-managemt plan a etický protokol ve formátu .pdf).
