metadata.title: Data set: Assessing Participatory Budgeting´s Impact on Voter Turnout: Evidence from the Capital City of Prague.
metadata.publisher: Mendelova univerzita v Brně

additional_descriptions.description: This study investigates the impact of participatory budgeting (PB) on voter turnout in Prague districts across different types of elections. Employing a methodological framework combining synthetic control and regression approaches, we analyse data from local, parliamentary, presidential, and European Parliament elections over a period from 2009 up to 2024. Our findings reveal that PB implementation has a statistically significant positive effect on voter turnout, particularly in local elections where it increased participation by 1.44 to 1.49 percentage points. A smaller but still significant effect was observed in parliamentary elections, with an increase of 0.83 to 0.91 percentage points. However, no significant impact was found for presidential or European Parliament elections. These results suggest that PB's influence on civic engagement is most pronounced at the local level, aligning with the hypothesis that it encourages greater interest in local issues and interaction with local politicians. The study contributes to the broader discourse on participatory democracy, indicating that while PB can be an effective tool for increasing citizen engagement, its impact varies across different levels of governance. These findings have implications for policymakers considering PB as a means to enhance democratic participation.

This study analyzes the impact of participatory budgeting (PB) implementation on voter turnout across Prague's 57 districts using a difference-in-differences approach based on the synthetic control method. The synthetic control method creates a counterfactual control group from untreated units to mimic the characteristics of treated districts, addressing the endogeneity problem where councils may implement PB with specific aims like enhancing participation or mobilizing voters. Control variables include demographic and socioeconomic factors such as population size, unemployment rate, education levels, age distribution, population stability indicators, and housing characteristics, sourced from the Czech Statistical Office and Census data.

The analysis examines four types of elections: local, parliamentary, presidential, and European Parliament elections, spanning from 2009 to 2024. A dummy variable indicates whether a PB round occurred between consecutive elections, with the main equation estimating voter turnout as a function of synthetic control values and the PB dummy variable. Data on PB initiatives were collected from multiple sources including NGO databases, media archives, and direct contact with municipal authorities due to the absence of a central PB database in the Czech Republic. Senate and regional elections were excluded due to geographical misalignment with Prague districts and administrative constraints respectively.

metadata.title: WRF surface data from central-European simulations of period 2015-2019
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: The stored data include selected surface meteorological arrays obtained from simulations performed with WRF model on domain covering central Europe. The simulation time-span is 2015-2019. Different types of urban models and urban canopy parameters were used to reveal a model sensitivity.
The stored data were created by selecting meteorological variables in surface layer from raw model outputs. The file describing data structure is included.
Data in the netCDF format, compressed by tar archiving utility.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Voltammetric determination of lercanidipine-hollow fiber-based liquid phase microextraction
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The stored dataset characterizes the application of hollow fiber-based liquid phase microextraction (HF-LPME) for the voltammetric determination of the antihypertensive drug lercanidipine (LCN) in distilled water, urine, blood plasma, and blood serum using screen-printed boron-doped diamond electrodes (SP-BDDE). 
The dataset contains the results of the investigation of the optimum conditions for the voltammetric determination of LCN using SP-BDDE in distilled water. It also includes the investigation of the optimal conditions for LCN HF-LPME (donor phase, acceptor phase, pH, ionic strength, solvents, stirring, extraction time, etc.). It also contains the results (calibration curves) of the LCN determination using HF-LPME and SP-BDDE in distilled water, urine, blood plasma, and serum.

1) Optimum conditions for voltammetric determination of lercanidipine (LCN) using screen-printed boron-doped diamond electrodes (SP-BDDE) were investigated.
2) Optimum conditions for LCN HF-LPME were investigated (donor phase, acceptor phase, pH, ionic strength, solvents, stirring, extraction time). 
3) LCN determination using HF-LPME and SP-BDDE in distilled water, urine, blood plasma, and serum was investigated.

PalmSens4 (PalmSens BV, Netherlands), controlled by a desktop computer equipped with Microsoft Windows 10 (64 bits) (Microsoft,
USA) operating system and PSTrace version 5.6 (PalmSens BV, Netherlands) electrochemical software was used for voltammetric
measurements.
The SP-BDDE (manufactured by Slovak Diamond group, obtained via Metrohm, Czech Republic) consisted of BDD as the working electrode (surface area 7.06 mm2, inner diameter of 3 mm), BDD counter electrode, and Ag|AgCl reference electrode, to which all potentials are referred.
Square wave voltammetry (SWV) was used for LCN determination.
Urine, plasma, and serum samples were prepared from a healthy adult volunteer.

metadata.title: WRF simulations on impact of urban areas on cloud cover and precipitation over cities in Central Europe
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: It is well known that urban areas have an effect on relevant meteorological variables such as temperature and wind speed, but the effect on cloud cover and precipitation is less study. To assess this impact, we use the WRF model over a domain covering Central Europe. Here we present the results obtained from a 15-members ensemble to quantify the impact of urban areas on temperature at 2 m (T2), specific humidity (Q2), cloud cover (CLDFRA_comp) and hourly precipitation (hourly_prec) over Central Europe from 2008 to 2017. These data are the basis for the analysis presented in the paper "Long-term impact of urban areas on meteorological conditions over Central Europe" by Villalba-Pradas, A., Karlický, J., Huszár, P., Žak, M., and Halenka, T.,  Annals of the New York Academy of Science, 2025  (check for final published version).

Data are based WRF simulations over a a 9 km x 9 km horizontal resolution domain (190 x 166 gridpoints) centered in Prague and covering Central Europe. The simulated period covers 10 years, from 2008 to 2017. These simulations were driven by ERA5 using CORINE Land cover data (CLC 2012 version).

A total of 15 simulations were conducted with different combinations of the urban, convection and microphysics parameterizations: a) NU_G3D – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the G3D convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. b) NU_GF – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the GF convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. c) NU_NT – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the new Tiedtke (NT) convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. d) NU_P3 – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the G3D convection scheme and the P3 microphysics scheme. e) NU_WDM6 – simulation with no urban scheme (all urban surfaces are replaces by crops) using the GF convection scheme and the WDM6 microphysics scheme. f) BULK_G3D – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the G3D convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. g) BULK_GF – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the GF convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. h) BULK_NT – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the new Tiedtke (NT) convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. i) BULK_P3 – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the G3D convection scheme and the P3 microphysics scheme. j) BULK_WDM6 – simulation with the bulk parameterization using the GF convection scheme and the WDM6 microphysics scheme. k) SLUCM_G3D – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the G3D convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. l) SLUCM_GF – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the GF convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. m) SLUCM_NT – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the new Tiedtke (NT) convection scheme and the Purdue and Lin microphysics scheme. n) SLUCM_P3 – simulation with the SLUCM urban scheme, the G3D convection scheme and the P3 microphysics scheme. o) SLUCM_WDM6 – simulation with nthe SLUCM urban scheme, the GF convection scheme and the WDM6 microphysics scheme. Data are provided for the following variables: T2, Q2, cloud fraction and hourly precipitation. Each file contains one ensemble member and one variable at hourly resolution. Format of data: netCDF.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Elimination Voltammetry with Linear Scan
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The coefficients of the elimination equations were calculated using Visual Basic macros for rate ratios of 0.25:0.5:1:2 for 2-6 individual currents (capacitive, diffusion, kinetically irreversible, etc.). In the range from 0 V to +7 V, voltammograms and elimination voltammograms were simulated for the substance undergoing reduction/oxidation in the adsorbed state under various parameters (scan rate, charge transfer coefficient, surface coverage, number of electrons transferred in the rate-determining step (rds), initial heterogeneous rate constant of the investigated irreversible process, initial surface excess of the electroactive substance (before the onset of electrode polarization) and electrode potential).

Application of elimination voltammetry with linear scan (EVLS) for studying systems recorded using linear voltammetry
Based on the different dependences of the individual currents that make up the total voltammetric current (diffusion, charging, kinetics, and various irreversible components) on the scan rate, EVLS can eliminate or preserve certain current components. Increased sensitivity and selectivity can be expected, especially in the case of a fully adsorbed electroactive particle subjected to an irreversible electrode process.

Software procedures and functions for the simulation of voltammetric records and calculations of their linear voltammograms were developed in Visual Basic for Applications in Excel 2019. The studied parameters were dependences on scan rate, charge transfer coefficient, surface coverage, number of electrons transferred in the rate-determining step (rds), initial heterogeneous rate constant of the investigated irreversible process, initial surface excess of the electroactive substance (before the start of electrode polarization), and electrode potential.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Experimental Data for the Dissociative Electron Attachment Study of Methyl Iodide
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains experimental data from a dissociative electron attachment (DEA) study of the CH3I molecule. The experiment was performed primarily using the dissociative electron attachment - velocity map imaging (DEA-VMI) experimental setup. In addition, the Cluster Beam (CLUB) apparatus was also employed to obtain high resolution mass spectra. Using the DEA-VMI setup, we measured the anionic mass spectra of CH3I produced due to DEA. Then we measured the ion-yield curves of I- and CH2- fragments. We also measured the high-resolution mass spectra at 8.0 eV incident electron beam energy using the CLUB setup. Velocity Map Images (VMI) of I- fragments were measured using the DEA-VMI setup at 0.01, 0.06, 0.1, and 8.0 eV incident electron energies. The angular distribution and kinetic energy distribution of the I- fragments were obtained from the VMI data. The data set also contained the simulated velocity map image of I- fragments based on measured kinetic energy distribution.

An effusive beam of CH3I formed in the DEA-VMI setup and was made to interact with energy controlled semi-monochromatised electron beam produced using a trochoidal electron monochromator. The time and arrival position of the produced anions were recorded using a microchannel plate based hexanode delay line detector. Two sets of data acquisition system were used to record a data, the CoboldPC software for the VMI and our own custom designed ELS software for the ion-yield curves. The angular and kinetic energy distributions were obtained from the VMI images and the ion-yield curves were obtained from the ELS software. For the CLUB setup the sample was introduced as a background gas in the vaccum chamber and an electron gun was used to produced the electron beam. A reflectron time of flight mass spectometer was used to analysed and record the data.

The dataset contains four folders, each with a different type of data. The "VMI_central_slices" folder contains ASCII files of the central sliced velocity map images (VMI) for four different incident electron energies. It also includes the radius distribution of I⁻ ions produced at 0.01 eV, used for creating the simulated VMI image. The "Mass_spectra_and_Ion_yield" folder contains the mass spectra obtained from both experimental setups and the ion yield curves recorded using the DEA-VMI setup. The "Angular_distribution" folder contains data on the angular distribution of the I⁻ fragments, and the "Kinetic_energy_distribution" folder contains data on the kinetic energy distribution of the I⁻ fragments.

metadata.title: VegVault: an interdisciplinary dataset linking paleo-, and neo-vegetation data with functional traits and abiotic drivers (v1.0.0 - flat files)
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description:
Here, we present VegVault (v1.0.0), an interdisciplinary dataset that uniquely integrates paleo- and neo-ecological plot-based vegetation data on a global and millennial scale, directly linking them with functional traits, soil, and climate information. VegVault (v1.0.0) currently comprises data from BIEN, sPlotOpen, TRY, Neotoma, CHELSA, and WoSIS, providing a comprehensive and ready-to-use resource for researchers across various fields to address questions about past and contemporary biodiversity patterns and their abiotic drivers. This upload is the flat-file version of an SQLite database. More details on the project website (https://bit.ly/VegVault).
See the SQL version of the VegVault (v1.0.0) under this DOI: https://doi.org/10.48700/datst.t21cz-2nq07

This is a flat-file export of a SQLite database. Therefore, it consists of several CSV files. See more information about the dataset compilation on the project website (https://bit.ly/VegVault). See the SQL version of the VegVault (v1.0.0) under this DOI: https://doi.org/10.48700/datst.t21cz-2nq07

The VegVault v1.0.0 is an databaset of consists of 32 interconnected tables with 87 fields (variables), which contains more than 480,000 Datasets, 13,000,000 Samples, 100,000 Taxa, 6 vegetation Traits,  11,000,000 Trait values, and 8 abiotic variables. See more information about the dataset compilation on the project website (https://bit.ly/VegVault).
This is the flat-file version of the SQLite database. See the SQL version of the VegVault (v1.0.0) under this DOI: https://doi.org/10.48700/datst.t21cz-2nq07

metadata.title: Kompletní sada dat pro detailní 3D rekonstrukci lesních ploch a simulaci leteckého laserového skenování
metadata.publisher: Ústav výzkumu globální změny AV ČR

additional_titles.title: Complete Dataset for Detailed 3D Forest Reconstruction and Airborne Laser Scanning Simulation

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset supports the publication "Complete Workflow for Detailed 3D Forest Reconstruction: From Terrestrial Laser Scanning to Complex 3D Radiative Transfer Modelling". It contains terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) point clouds, real airborne laser scanning (ALS) data, and simulated ALS datasets generated using the Helios++ ray tracing engine. The dataset covers four forest plots located in the Silesian Beskids, Czech Republic, representing mixed broadleaf and coniferous stands.

The TLS data were used to reconstruct biologically realistic 3D tree models, including wood and foliage, using a custom-trained PointNet++ classifier and quantitative structure models (QSMs). The Simulated ALS point clouds were produced by scanning these reconstructed plots virtually, under conditions matching real flight parameters. The Real ALS data provide reference observations acquired under leaf-on canopy conditions in July 2019 using a RIEGL LMS-Q780 sensor.

All data are spatially referenced, pre-processed (e.g., height normalisation, classification), and formatted as .laz files. This dataset enables the validation of virtual laser scanning outputs against real-world observations and supports further research in forest structure analysis, radiative transfer modelling, and synthetic remote sensing dataset generation.

This dataset accompanies the manuscript "Complete Workflow for Detailed 3D Forest Reconstruction: From Terrestrial Laser Scanning to Complex 3D Radiative Transfer Modelling". It contains terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) point clouds, real airborne laser scanning (ALS) data, and simulated ALS data used for validating 3D forest reconstruction and radiative transfer modelling. The data were collected from four circular forest plots (radius = 15 m) located in the Silesian Beskids, Czech Republic. The plots represent typical Central European mixed-species forests, dominated by European beech and Norway spruce. TLS data were used to reconstruct biologically realistic 3D models of individual trees, which were then used to simulate ALS data using the Helios++ ray-tracing engine. The resulting simulated point clouds were validated against real ALS data acquired in July 2019.

# Dataset: Complete Dataset for Detailed 3D Forest Reconstruction and Airborne Laser Scanning Simulation This dataset accompanies the manuscript "Complete Workflow for Detailed 3D Forest Reconstruction: From Terrestrial Laser Scanning to Complex 3D Radiative Transfer Modelling". It contains terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) point clouds, real airborne laser scanning (ALS) data, and simulated ALS data used for validating 3D forest reconstruction and radiative transfer modelling. The data were collected from four circular forest plots (radius = 15 m) located in the Silesian Beskids, Czech Republic. The plots represent typical Central European mixed-species forests, dominated by European beech and Norway spruce. TLS data were used to reconstruct biologically realistic 3D models of individual trees, which were then used to simulate ALS data using the Helios++ ray-tracing engine. The resulting simulated point clouds were validated against real ALS data acquired in July 2019. --- ## TLS/ This folder contains terrestrial laser scanning (TLS) point clouds acquired in August (Plot 5) and September (Plots 8, 9, 20) 2019 using a RIEGL VZ-400 scanner. Each plot was scanned from five positions to ensure full coverage. Scans were aligned using reference spheres and voxelised to a resolution of 0.05 m. The point clouds were height-normalised (relative to the ground). Filename structure: `TB_TLS_2019_MM_plot_ID_[X]_grhrz.laz` - MM: Month of acquisition (08 = August, 09 = September) - plot_ID_[X]: Plot identifier (5, 8, 9, 20) - grhrz: Ground-referenced height-normalised data - .laz: Compressed point cloud format These files served as the basis for 3D reconstruction (tree segmentation, classification, and foliage placement). --- ## Real_ALS/ This folder contains airborne laser scanning (ALS) point clouds acquired on 26 July 2019 under leaf-on conditions using a RIEGL LMS-Q780 scanner. The flight was conducted by the Flying Laboratory of Imaging Systems. Data were collected at an altitude of 825 m with a pulse repetition frequency of 400 kHz, achieving a point density of up to 10 points/m². The ALS data were processed using LAStools and include classified and height-normalised point clouds (ground vs. vegetation). These serve as reference data for validating the simulated ALS point clouds. Filename structure: TB_ALS_2019_07_plot_ID_[X]_grhrz.laz - plot_ID_[X]: Plot identifier (5, 8, 9, 20) - grhrz: Height-normalised data - .laz: Compressed format --- ## Simulated_ALS/ This folder contains synthetic ALS point clouds generated using the Helios++ simulation engine. The simulations replicate the real ALS acquisition conditions (flight altitude, sensor type, pulse density, scan angle) and were performed on fully reconstructed 3D forest scenes derived from TLS data. The simulated point clouds were post-processed to classify ground and vegetation returns and normalised by height above ground, mirroring the processing of real ALS data. Filename structure: TB_simALS_2019_07_plot_ID_[X]_grhrz.laz - simALS: Simulated ALS - plot_ID_[X]: Plot identifier (5, 8, 9, 20) - grhrz: Ground-referenced height-normalised data - .laz: Compressed format --- ## File Format All point clouds are provided in compressed **LAZ format** (compatible with LAS 1.2), using ETRS-89 / UTM Zone 33N coordinate reference system. The point clouds are classified (when applicable) and height-normalised with respect to the digital terrain model (DTM). --- For questions or further information, please contact: Tomáš Hanousek – [hanousek.t@czechglobe.cz]

metadata.title: Dataset for Adsorptive Removal of Pimavanserin from Water Using β-Cyclodextrin Polymer Coated PVDF Nanofibers
metadata.publisher: Technická univerzita v Liberci

additional_descriptions.description: This data supports the article: Adsorptive Removal of Pimavanserin from Water Using β-Cyclodextrin Polymer Coated PVDF Nanofibers

Nuclear Magnetic Resonance: Liquid and Solid-state (SS) NMR spectra were recorded using a 400 MHz JNM-ECZ400R/M1 NMR spectrometer (JEOL) at ambient temperature (295 K). 1H and ROESY measurements were recorded in D2O, with the residual solvent peak used for referencing chemical shifts. For SS-NMR analysis, samples were packed into a 3.2 mm ZrO2 rotor, with 13C spectra recorded using CP/MAS or spin-echo (HahnEcho/MAS) at a MAS of 20 kHz with high-powered 1H decoupling (TTPM). Recorded sample spectra were externally referenced to γ-glycine (174.1 ppm carbonyl signal) with relaxation delays and number of scans optimized based on samples measured. For CP/MAS, optimized parameters used included 2,048 scans, 5 s relaxation delay, a π/2 pulse of 2.35 µs at 8.7 dB, and a contact time of 2 ms. HahnEcho/MAS using 2,800 scans, 25 s relaxation delay, a π/2 pulse of 2.13 µs at 8.7 dB, and with 50 µs pre- and post-echo's were used to acquire spectra. Spectra were subsequently analyzed using MestReNova software.

Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC): Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) was undertaken using a Nano ITC low volume instrument (TA Instruments), with measurements taken at 25 °C in distilled water. Samples were sonicated for 30 min to reduce amounts of dissolved gases before measurements, with analysis of obtained data using NanoAnalyze.

Surface Area (BET): Prior to analysis, the samples were degassed under vacuum. The raw data was obtained using adsorption of krypton at liquid nitrogen temperature (Autosorb 6100, Anton Paar), with the BET surface area calculated using software Anton Paar Kaomi for Autosorb.

Ultraviolet-visible Spectroscopy 

Ultraviolet-visible (UV-VIS) spectra were recorded over a 190-400 nm wavelength range with HACH LANGE UV-VIS DR 6000 spectrophotometer. Background correction was applied to ensure accurate spectral readings.

Data structure: 
- NMR.zip, ITC.zip, BET.zip, Adsorption UV-Vis.zip

metadata.title: Dataset for average bipolar solar active region
metadata.publisher: Astronomický ústav AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Source data files for the study published in a paper Švanda et al., Average solar active region: I. Intensities, velocities, and the photospheric magnetic field, Astronomy and Astrophysics, preprint arXiv:2506.11713. The dataset contains the final ensemble-averaged FITS files of the datacubes capturing the evolution of the average normalised bipolar active region on the Sun in various physical quantities. The datacubes record the emergence and growth of this ensemble average obtained from averaging over 36 individual bipolar active regions in the following physical quantities: the continuum intensity, line-of-sight magnetogram, vector magnetogram (Bvec), inclination of the magnetic field (Bvec_inc) and the horizontal components (Bvec_X, Bvec_Y), line-of-sight Dopplergram, horizontal velocity calculated by LCT method, and intensities in three SDO/AIA channels: 1600, 304, and 171 Angstrom.

A set of 36 carefully selected bipolar solar active regions was identified in the SDO/HMI archive. These active regions were normalised both in space in time. For the normalisation, key points were identified. In the time domain, the key points consisted of the time when a rapid emergence began, the second key time was the time when the maximum magnetic flux was registered. This allowed a linear normalisation of the time axis between 0 (emergence) and 1 (flux maximum). In the spatial domain, the key points consisted of (frame-by-frame detected) barycentres of both (leading and trailing) polarities of the active region. A final product of the average active region was obtained as an ensemble average of the normalised datacubes of the individual active regions, aligning on indicated key points both in time and space. Several observables (continuum intensity, line-of-sight magnetogram, vector magnetogram, line-of-sight Dopplergram, LCT velocity vector, SDO/AIA low-temperature filtergrams) were computed. 
For details of the data processing read associated paper.

Multidimensional datacubes stored in the FITS (https://fits.gsfc.nasa.gov/fits_home.html) format. All necessary metadata are stored properly within the FITS header. In all cases, the first dimension indicates the time (in normalised units), the second and the third the spatial dimensions (in normalised units following the "image-ordering" convention) and possibly a fourth axis in the case of multicomponent observable. For proper axes scaling follow the corresponding CRPIXn and CDELTn keywords in the headers. The data are stored in physical units indicated by BUNIT keyword.

additional_descriptions.description: Here, we present VegVault (v1.0.0), an interdisciplinary SQLite database that uniquely integrates paleo- and neo-ecological plot-based vegetation data on a global and millennial scale, directly linking them with functional traits, soil, and climate information. VegVault (v1.0.0) currently comprises data from BIEN, sPlotOpen, TRY, Neotoma, CHELSA, and WoSIS, providing a comprehensive and ready-to-use resource for researchers across various fields to address questions about past and contemporary biodiversity patterns and their abiotic drivers. More details on the project website (https://bit.ly/VegVault).
See more information about the dataset compilation on the project website (https://bit.ly/VegVault).

The VegVault v1.0.0 is an SQLite database of a size of ~ 110 GB, which contains more than 480,000 Datasets, 13,000,000 Samples, 100,000 Taxa, 6 vegetation Traits,  11,000,000 Trait values, and 8 abiotic variables.
It consists of 31 interconnected tables with 87 fields (variables).
See more information about the dataset compilation on the project website (https://bit.ly/VegVault).

metadata.title: Dataset for: Optimization of MoNiCr Alloy Production Through Additive Manufacturing
metadata.publisher: COMTES FHT

additional_descriptions.description: The paper connected to this dataset briefly describes the optimization of MoNiCr alloy production for Generation IV reactor applications, focusing on additive manufacturing using the DED-LB process. Emphasis was placed on adjusting the chemical composition and input powder quality, which significantly influenced the final mechanical properties and minimised defects. A dataset (200 MB) is provided alongside the paper, including original metallographic images, tensile test results (Excel), flowability and PSD graphs (pdf), and an EDAX analysis (Word).

The MoNiCr nickel alloy was prepared in a vacuum induction furnace capable of melting under vacuum as well as in a protective argon atmosphere. The chemical composition was monitored using a Q8 Magellan optical emission spectrometer. Ingots weighing 50 kg were subsequently hot forged and rolled, followed by cold rapid forging to produce a suitable preform for ultrasonic atomization. Particle size distribution (PSD) was measured using a HORIBA LA-960 V2 analyzer in accordance with ISO 13320. The PSD measurement data are provided in PDF format, including raw data and graphs. Flowability was assessed according to ASTM B213, and the results are available in Excel format. The powders were used for additive manufacturing of samples by the DED-LB method using the INSSTEK MX-600 system, followed by heat treatment in a CLASIC vacuum furnace. The tensile test results are provided in Excel format, including raw data. Metallographic data are stored in JPG and TIFF formats.
The data is stored in the compressed data set named Open_data. The overall size of the dataset is aproxximately 200 MB.

metadata.title: Data k rukopisu - Vliv podmínek depozice na rychlost tisku, množství in-situ vzniklého austenitu a mechanické vlastnosti oceli SAF2507 připravené metodou přímé depozice
metadata.publisher: COMTES FHT

additional_titles.title: Data to manuscript Influence of Deposition Parameters on the Building rate, In‑Situ Formation of Austenite and Tensile Properties of SAF 2507 SDSS Fabricated by Directed Energy Deposition

additional_descriptions.description: Industry applications of duplex stainless steels require a two-phase microstructure with a nearly balanced austenite-ferrite content to achieve good mechanical properties and good corrosion resistance. The most widespread additive manufacturing method such as laser powder bed fusion provides nearly fully ferritic microstructures due to the very high cooling rate, and the post-processing heat treatment must be performed. In contrast, other additive manufacturing methods, such as directed deposition methods, reach lower cooling rates and enable to obtain an equal ferrite–austenite ratio directly in the microstructure of the as-built state. In this work, the super duplex stainless steel SAF 2507 was deposited by directed energy deposition. The process parameters, namely, the laser power, laser beam spot, and powder feed rate, were varied to investigate the impact of the energy area density on the microstructure and tensile properties. A decrease in the energy area density caused an increase in the austenite content of 48 vol% and a numerical simulation of the sample temperature distribution inside the block during deposition, explaining the relationship between the amount of in situ formed austenite and the deposition parameters, was provided. Simultaneously, the deposition rate increased by more than five times. In contrast, the samples deposited at a higher building rate are characterized by greater porosity and lower elongation values in the vertical direction. The effect of the deposition parameters on the yield strength is low.

The data set is related to the investigation of the influence of deposition parameters on the microstructure, austenite formation, and tensile properties of SAF 2507 super duplex stainless steel fabricated using directed energy deposition (DED). The experiments were carried out at COMTES FHT (Czech Republic) using an INSSTEK MX-600 system equipped with a 2 kW Yttrium fiber laser. SAF 2507 powder (particle size 45–150 μm) was deposited under three different parameter sets, varying laser power, beam diameter, layer thickness, hatch distance, and powder feed rate, while maintaining constant scan speed. A zig-zag deposition strategy was applied under a protective argon atmosphere (5.0, 99.999%). For each parameter set, 20×20×20 mm cubes were built and used for analysis. 
Microstructure and phase composition were characterized using optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, EBSD analysis, X-ray diffraction. Tensile properties were evaluated using miniature tensile tests. Thermal simulations of the deposition process were carried out using Simufact Welding 2024.1 with the Advanced Thermal Cycle (ATC) method.
The sets of experimental data were recorded on several instruments within COMTES FHT. In cases where possible (XRD, MTT, EBSD), the data are in xlsx format enabling any replotting.

metadata.title: Dataset: Characterization of nanosponges with FTIR and NMR methods
metadata.publisher: Technická univerzita v Liberci

additional_descriptions.description: This data support article: Fluroine-free cyclodextrin nanosponges designed for high-affinity and broad spectrum PFAS removal

FTIR spectra were recorded in the 4000–400 cm⁻¹ range using ATR mode with a diamond crystal on Nicolet iZ10 and iN10 MX instruments (Thermo Scientific).

Solid-state ¹³C NMR spectra were acquired on a JEOL JNM-ECZ400R/M1 (400 MHz) spectrometer at 295 K using 3.2 mm ZrO₂ rotors and a MAS rate of 20 kHz. Spectra were externally referenced to the carbonyl signal of γ-glycine (174.1 ppm) and processed with MestReNova software.

For structural confirmation, ¹³C CP/MAS NMR spectra were measured (1800 scans, 4–5 s relaxation delay, 2 ms contact time, 2.35 µs π/2 pulse, and high-power ¹H decoupling).
Quantitative ¹³C MAS NMR was used to determine the cyclodextrin-to-crosslinker molar ratio (2000–4000 scans, 22–40 s relaxation delay, 2.13 µs π/2 pulse, with TTPM decoupling).

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Structural and Chemical Changes in Si Nanoparticle-Based Anodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries during the (De)lithiation Processes Studied by In Situ Raman Spectroelectrochemistry
metadata.publisher: Fyzikální ústav AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset supports the published article titled "Structural and Chemical Changes in Si Nanoparticle-Based Anodes in Lithium-Ion Batteries during the (De)lithiation Processes Studied by In Situ Raman Spectroelectrochemistry." It contains in situ Raman spectroelectrochemical data, as well as data from cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements of silicon anodes prepared from 6 nm Si nanoparticles. The in situ Raman SEC provides unique insight into the crystal lattice changes and degradation/amorphization pathways of the Si nanocrystals and the electrolyte (LiPF6 in EC/DMC) decomposition during the electrochemical lithiation and delithiation processes. The evolution of the spectral parameters (shift, line width, intensity) of the first-order Raman peak of crystalline Si at 520 cm–1 is found to be related to the stress buildup in the nanoparticles. This stress originates from the (i) SEI layer formation on the electrode surface within the initial charge/discharge cycle, (ii) the lithiation-induced stress in Si nanoparticles and the native oxide on their surface, and also (iii) the progressive crystalline-to-amorphous Si phase transition. The structural changes in the anodes determined using in situ Raman SEC show good agreement with the results obtained from cyclic voltammetry measurements, revealing a progressive crystalline-to-amorphous Si phase transition and a complex energy storage mechanism in nanostructured silicon anodes in LIBs.
TEM, Raman spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge, in situ Raman spectroelectrochemistry
See the experimental part in https://pubs.acs.org/doi/full/10.1021/acsaem.5c00066.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset of calcium influx of Jurkat cells on a functionalized coverslip
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset shows the calcium influx of GCaMPfu transfected Jurkat cells interacting with a surface that mimics microvilli and is functionalized with biotinylated anti-human CD3ε (MEM-57). The cells were imaged using TIRF microscopy and saved in .tif files. The data shows the first contact of the cell to the surface and the calcium dynamics in single cells. The raw data can be analysed quantitatively by examining the fluorescence produced by each cell.

Cell culture and transfection. Jurkat cells (clone E6-1; ATCC) were grown in RPMI-1640 medium (Fischer Scientific) supplemented with 10% of fetal bovine serum (Fischer Scientific), non-essential amino acids (Fischer Scientific) and 10 mM HEPES under controlled humidity in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO 2 . The cell line was regularly tested for morphology and mycoplasma infection. For the expression of GCaMP6fu and GCaMP6fast, the Jurkat cells were transiently transfected with DNA plasmids using the Neon® transfection system (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The transfection was performed using 1 µg of the DNA vector and 300,000 cells in 0.5 ml of prewarmed supplemented RPMI culture medium. The instrument settings were 3 pulses, each 1500 V for 10 ms. The cells were incubated for 16-24h before their use. Single-cell calcium measurement. The coverslips were mounted, and the cells were prepared and transferred to the coverslip for the experiment as previously described for the large-scale calcium measurement. The cells were imaged in a home-built super-resolution wide-field TIRF microscope in thecnical information

The imaging was performed with TIRFM using the 488nm laser with a 5% power intensity; the emission was filtered using 525/50 filter (Semrock) in the EMCCD camera (iXon DU-897U, Andor). EM gain was set to 100 with an exposure time of 100 ms; single cells were imaged for at least 15 min. The calcium influx was analyzed by the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) changes of the cells landing on the coverslips as previously described (Franke et al., 2022).

metadata.title: A method for determination of transport efficiency in laser ablation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry for tissue analysis
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description: Recent studies have demonstrated the applicability of the "single particle" mode in laser ablation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry to map and size particles simultaneously. The transport efficiency (TE) is an important parameter in this configuration and affects the detection of individual nanoparticles, reliability of nanoparticle characterization, and related applications. This study introduces a novel method for the precise determination of TE, based on counting upconversion nanoparticles from gels characterized by fluorescent microscopy. The method was found to be most suitable for the 2940 nm laser ablation system, achieving virtually quantitative nanoparticle desorption, with TE primarily governed by ablation cell design and aerosol transport efficiency. With the 213 nm laser, attention had to be paid to incomplete desorption and possible nanoparticle redeposition at low laser fluences to avoid variability in TE measurements. Finally, use of the 193 nm laser induced nanoparticle disintegration, resulting in elevated baseline noise and lower sensitivity, which prevented the use of this approach for the determination of TE. This study highlights the versatility of the proposed method, while also identifying its limitations in terms of wavelength and fluence.

Agarose layers embedded with photon-upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) were prepared by mixing UCNP dispersion with 1.25% w/v molten agarose (NEEO ultra-quality, Carl Roth, Germany) at 60 °C and casting it between glass slides to form ~38 µm layers. After cooling and drying, predefined grid areas (500 × 500 µm²) were patterned using a 2940 nm OPO laser (Opolette 2940, Opotek, USA) at 42 J/cm².

Upconversion microscopy (UCM) was performed using a lab-built epifluorescence microscope with a 976 nm excitation diode laser (Roithner Lasertechnik, Austria) and an EM-CCD camera (iXon Life 888, Andor Technology, UK). Emission was filtered at 800 ± 25 nm, and images were analyzed by a convolutional neural network implemented in Python/Keras.

Laser ablation was conducted using three systems: a 2940 nm IR laser coupled to an Agilent 7900 quadrupole ICP-MS (Agilent Technologies, USA) via a Glass Expansion low-volume adapter;  a 213 nm Nd:YAG laser (LSX-213 G2+, Teledyne Photon Machines, USA) with HelEx II cell, connected to the same ICP-MS; and a 193 nm excimer laser (Analyte G2, Teledyne Photon Machines, USA) coupled to either the Agilent 7900 quadrupole ICP-MS or the Vitesse ICP TOF-MS (Nu Instruments, UK) via an ARIS interface.

Transport efficiency (TE) was calculated by comparing nanoparticle counts from LA SP ICP MS with those obtained from UCM in either randomly selected or spatially matched (grid-based) regions. Additional re-ablation and post-ablation UCM were used to assess residual particles and validate the completeness of NP desorption.

metadata.title: Data set for Milligram-Scale Enzymatic Synthesis of Base-Modified DNA through Primer Exension. Polymerase Synthesis and NMR Structures of DNA Containing Phenyl-Substituted Nucleobases
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The influence and specific interactions of chemical modifications in the DNA major groove have been systematically investigated for a long time, being useful in many applications. The standard sub-nanomolar quantities of enzymatically synthesized base-modified ONs or DNAs are usually not sufficient for structural determination by NMR or X-ray analyses and hence the structures of base-modified DNAs have not been systematically studied. To fill this gap in the enzymatic methodology for large-scale synthesis of base-modified DNA, we present here the scalable enzymatic synthesis of base-modified DNA in milligram scale. For this synthesis, four 2´-deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates bearing an electron-rich phenyl group at position 7 of 7-deazapurines and at position 5 of pyrimidines (dNPhTPs, N=A, G, C, U) were prepared according to published procedures and used in primer extension (PEX) and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) experiments with in-house expressed KOD(exo-) DNA polymerase. All four modified dNPhTPs served as good substrates in PEX providing phenyl-modified dsDNA in a milligram-quantities sufficient for extended structural studies (Tm, CD, MALDI-TOF), focusing mainly on proper NMR structural determination supported by additional molecular dynamics simulations. The main goal is particularly to understand the various biological processes and bioorthogonal reactions of macromolecules.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Metastable Evaporation of Molecules from Water Clusters
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The data set contains the mass spectra used in the article, which evaluates the properties of water clusters produced by supersonic expansion. In particular, the binding energies of negative water clusters and energy which is deposited during the interactions with electrons of energies ranging from near zero to 70 eV. Besides the mass spectra of positively and negatively charged water clusters, the data set contains also ratios of metastable mass peaks to their parent peaks.

Water molecular beam was formed by supersonic expansion. The expansion conditions was varied by expanding only water vapor or adding the water vapor Ne or Ar carrier gas.  Generated clusters were ionized by electrons with 70 eV energy. Electrons at low energy around 1-2 eV were attached to the clusters. Ions were analyzed with reflectron time-of-flight mass spectrometer exploiting different setting of reflectron to identify the matstable cluster fragments.
Mass calibration constants shown in .asc files were used to convert the time scale to mass scale.

metadata.title: Design and characterization of the setup for electron-ion trapping experiments
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description:
Supporting data for the article titled Design and characterization of the setup for electron-ion trapping experiments.
Abstract of the article:
This work presents the Electron-Ion Trap Experiment (EiTEx), a setup designed for microwave detection and the co-trapping of electrons and ions within the same region. EiTEx utilizes a custom-made dual-frequency Paul trap, leveraging Coulomb interactions to achieve efficient electron cooling and radio-frequency photon detection by monitoring changes in the spectral signature of the electron-ion compound quantum system. The system integrates 3D-printed ultrahigh vacuum-compatible high-quality microwave resonator and a neutral atom source, and a simple yet cost-effective optical system for imaging of ion Coulomb crystals. By enabling co-trapping of ions and electrons, EiTEx provides a robust platform for microwave detection and the exploration of electron-ion interactions, paving the way for future advancements in quantum sensing technologies.
Data from measurement devices were processed by custom scripts and plotted by custom scripts.
To each figure in the article, a dedicated folder is assigned that contains unprocessed data from measurement devices, scripts used for processing them and processed data.

metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The dataset contains the inputs for DMRG calculations, outputs, reduced density matrices and the quantum information measures.
Orbital entropies, pair entropies, and mutual information have become popular tools for analysis of strongly correlated wave functions.
They can quantitatively measure how strongly an orbital (e.g. from the DMRG active space) participates in the strong correlation
and reveal the correlation pattern between different orbitals.
However, this pattern can become rather complicated and sometimes difficult to interpret for large active spaces
and is not invariant with respect to the spin projection ($M_s$) component of the spin multiplet state.
We introduce a modified spin-free orbital entropy, pair entropy, and mutual information, which  simplify the correlation analysis and are invariant with respect to $M_s$.
By comparison of these quantities with their ``original'' spin-including counterparts
one can distinguish static correlation due to spin couplings from the ``genuine'' strong correlation  due to a multiconfigurational character of the wave function.
We illustrate the approach on a model consisting of a non-interacting dimer of triplet diradicals and
on a more realistic example of iron-sulfur bound complexes with one and two iron atoms.
Programs: Orca, Molmps, human-readable ASCII inputs/outputs

metadata.title: Statistics Data - Awareness of Ischemic Heart Disease Risk Factors in 40–64 year adults in the Czech Republic: a cross-sectional study
metadata.publisher: Jihočeská univerzita v Českých Budějovicích

additional_descriptions.description:
Objectives: The research aims to prepare the basis for targeted educational activities to increase awareness of IHD risk factors in middle-aged adult populations.
Methods: A cross-sectional study. A combination of a non-standard questionnaire dealing with stable ischemic heart disease and a standardized health literacy questionnaire (HLSQ-16) was used for data collection. The sample of citizens was a quota sample; its structure corresponded to the composition of all citizens of the Czech Republic in terms of gender, age, and region. It comprised 1,391 respondents.
Results: 66.9% of respondents answered that they knew heart disease risk factors. A significant correlation was identified between sex and the knowledge of risk factors of heart diseases (p=0.015), risk factors of high cholesterol (p=0.036), coffee (p=0.012), and contraception (p=0.000). In respondents taking part in regular physical examinations, a significant correlation was identified concerning the opinions of obesity (p=0.001), hypertension (p =0.004), heredity (p=0.000), immunity disorders (p=0.036), high cholesterol levels (p =0.003), coffee (p=0.011), hormonal contraception (p=0.001), smoking (p=0.000), stress (p=0.000), diabetes (p=0.005) as risk factors of heart disease. The population with a higher productive age (40 – 64 years) is homogeneous.
Conclusion: Based on this study, targeted educational activities for the 40-64 age group can be recommended to address specific risk factors and attitudes in this category. The study showed a satisfactory awareness of IHD risk factors in the population. However, there is room for improvement, especially in the 40-64 age group.

A cross-sectional study. A combination of a non-standard questionnaire dealing with stable ischemic heart disease and a standardized health literacy questionnaire (HLSQ-16) was used for data collection. The sample of citizens was a quota sample; its structure corresponded to the composition of all citizens of the Czech Republic in terms of gender, age, and region. It comprised 1,391 respondents.
Statistics data from research (processed in the program SASD) - Public and Nursis

metadata.title: Data set for Synthesis of 2-substituted ATP derivatives and their use in enzymatic synthesis and postsynthetic labelling of RNA
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: A series of adenosine triphosphate derivatives bearing chloro, fluoro, amino, methyl, vinyl and ethynyl groups at position 2 were synthesized and tested as substrates for RNA and DNA polymerases. The modified nucleotides worked well in in vitro transcription with T7 RNA polymerase and primer extension (PEX) using engineered DNA polymerases (TGK, 2M) except for the bulkier 2-vinyl- and 2-ethynyl-ATP derivatives that gave truncated products. However, in single nucleotide incorporation followed by PEX they still could be used for site-specific incorporation of reactive modifications into RNA that could be further used for post-synthetic labelling through thiol-ene or CuAAC reactions. All modified ATPs worked in polyadenylation catalyzed by poly(A) polymerase (ScPAP) to form long 3'-polyA tails containing the modifications that also can be used for labelling.

metadata.title: Dataset for Polymerase Synthesis of Hypermodified DNA Displaying a Combination of Thiol, Hydroxyl, Carboxylate and Imidazole Functional Groups in the Major Groove (ver. 2)
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: We designed and synthesized a set of six 2'-deoxyribonucleoside 5'-O-triphosphates (dNTPs) bearing functional groups mimicking amino acid side chains in enzyme active sites (OH, SH, COOH and imidazole) attached to position 5 of pyrimidines or position 7 of 7-deazapurines through different linkers. These modified dNTPs were studied as substrates in enzymatic synthesis of modified and hypermodified DNA using several DNA polymerases. In primer extension (PEX), all modified dNTPs provided DNA containing one, two, three, or (all) four modified nucleotides each bearing a different modification, although the thiol-modified dNTPs were worse substrates compared to the others. In PCR, we observed exponential amplification for any combination of one, two or three non-sulfur dNTPs but the thiol-modified dNTP did not work well in any combinations. Sequencing of the hypermodified DNA confirmed the good fidelity of the incorporation of all the modified nucleotides. This set of modified dNTPs extends the portfolio of building blocks for prospective use in selections of functional nucleic acids.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for anthraquinone switch
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset describes data for study of the electronic properties of molecules for molecular electronics. Functional molecular electronics require molecular design that provides integrity and stability. In this work, we explored two types of single molecule devices differing in anchoring to the conducting leads. Single molecule conductance was measured by STM break junction method and the molecular conductor was composed of the redox active anthraquinone center (switching element) containing either 4-pyridyl or p-phenylene thioacetate anchoring groups. The experimental results were supported by quantum chemical charge transport calculations. Molecular junctions containing 4-pyridyl anchors displayed two stable configurations with conductance values of 4.9 nS and 20 pS, respectively. Molecules anchored via p-phenylene thioacetate groups led to one main junction configuration with conductance of 0.1 nS. Junctions employing 4-pyridyl anchoring groups had higher junction formation probability, which in combination with lower conductance makes them better candidates for switching purposes.
Scanning Tunneling Microscopy Break Junction method, DFT calculations

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
The Dinuclear Zirconocene Complex as a Platform for Small Molecule Activation - experimental dataset
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains relevant raw data for the publication such as NMR and IR spectra, and results of DFT calculations. The dinuclear title compound [(Cp2Zr)2(μ-Me)(μ-C2Ph)] 5 was prepared from a zirconocene alkynyl methyl complex and Rosenthal's zirconocene source [Cp2Zr(py)(η2-Me3SiC2SiMe3)] in a formal comproportionation reaction. This complex shows catalytic activity for the dehydrocoupling of amine boranes, with a dinuclear hydride-bridged alkynyl complex 6 being formed as a catalytically relevant species. The structure of this complex was confirmed for the first time by single-crystal X-ray analysis. The reaction of complex 5 with hydrogen results in hydrogenation of the alkynyl ligand, yielding a highly labile trinuclear hydride-bridged complex as a possible intermediate of zirconocene dihydride/ethylbenzene formation. This complex shows an unusual distorted planar tetracoordinate environment at the central carbon atom positioned between the three Zr centers. The reaction of complex 5 with 2-cyanopyridine and acetonitrile is characterized by a reduction of the substrates. The herein reported reactivity of complex 5 demonstrates the remarkable potential of well-established dinuclear zirconocenes to stabilize unusual bond situations, which were analyzed comprehensively using spectroscopic, structural, and computational methods.

NMR spectra recorded on a Bruker Avance Neo 500, IR spectra recorded on a Nicolet Avatar spectrometer (ATR, Si crystal), DFT calculations (details in the file). For details see the article and its supporting information file.

metadata.title: Datová sada pro Údaje o zdravotní gramotnosti a depresi seniorů ve věku 65 a více let
metadata.publisher: Jihočeská univerzita v Českých Budějovicích

additional_titles.title: Data matrix for Data on Health literacy and depression of Seniors aged 65 and over

additional_descriptions.description:
Cílem výzkumu bylo popsat úroveň zdravotní gramotnosti u seniorů Jihočeského kraje ve vztahu k depresi.  Zdravotní gramotnost lze sledovat v oblasti zdravotní péče, prevence nemocí a podpory zdraví.

Metody: Výzkum byl realizován prostřednictvím kvantitativní výzkumné strategie pomocí řízeného rozhovoru. Výzkum byl koncipován jako sociologický. Struktura rozhovoru byla odvozena od standardizovaného dotazníku HLS-EU-Q16, doplněného o otázek sociodemografického charakteru, a hodnocení subjektivního zdravotního stavu a zdravotního chování. Celkem bylo osloveno 403 jedinců vybraných kvótním výběrem, Z hlediska pohlaví tvoří soubor 43,7% mužů a 56,3% žen, ve věku 65- 74 let bylo 57,9% respondentů, ve věku 75 –84let -32,9%, ve věku 85 a více let- 9% respondentů.  

Výsledky: Úroveň zdravotní gramotnosti u seniorů Jihočeského kraje ve věku 65 a více let byla u 9% jako dostatečná, u 24,1% dotazovaných problematická, a u 16,1% dotázaných byla zjištěna neadekvátní (nedostatečná) zdravotní gramotnost. Úroveň zdravotní gramotnosti roste s úrovní nejvyššího dosaženého vzdělání. U seniorů, u kterých byla naměřena neadekvátní zdravotní gramotnost, ve významně větší míře trpí mírnou depresí.

Závěr: V budoucnosti by se měla výzkumná šetření zaměřit na roli nízké zdravotní gramotnosti u osob trpící depresí.

Objective: The research aimed to describe the level of health literacy among seniors in the South Bohemian Region aged 65+ about depression.
Design: descriptive and cross-sectional
Participants: 403 individuals selected by quota sampling were included in the study. The sample is representative in terms of gender, age, and region.

Methods: The interview structure was derived from the standardized questionnaire HLS-EU-Q16, supplemented by socio-demographic questions.

Results: The level of health literacy among seniors was considered sufficient for 59.9%, problematic for 24.1% of respondents, and inadequate (insufficient) health literacy was found for 16.1% of respondents. Seniors who were measured to have inadequate health literacy were found to suffer from mild depression to a significantly great extent.

Conclusion: Research should focus on the role of low health literacy in people suffering from depression.
datová matice zpracovaná v programu IBM SPSS Statistics 30.0

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
The library of simulated STED-FCS diffusion law plots
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Formation of functional nanoscopic domains is an inherent property of plasma membranes. Stimulated emission depletion combined with fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (STED-FCS) has been used to identify such domains, however, the information obtained by STED-FCS has been limited to presence of such domains while crucial parameters have not been accessible, such as size, the fraction of occupied membrane surface, in-membrane lipid diffusion inside and outside the nanodomains as well as their self-diffusion. Here, based on a revision of the 'diffusion law', we present an approach to retrieve these five parameters from STED-FCS data. We verify that approach on ganglioside nanodomains in giant unilamellar vesicles (GUVs), validating the Saffman-Delbrück assumption for domain diffusion. We examined STED-FCS data in both plasma membranes of living PtK2 cells and in giant plasma membrane vesicles (GPMVs) and present a quantitative framework for molecular diffusion modes in biological membranes.

The provided macro contains an extensive set of simulated membrane probe diffusion coefficients for different focal spot sizes in membranes containing mobile nanodomains of different characteristics. By inserting your experimentally measured diffusion coefficients for given focal spots, the macro calculates the chi-squared parameters characterizing the similarity between the experimentally measured and simulated STED-FCS diffusion law plot. The diffusion coefficient for molecules outside of membrane nanodomains, Dout, can be adjusted to more closely align with the sample's conditions, enhancing the precision of the fit. If the table does not yield satisfactory results, or if you wish to simulate the probe diffusion under different conditions a simulation code is included to this macro.

The provided code enables users to simulate probe diffusion in nanoscopically heterogeneous membranes containing mobile nanodomains. Simulation input parameters are specified at the beginning of the code. The output, which consists of an intensity fluctuation trace, can be saved to a specified location on your computer by defining the desired file path. For guidance on saving data and extracting parameters necessary for intensity correlation, refer to the section "saving data and extracting parameters for intensity correlation" located at the end of the code. The intensity time trace needs to be auto-correlated by 'standard' software to receive an autocorrelation function and the auto-correlation function fitted to retrieve the measured probe diffusion coefficient.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Resonance Raman scattering and anomalous anti-Stokes phenomena in CrSBr
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The data set underpins the Raman, resonance Raman (both including polarization-dependent measurements, also in the antiStokes region), photoluminescence, and optical absorption study of CrSBr crystals, a van der Waals, air-stable semiconductor material, showing its appealing intrinsic properties such as crystalline anisotropy, quasi-1D electronic characteristics, layer-dependent antiferromagnetism, and non-linear optical effects. The data show the differences between the absorption and emission spectra, focusing on the origin of the emission peak near 1.7 eV observed in the photoluminescence spectrum of CrSBr. Excitation-dependent Raman measurements in the relevant spectral region evidence the indirect nature of the 1.7 eV emission peak. The anti-Stokes Raman spectra show anomalously high anti-Stokes to Stokes intensity ratio of up to 0.8, which varies significantly with excitation laser power and crystallographic orientation relative to the polarization of the scattered light. Lastly, we examine stimulated Raman scattering (with a continuous 1.96 eV laser excitation) for the A2 Raman mode and calculate the Raman gain in CrSBr, which attains a value of 1 × 10<sup>8</sup> cm/GW, nearly four orders of magnitude higher than that of previously studied three-dimensional systems.
Raman spectroscopy, resonant Raman spectroscopy, photoluminescence, optical absorption
Data are provided in accordance with individual figures published in the article, including statistics on peak fitting. The labeling of the files and sets follows the labeling of the related figures.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Tuning of MoS2 Photoluminescence in Heterostructures with CrSBr
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset supports the published article " Tuning of MoS2 Photoluminescence in Heterostructures with CrSBr."  In this work, we present a method for modulating the photoluminescence (PL) of monolayer (1L) MoS2 through its interaction with the semiconductor CrSBr. We find that CrSBr layers up to 18 nm thick enhance MoS2 PL, while thicker flakes tend to quench it. This behavior is attributed to an n- to p-doping transition in MoS2, driven by charge transfer in a type-II band alignment. Our findings are supported by first-principles calculations, transport measurements, Kelvin probe force microscopy, and time-resolved PL spectroscopy. Additionally, we fabricate a lateral CrSBr/MoS2 photodetector operating in the AC regime, achieving a responsivity of 105 A/W.
Sample preparation. Optical, atomic force, and kelvin probe force microscopy. Raman and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Electrical characterization. Photodetector characteristics.

The data is provided in Excel spreadsheets or text files, wherever possible. Some datasets are provided in their respective software formats, which cannot be exported to Excel or text directly. All the datasets are accompanied by the respective figures and located in folders named after the figures (eg, Figure 1, Figure 2, etc.).

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Experimental data for "Electron attachment to complexes of polyaromatic hydrocarbons with O2 and CO2"
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains negative ion mass spectra of naphthalene, phenanthrene and anthracene clusters both with and without doping with O2 and CO2 molecules. Investigating such systems is important in order to more thoroughly understand how the properties of the constituents (e.g electron affinity) affect fundamental processes relevant from the astrochemical point of view.

Cluster beams of naphthalene, phenanthrene and anthracene were produced in supersonic expansion. Afterwards, carbon dioxide and oxygen molecules were picked up on the clusters. Both the doped and pure cluster beams were ionized with low-energy electrons and thus formed ions were detected using time-of-flight spectrometer.

The main folder contains several subfolders corresponding to a specific date. The subfolders hold four types of files belonging to a particular measurement (.asc; .mas; .tfa; .tfb). Mass spectra with calibration constants (the header) can be found in the .asc file; .tfa file contains TOF setting experimental information; .tfb bears the mass spectra in binary format and .mas contains only the calibration constants. The main folder also houses "data_details.xlsx", which assigns data to the corresponding figures in the main article and the supporting information document.
Mass calibration constants shown in .asc files were used to transform the raw spectra from time to mass over charge domain. These primary data are further recalibrated more precisely by a polynomial based on the real mass of assigned fragments. Recalibrated data are then used in figures. Electron energies (found within .tfa files) were also calibrated as described in the main article.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Experimental data for "Electron attachment to CH3COCl molecule and clusters"
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Uploaded files contain data for measured mass spectra of CH3COCl molecule and clusters after an electron attachment. The interpretation of the experimental results is supported and further explained with ab-initio calculations. Together with theoretical calculations, this work expand the knowledge about the influence of environment on electron-molecule interaction and contributes to the field of atmospheric chemistry, more specifically to the topic of halogenated pollutants residues.

Data set contains three file types: ascii with extension .asc - mass spectrum with mass calibration constants and spectrum resolution as a header; binary with extension .tfb - raw spectrum in time domain; text with extension .tfa - parameters of the detector. The data file to figure assignment is in the info.txt file. Cluster/molecular beam was formed in supersonic expansion, ionized by electron attachment and ions then analyzed with reflectron time-of-flight mass spectrometer.

Mass calibration constants shown in .asc files were used to transform the raw spectra from time to mass over charge domain. These primary data are further recalibrated more precisely by a polynomial based on the real mass of assigned fragments. Recalibrated data are then used in figures.

Dataset containing the quantum chemical calculations published in: "Importance of Electron Correlation on the Geometry and Electronic Structure of [2Fe–2S] Systems: A Benchmark Study of the [Fe2S2(SCH3)4]2–,3–,4–, [Fe2S2(SCys)4]2–, [Fe2S2(S-p-tol)4]2–, and [Fe2S2(S-o-xyl)4]2– Complexes"
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This dataset contains the primary data related to the publication "Importance of Electron Correlation on the Geometry and Electronic Structure of [2Fe–2S] Systems: A Benchmark Study of the [Fe2S2(SCH3)4]2–,3–,4–, [Fe2S2(SCys)4]2–, [Fe2S2(S-p-tol)4]2–, and [Fe2S2(S-o-xyl)4]2– Complexes".
Specifically it contains files related to the portion of quantum chemical calculations which were carried out at the Heyrovsky Institute of Physical Chemistry.
Included are the input and output files for the Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) and Adiabatic Connection (AC) calculations of the FES Clusters, including 1 and 2 RDMs from the DMRG calculations.

metadata.title: Dataset for Polymerase Synthesis of Hypermodified DNA Displaying a Combination of Thiol, Hydroxyl, Carboxylate and Imidazole Functional Groups in the Major Groove
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: We designed and synthesized a set of six 2'-deoxyribonucleoside 5'-O-triphosphates (dNTPs) bearing functional groups mimicking amino acid side chains in enzyme active sites (OH, SH, COOH and imidazole) attached to position 5 of pyrimidines or position 7 of 7-deazapurines through different linkers. These modified dNTPs were studied as substrates in enzymatic synthesis of modified and hypermodified DNA using several DNA polymerases. In primer extension (PEX), all modified dNTPs provided DNA containing one, two, three, or (all) four modified nucleotides each bearing a different modification, although the thiol-modified dNTPs were worse substrates compared to the others. In PCR, we observed exponential amplification for any combination of one, two or three non-sulfur dNTPs but the thiol-modified dNTP did not work well in any combinations. Sequencing of the hypermodified DNA confirmed the good fidelity of the incorporation of all the modified nucleotides. This set of modified dNTPs extends the portfolio of building blocks for prospective use in selections of functional nucleic acids.

metadata.title: Staining Tissues with Basic Blue 7: A New Dual-Polarity Matrix for MALDI Mass Spectrometry Imaging
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description: Obtaining high-quality matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (MALDI MS) images and the reproducibility of the technique depend strongly on the sample preparation protocol. The most crucial part is the application of the MALDI matrix, which often relies on expensive spraying or sublimation coaters. In this work, we present a new dual-polarity matrix for MALDI mass spectrometry imaging (MSI): Basic Blue 7 (BB7), which belongs to the group of triarylmethane dyes. Thanks to its good solubility in water, this matrix allows a quick and simple sample preparation protocol without the need for sophisticated spraying or sublimation instrumentation: dipping the glass with tissue into the dye solution. This technique closely resembles the staining methods employed in classical histopathology. The technique is demonstrated on MSI of lipids in mouse brain sections in positive and negative ion modes using a subatmospheric pressure MALDI source coupled with an orbital trap mass spectrometer. The results are compared with traditional matrices, such as 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB) and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (DAN). BB7 excels, especially in negative ion mode, offering low background signals and high signal intensities of many lipid classes. Furthermore, the stained tissue can simply be inspected visually and allows basic histopathology annotation prior to MSI. Here, we demonstrate that staining offers excellent image quality, reproducible sample preparation, and the potential for automation and utilization for high spatial resolution MSI.

A series of sections from the same brain was stained with BB7 or covered with classical matrices by sublimation in a glass sublimation chamber (model GPE-1207–030PS, GPE Scientific, United Kingdom). An amount of 200 mg of DHB or DAN was homogeneously spread in the bottom part of the sublimation chamber. The sublimation parameters were 135 °C, pressure ≤ 100 mTorr, 4 min 20 s for DHB. DAN was sublimated at 130 °C, pressure ≤ 100 mTorr for 2 min 20 s.

For BB7 staining, 100 mg of BB7 (dye content 90%) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was sonicated for 5 min in an ultrasonic bath and decanted prior to staining to remove residual undissolved dye particles. Subsequently, the glass slide with the tissue section was immersed 3 times for 10 s and 3 times for 30 s, for positive and negative mode measurements, respectively.

MALDI MS and MSI data were acquired using an orbital trap (Q Exactive Plus Orbitrap, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany) equipped with a subatmospheric (subAP) MALDI source (SubAP/MALDI(ng), MassTech, Inc., USA).

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for DFT Modeling of Polyethylene Chains Cross-linked by Selected ns1 and ns2 Metal Atoms
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
We analyze the structures, stabilities, and thermo-
chemical properties of polyethylene (PE) oligomer chains cross-
linked by metal (M) atoms through C−M−C bonds. Representa-
tive PEn−Mm−PEn complexes contain between 7 and 15 carbon
atoms in each oligomer and one to three Li, Be, Mg, Zn, Ag, or Au
cross-linking metal elements. PEn−Mm−PEn complexes are
quasiplanar with nearly parallel PE chains. Their stability is
determined by covalent C−M−C bonds accompanied by non-
covalent dispersion interactions between PEn chains. Using the
CAM-B3LYP+D3BJ+ABC functional, the binding energies of
PE15−M−PE15 with respect to two PE15 radicals and metal
fragments are −225, −230, −322, −551, −289, and −303 kJ/mol
for Li, Ag, Au, Be, Mg, and Zn atoms, respectively. Entropy contributions (109 to 121 kJ/mol at 298.15 K) destabilize all complexes
significantly. With two cross-linking metal elements in PE15−M2−PE15 complexes, binding energies are about double. Complexes
with several open-shell Li, Ag, or Au doublet atoms have spins located on separated C−M−C bonds. High-spin PE15−Mm1−PE15−
Mm2−PE15 complexes of three PE oligomers cross-linked by up to five doublet metal atoms create parallel PE tubes, which are
suggested as elementary cells for modeling magnetic polymer tubes.
quantum chemical computations: DMRG

computation results of the part of the work done by J. Pittner

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for DMRG-Tailored Coupled Cluster Method in the 4c-Relativistic Domain: General Implementation and Application to the NUHFI and NUF3 Molecules
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
Heavy atom compounds represent a challenge for computational
chemistry due to the need for simultaneous treatment of relativistic and correlation
effects. Often such systems also exhibit strong correlation, which hampers the
application of perturbation theory or single-reference coupled cluster (CC) methods.
As a viable alternative, we have proposed externally correcting the CC method using
the density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) wave functions, yielding the
DMRG-tailored CC method. In a previous paper [J. Chem. Phys. 2020, 152, 174107],
we reported a first implementation of this method in the relativistic context, which was
restricted to molecules with real double group symmetry. In this work, we present a
fully general implementation of the method, covering complex and quaternion double
groups as well. The 4c-TCC method thus becomes applicable to polyatomic molecules,
including heavy atoms. For the assessment of the method, we performed calculations of
the chiral uranium compound NUHFI, which was previously studied in the context of
the enhancement of parity violation effects. In particular, we performed calculations of a cut of the potential energy surface of this
molecule along the stretching of the N−U bond, where the system exhibits strong multireference character. Since there are no
experimental data for NUHFI, we have performed also an analogous study of the (more symmetric) NUF3 molecule, where the
vibrational frequency of the N−U bond can be compared with spectroscopic data.

quantum chemical calculations
relativistic 4-component calculations
DFT
DMRG
taiilored CC

inputs and output of the underlying calculations in human readable format:
programs Dirac and Budapest-DMRG

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Heteroatom-vacancy centres in molecular nanodiamonds: a computational study of organic molecules possessing triplet ground states through sigma-overlap
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
Small molecules possessing a triplet ground state are fundamentally intriguing but also in high demand for applications such as quantum sensing and quantum computing. Such molecules are rare, and most examples involve extended π-systems. Topology and shape of the spin density will be very different for molecules where the triplet state arises from s-overlap. Drawing inspiration from NV- (anionic nitrogen-vacancy) centres in a diamond crystal, which possess triplet ground states that are robust due to the distortion-preventing crystal lattice, we investigate hetero-atom substituted diamondoids (molecular nanodiamonds) as molecular mimics for NV- centres. It is found that even in these small systems, distortions that stabilize singlet states are energetically costly, and the triplet states are more stable than the singlets. The stabilization of the triplet over the singlet is 13, 16, and 18 kcal/mol, in anionic C3v-C33H36N- and in the charge-neutral molecules C3v-C33H36O and C3v-C33H36S, respectively, using CAM-B3LYP-D3(BJ)/Def2-QZVPP. Comparable numbers are obtained with other density functional theory (DFT) methods, including double-hybrids. Wavefunction-based approaches on the other hand disagree in their predictions: While the MP2 method applied with the DLPNO approximation predicts a preference for the singlet, density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations qualitatively agree with DFT in their prediction of a triplet ground state, although by a small margin, for C3v-C33H36N- and C3v-C33H36O, but not for C3v-C33H36S. Weighing the evidence, we conclude, with reasonable confidence for C3v-C33H36N- and C3v-C33H36O and lesser confidence for C3v-C33H36S, that the ground state for the molecular nanodiamonds studied is a triplet state.
