metadata.title: Data for Unveiling stimulus transduction artifacts in auditory steady-state response experiments
metadata.publisher: Národní ústav duševního zdraví

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains hdEEG records of 15 subjects after excluding poor-quality records. Three records for each subject were recorded during the ASSR experiment, first with headphones stimulation, second with speakers stimulation, and third with speakers stimulation and distinctive muscle artifacts. The subjects were males and females, between the ages of 25 and 46. All the subjects were right-handed without any known neuropsychiatric diseases or hearing impairment. The experiment was conducted in accordance with the Helsinki Declaration of Human Rights. The ethical committee of the National Institute of Mental Health (Klecany, Czechia) approved the study. Each subject was, before recording, instructed about the risks and the measurement procedure and signed informed consent.

The entire dataset was recorded during the Auditory Steady State Response (ASSR) experiment by hdEEG. Each subject was measured three times in a row with a similar design. An ASSR protocol with click train stimulation was performed during all experiments. The click train stimulus was 500 ms, and it contained 20noise clicks, each lasting 1.5 ms. The noise clicks were repeated with a frequency of 40Hz. The number of trials was 150 in each condition. During all measurements, the subjects remained with open eyes fixed on a cross. The sound pressure level was calibrated to the loudness of 60dB SPL (sound pressure level) at the subjects' ears. Headphones were placed on the subject's head, and click train ASSR stimuli started. The subjects were stimulated only by headphones in this block (record 1 for each subject). The headphones were removed immediately after the end of stimulation. The same stimulation protocol continued in the second block (record 2 for each subject), but speakers were used as a stimulus source. In the third block (record 3 for each subject), subjects randomly produced a muscle artifact during speaker click train stimulation.

hdEEG records were acquired with MagStim EGI NetAmp GES 400 HdEEG amplifier with an EGI FICS (Field Isolation Containment System) electromagnetic shield box. We used EGI 256 channel sponge water-based net caps. For auditory stimulation, we used an Acoustique Quality M4 DO 2 × 50 W audio dual-channel amplifier. Acoustique Quality loudspeaker TANGO 85 WHITE speakers and Sennheiser hda 280 headphones were used. Speakers were placed at the height of the subject's head. Both speakers were 198 cm away from the subject and 203 cm away from each other. The experiments were performed in an electrically shielded room. The sound volume level was set and measured with an 8922 type GSH 8922 calibrated digital sound meter.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Strain-Induced Decoupling Drives Gold-Assisted Exfoliation of Large-Area Monolayer 2D Crystals
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This is a dataset supporting a published article "Strain-Induced Decoupling Drives Gold-Assisted Exfoliation of Large-Area Monolayer 2D Crystals".
Gold-assisted exfoliation (GAE) is a groundbreaking mechanical exfoliation technique that produces centimeter-scale single-crystal monolayers of 2D materials. Such large, high-quality films offer unparalleled advantages over the micron-sized flakes typically produced by conventional exfoliation techniques, significantly accelerating the research and technological advancements in the field of 2D materials. Despite its wide applications, the fundamental mechanism of GAE remains poorly understood. In this study,
using MoS2 on Au as a model system, ultra-low frequency Raman spectroscopy is employed to elucidate how the interlayer interactions within MoS2 crystals are impacted by the gold substrate. The results reveal that the coupling at the first MoS2-MoS2 interface between the adhered layer on the gold substrate and the adjacent layer is substantially weakened, with the binding force being reduced to nearly zero. This renders the first interface the weakest point in the system, thereby the crystal preferentially cleaves at this junction, generating large-area monolayers with sizes comparable to the parent crystal. Biaxial strain in the adhered layer, induced by the gold substrate, is identified as the driving factor for the decoupling effect. The strain-induced decoupling effect is established as the primary mechanism of GAE, which can also play a significant role in general mechanical exfoliations.
Raman spectroscopy, ultra-low frequency Raman spectroscopy, optical microscopy, atomic force microscopy, density functional theory simulations

Data are provided in accordance with individual figures published in the article, including additional data from which statistics (averages and standard deviations) were obtained. Where relevant, detailed data acquisition conditions are given in a separate file. All general experimental details are given in the Experimental Section of the article.  Figure 1: Optical microphotographs (tif) and fit results of Raman spectroscopy for layer number determination (csv). Figure 2: Raman spectroscopy in the normal modes range with spectra selected for the figure and also all other spectra measured for the layer numbers (txt), including their averaged fitted positions (csv). Figure 3: Raman spectroscopy in the ultra-low frequency range (asc). Figure 4: fitted values with errors for 2-4 layers from Figure 3 (csv). Figure 5: Optical microphotographs (tif) and related Raman spectra (txt). Figure 6: DFT-calculated energy vs strain values (dat). Figure S1: Optical microphotographs (tif). Figure S2: Optical microphotograph (tif), AFM data (ibw), line profiles across the AFM data (csv). Figure S3: Additional Raman spectra for MoS2 on different substrates (asc). Figure S4: Fitted ULF peak positions for MoS2 on other substrates (csv). Figure S5: Optical microphotographs (tif).

metadata.title: Data pro Zdraví v kontextu zdravého stárnutí u osob ve věku 65+ v Jihočeském kraji
metadata.publisher: Jihočeská univerzita v Českých Budějovicích

additional_titles.title: Data for Health in the context of a healthy aging in people aged 65+ in the South Bohemian Region

additional_descriptions.description:
Cíle: Cílem výzkumu bylo objasnit vztahy mezi využívanou zdravotní péčí a její dostupností, přípravou na aktivní stáří a stárnutí a zapojení se do péče o své vlastní zdraví u osob ve věku 65 let a více.

Metodika:: Pro naplnění cíle byl využit kvalitativní výzkum (zakotvená teorie). Výzkum byl realizován od počátku února do konce dubna r. 2024. Informanty byly osoby ve věku 65 let a více, které žily v Jihočeském kraji. Bylo využito polostrukturovaných rozhovorů.

Výsledky: Jako centrální kategorie bylo identifikováno zdraví. Z dalších kategorií byly identifikovány: dostupnost péče, služby praktického lékaře, aktivní stáří, alternativní medicína a hospitalizace. Nalezené souvislosti ukazují, že je zdraví klíčem k naplnění myšlenky zdravého stáří a stárnutí, přičemž je v úzkém vztahu k soběstačnosti. Soběstačnost je přitom z pohledu aktivního stáří v očích informantů nezbytná. Jejímu udržení z pohledu plánování budoucnosti věnují pozornost. Nemoc je naopak faktorem ovlivňujícím úvahy o budoucnosti, o dostupnosti péče i o potřebě informací. Významným prvkem se tak stává i komunikace, která se celou koncepcí úspěšného stáří a stárnutí prolíná.

Závěr: Výsledky naznačují, že je v budoucnu nezbytné věnovat větší pozornost zkoumání očekávání pacientů a zdravotníků ohledně péče zaměřené na udržení a obnovení zdraví, případně na realizaci změn.

Pro naplnění cíle byl využit kvalitativní výzkum (zakotvená teorie). Výzkum byl realizován od počátku února do konce dubna r. 2024. Informanty byly osoby ve věku 65 let a více, které žily v Jihočeském kraji. Bylo využito polostrukturovaných rozhovorů.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dynamic Light Scattering Data of Large Unilamellar Vesicles with Reconstituted Sec61/TRAP
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The interleaflet transport of phospholipids is a crucial physiological process in the cell plasma membrane. This process can be facilitated by specific proteins—scramblases—without ATP consumption. We designed experiments, testing lipid scrambling activity of the Sec61/TRAP translocon, a protein complex previously known for facilitating the membrane translocation of nascent polypeptide chains. To investigate such an activity, we employed experiments based on NBD fluorescence quenching. Employment of these experiments requires reconstitution of Sec61/TRAP in large unilamellar vesicles. Here are provided data, controlling the polydispersity index (PDI) and distribution of hydrodynamic diameter of the LUVs before and after the reconstitution procedure, determined by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)

Large Unilamellar Vesicles (LUVs) composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC and 0.4% (mol) of either 16:0-12:0 NBD-PS or 16:0-06:0 NBD-PC (Avanti) were prepared by extrusion. Sec61/TRAP reconstitution in LUVsFor protein reconstitution into membranes, we followed the procedure of Brunner and Schenck. Specifically, LUVs were mixed with SEC buffer and 10% (v/w) Triton X-100 to final concentrations of 3 mM total lipid and 0.19% (v/w) detergent. This mixture was incubated for 20 minutes at room temperature to destabilize the LUVs. The protein was then added to a final concentration of 79.49 nM, resulting in a total lipid concentration of 1.77 mM.After one hour of incubation at room temperature with gentle mixing, 150 mg/mL of Bio-Beads SM2 (BioRad) was added. The mixture was incubated overnight at 277 K with gentle mixing. In parallel, a negative control was prepared in the same manner but with SECb buffer instead of protein solution. The size distribution of LUVs containing reconstituted translocon was assessed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano S, Malvern Panalytical). Measurements were performed at 298.15 K with N = 3 replicates.

The dataset contains DLS data from samples for Fluorescence Assays and Western Blot. For both measurment series is available original file, readable in Zetasizer software and excel file, contianing data and figures, extracted from the Zetasizer.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dithionite and BSA Fluorescence Quenching Data for Sec61/TRAP Lipid Scrambling Experiments
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The interleaflet transport of phospholipids is a crucial physiological process in the cell plasma membrane. This process can be facilitated by specific proteins—scramblases—without ATP consumption. The experimental data provided here describe the lipid scrambling activity of the Sec61/TRAP translocon, a protein complex previously known for facilitating the membrane translocation of nascent polypeptide chains. In this study, we reconstituted Sec61/TRAP into large unilamellar vesicles and employed two complementary fluorescence-based assays—NBD fluorescence quenching by bovine serum albumin and sodium dithionite—to investigate its scrambling activity. Both approaches confirmed that Sec61/TRAP facilitates lipid scrambling. Notably, blocking the Sec61 translocation cavity did not inhibit scrambling, suggesting the presence of an alternative lipid transport pathway within the translocon complex.

Sec61/TRAP was reconstituted into large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs) containing 0.4% (mol) NBD-labeled lipid. The labeled lipid is assumed to be equally distributed between both membrane leaflets. Upon the addition of sodium dithionite to the experimental mixture, all sterically accessible NBD molecules are reduced, leading to a fluorescence decrease of approximately 50% from the initial intensity. However, if a scramblase is present and actively facilitates rapid phospholipid translocation between leaflets, the NBD fluorescence ideally decreases to zero. Because sodium dithionite is a small molecule that could theoretically penetrate the membrane through pores, we conducted complementary experiments using bovine serum albumin (BSA). BSA can quench 50% of the NBD fluorescence but, due to its size, cannot cross the membrane. Experiments were performed in the presence of different inhibitors, demonstrating that steric blocking of the Sec61 cavity does not affect lipid scrambling. This result supports the relevance of TRAP in lipid scrambling activity.

Large Unilamellar Vesicles (LUVs)
LUVs composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC and 0.4% (mol) of either 16:0-12:0 NBD-PS or 16:0-06:0 NBD-PC (Avanti) were prepared by extrusion.

Sec61/TRAP reconstitution in LUVs

For protein reconstitution into membranes, we followed the procedure of Brunner and Schenck. Specifically, LUVs were mixed with SEC buffer and 10% (v/w) Triton X-100 to final concentrations of 3 mM total lipid and 0.19% (v/w) detergent. This mixture was incubated for 20 minutes at room temperature to destabilize the LUVs. The protein was then added to a final concentration of 79.49 nM, resulting in a total lipid concentration of 1.77 mM.

After one hour of incubation at room temperature with gentle mixing, 150 mg/mL of Bio-Beads SM2 (BioRad) was added. The mixture was incubated overnight at 277 K with gentle mixing. In parallel, a negative control was prepared in the same manner but with SECb buffer instead of protein solution. The size distribution of LUVs containing reconstituted translocon was assessed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer Nano S, Malvern Panalytical). The lipid concentrations are theoretical, based on the initial amounts used in preparation.

Fluorescence Experiments 

The dithionite assay was performed in a 96-well plate (Nunc MicroWell 96-Well Optical-Bottom, Thermo Scientific) using an Infinite M Nano+ (Tecan) plate reader. Samples containing reconstituted proteins and negative controls were measured in the same run. For each experiment, 9 μL of the reconstitution mixture was added to 191 μL of SECb buffer. Excitation was performed at 460 nm, and emission was detected at 535 nm. After temperature equilibration, fluorescence intensity was recorded at 15 or 20 s intervals for at least 180 s to establish an initial plateau. Then, 30 μL of 0.3 M sodium dithionite was added to all samples, including negative controls. The BSA assay was conducted using the same plate reader setup and initial reaction mixture composition as described for the dithionite assay. After temperature equilibration and recording the initial plateau, 30 μL of fatty acid-free BSA (25 mg/mL) was added. 

Effect of Sec61 Inhibitors 
Samples containing inhibitors were measured in parallel with inhibitor-free samples. Two wells of the 96-well plate contained vesicles with reconstituted translocon and 1 μM inhibitor, while two additional wells contained vesicles with translocon but without the inhibitor. Simultaneously, duplicate measurements were taken for negative controls (vesicles without reconstituted protein) under both inhibitor and non-inhibitor conditions.

The Infinite M Nano+ plate reader records measured values in an MS Excel sheet. The provided Excel file contains five sheets: 1_PC_BSA, 2_PC_dit, 3_PS_BSA, 4_PS_dit, and 5_FINAL. Sheets 1–4 include both raw and processed data from specific experiments. PC_BSA refers to the experiment with NBD-PC and BSA quenching. PS_dit corresponds to the dithionite assay with NBD-labeled PS, and so on. Each sheet contains: Experimental parameters, automatically recorded by the plate reader. Raw data (columns A–O). Normalized data and averaged normalized data. The averaged normalized data are labeled as follows: sample – Liposomes with reconstituted protein. sample_I – Sample with the inhibitor Ipomoeassin F. sample_A – Sample with the inhibitor Apratoxin A. sample_C – Control for dilution effect (buffer added instead of a quenching agent). nc – Negative control (liposomes without reconstituted protein). nc_C – Negative control with dilution effect control. To maintain a continuous experimental timeline, the time gap introduced by pipetting during the experiment has been subtracted. Operations involving time adjustments are recorded in the columns labeled "time". The final processed data for each experiment can be found in the "Summary" sheet.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Dataset for electrochemical oxidation of triticonazole published in Electrochemical Oxidation of Triticonazole on Boron-Doped Diamond-Based Electrodes for Analysis of Real Matrices
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The voltammetric behavior of the triazole fungicide triticonazole (TTC) was investigated for the first time using a boron-doped diamond electrode (BDDE). TTC provides several current responses on BDDE. An irreversible first anodic peak was observed at a potential of +1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl/KCl, sat.), while a second indistinctive oxidation peak was observed at approximately +2.0 V in the supporting electrolyte current onset, and a reduction peak was observed at a potential of 0 V. The mechanism of TTC oxidation was proposed and supported by theoretical calculations, electrochemical measurements, and the exhaustive electrolysis accompanied with product analysis using liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry (LC/MS). Subsequently, a voltammetric method for the determination of the fungicide was developed, employing square-wave voltammetry. This method exhibited a low limit of detection (0.125 µmol L-1), a broad linear dynamic range (0.5-20 µmol L-1), and excellent recovery in the analysis of pesticide preparations. Considering the low concentrations of TTC in natural waters, a sample pre-concentration technique using solid phase extraction was developed for these applications. The novel method was successfully transferred to screen-printed sensors with BDD electrodes (SP/BDDE). The obtained results indicate that SP/BDDE enable an expansion of the linear dynamic range (0.25-60 µmol L-1) of the proposed method. SP/BDDEs were successfully applied in the analysis of pesticide formulations and demonstrated 1.68 to 1.85 times better sensitivity.

Cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, chromatography HPLC-MS.
Density functional theory calculations of spatial distribution and energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) were performed using the B3LYP functional and 6-31G* basis set in Spartan '14, v.1.1.8 software (Wavefunction, Inc. USA). The spatial distribution of HOMO orbitals and atomic charges of radical cations of molecules TTC and proposed oxidation products were calculated for the geometry-optimized molecules in a vacuum.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Absorption and emission spectra and fluorescence lifetimes of lissamine rhodamine B in Caelyx-like liposomes
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset presents absorption and emission spectra and fluorescence lifetimes of lissamine rhodamine B in Caelyx-like liposomes used in the study as drug carries for nanomedicine.

Liposomes were prepared as above described, with minor adjustments. Briefly explained, dried lipid films with desired molar percentage DOPE-LR, were rehydrated in PBS (pH 7.4) and subsequently extruded to form liposomes. The lipid suspension was passed 31 times through a 100 μm polycarbonate filter in an Avanti Mini-Extruder (Avanti Polar Lipids, Inc., Alabaster, USA) at >10 ◦C above highest tm. Liposome stock solution (10 mM) was diluted 30 times in PBS to a final lipid concen- tration of 0.33 mM. 40% FBS solutions were formulated in PBS. Samples were vortexed and measured immediately. The data is presented in form of an amplitude-weighted averaged lifetime. Every point was measured 3 times for the same sample preparation. The samples were measured in PBS and PBS supplemented with 40% serum. Absorption spectroscopy profiles were recorded with a Shimadzu UV2600 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) at 25 ◦C and 65 ◦C in 0.5 cm Hellma Analytics quartz cuvettes (Hellma GmbH & Co. KG, Müllheim, Germany). Fluorescence steady-state and lifetime measurements were carried out with a Pico- quant FluoTime300 spectrofluorometer (Picoquant, Berlin, Germany) at 25 ◦C in 0.5 cm Hellma Analytics quartz cuvettes

metadata.title: VegVault: an interdisciplinary database linking paleo-, and neo-vegetation data with functional traits and abiotic drivers
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description:
Understanding the dynamics and persistence of biodiversity patterns over short (present) and long (thousands of years) time scales is crucial for predicting ecosystem changes under global climate and land-use changes. A key challenge is integrating currently scattered ecological data to assess complex vegetation dynamics over time. Here, we present VegVault, an interdisciplinary SQLite database that uniquely integrates paleo- and neo-ecological plot-based vegetation data on a global and millennial scale, directly linking them with functional traits, soil, and climate information. VegVault currently comprises data from BIEN, sPlotOpen, TRY, Neotoma, CHELSA, and WoSIS, providing a comprehensive and ready-to-use resource for researchers across various fields to address questions about past and contemporary biodiversity patterns and their abiotic drivers.To further support usability of the data, VegVault is complemented by the {vaultkeepr} R package, enabling streamlined data access, extraction, and manipulation. This study introduces the structure, content, and diverse applications of VegVault, emphasizing its role in advancing ecological research to improve predictions of biodiversity responses to global climate change.

More details on project website.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for "Proton affinities of aldehyde molecules determined from the forward and backward gas-phase proton transfer reactions in SIFDT"
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were performed using the B3LYP functional with the 6-311++G(d,p) basis set and D4 dispersion correction to study neutral and protonated structures of aldehydes. Geometry optimisations were carried out for all neutral molecules and bent protonated ions. Thermodynamic properties such as enthalpy (H), entropy (S), and Gibbs free energy (G) were calculated for each optimised structure, and the respective ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG values were at 298.15 K.

 

The dataset includes ORCA output files containing charge distributions and thermodynamic data. All data are organised in a single file, each named according to the compound and its geometry (neutral or protonated).

additional_descriptions.description: Topology and input files for classical molecular dynamics simulations of lipase CALB and its mutants in water, acetonitrile and toluene. They accompany the draft of the manuscript in the title.
Classical molecular dynamics simulations in Gromacs programm package.

The force field parameters are summarized in the .top files. Their name contains the name of the mutant of CALB (Wild type, G or N mutant) and solvent. The "-pull" in the name of the file indicates presence of distance restraints employed during pulling simulations. The .mdp files are the input files details for performing the simulation.

metadata.title: Enzymatic Synthesis of Reactive RNA Probes Containing Squaramate-Linked Cytidine or Adenosine for Bioconjugations and Cross-Linking with Lysine-Containing Peptides and Proteins
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Protein-RNA interactions play important biological roles and hence reactive RNA probes for cross-linking with proteins are important tools in their identification and study. To this end, we designed and synthesized 5′-O-triphosphates bearing a reactive squaramate group attached to position 5 of cytidine or position 7 of 7-deazaadenosine and used them as substrates for polymerase synthesis of modified RNA. In vitro transcription with T7 RNA polymerase or primer extension using TGK polymerase was used for synthesis of squaramate-modified RNA probes which underwent covalent bioconjugations with amine-linked fluorophore and lysine-containing peptides and proteins including several viral RNA polymerases or HIV reverse transcriptase. Inhibition of RNA-depending RNA polymerases from Japanese Encephalitis virus was observed through formation of covalent cross-link which was partially identified by MS/MS analysis. Thus, the squaramate-linked NTP analogs are useful building blocks for the synthesis of reactive RNA probes for bioconjugations with primary amines and cross-linking with lysine residues.

metadata.title: Small solar flare in region NOAA 13057 on 18 July 2022 from Solar Patrol, Ondřejov observatory
metadata.publisher: Astronomický ústav AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset captures an evolution of a small flare that peaked on 18 July 2022 at around 14.00 UT. The observations were made using a full-disc telescope of the Solar Patrol service, Astronomical Institute of the Czech Academy of Sciences in H-alpha line. The dataset consists of 760 frames spanning from 13.57 UT to 14.09 UT with an average cadence of about 1 frame per second. The dataset was coaligned (solar north is up), all the necessary metadata respecting the Solarnet convention are available within headers of the individual FITS files. A short overview video is also available.

The observations were captures in the H-alpha line using a full-disc telescope at the Solar Patrol at Ondřejov Observatory. The post-processing involved the coalignment of the individual frames with the WCS-respecting coordinate system, cropping regions far from solar disc, and ingestion of the frame-by-frame spacific metadata respecting the Solarnet convention to the headers of the individual FITS files.
Frame-by-frame observations, 2200x2200 pixel frames. Coronado SolarMax II H-alpha filter. Refractor 150/750 mm. ZWO MONO Astro Camera ASI1600MM.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Catalytic dehalogenation with activated borane, a porous borane cluster polymer - analytical data
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Activated borane (ActB), a metal- and halogen-free porous borane cluster polymer with a significant Lewis acidity, has been successfully used as a heterogeneous catalyst for hydrodehalogenation reactions of aliphatic fluorides and chlorides using triethylsilane as a reductant. In analogy to known homogeneous systems, full dehalogenation of organohalides is achieved with a predominant formation of Friedel–Crafts alkylation products in aromatic reaction media. Importantly, the herein described material is robust, tolerates elevated reaction temperatures and can be re-used, while reaching a turnover number (TON) of up to 5190. These features make it an attractive candidate for a sustainable disposal of halogenated pollutants by heterogeneous catalysis.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Gas chromatography (GC) and Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) analyses, Adsorption isotherms, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM).
See the experimental section in the Supporting Information file at https://doi.org/10.1039/D4CY00732H

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Simulated diffusion coefficients of membrane probes with the code
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Formation of functional nanoscopic domains is an inherent property of plasma membranes. Stimulated emission depletion combined with fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (STED-FCS) has been used to identify such domains, however, the information obtained by STED-FCS has been limited to presence of such domains while crucial parameters have not been accessible, such as size, the fraction of occupied membrane surface, in-membrane lipid diffusion inside and outside the nanodomains as well as their self-diffusion. Here, based on a revision of the 'diffusion law', we present an approach to retrieve these five parameters from STED-FCS data. We verify that approach on ganglioside nanodomains in giant unilamellar vesicles (GUVs), validating the Saffman-Delbrück assumption for domain diffusion. We examined STED-FCS data in both plasma membranes of living PtK2 cells and in giant plasma membrane vesicles (GPMVs) and present a quantitative framework for molecular diffusion modes in biological membranes.

The provided macro contains an extensive set of simulated membrane probe diffusion coefficients for different focal spot sizes in membranes containing mobile nanodomains of different characteristics (see the parameters specified in the yellow box). By inserting your experimentally measured diffusion coefficients for given focal spots, the macro calculates the chi-squared parameters characterizing the similarity between the experimentally measured and simulated STED-FCS diffusion law plot. The diffusion coefficient for molecules outside of membrane nanodomains, Dout, can be adjusted to more closely align with the sample's conditions, enhancing the precision of the fit. If the table does not yield satisfactory results, or if you wish to simulate the probe diffusion under different conditions a simulation code is included to this macro.

The provided code enables users to simulate probe diffusion in nanoscopically heterogeneous membranes containing mobile nanodomains. Simulation input parameters are specified at the beginning of the code. The output, which consists of an intensity fluctuation trace, can be saved to a specified location on your computer by defining the desired file path. For guidance on saving data and extracting parameters necessary for intensity correlation, refer to the section "saving data and extracting parameters for intensity correlation" located at the end of the code. The intensity time trace needs to be auto-correlated by 'standard' software to receive an autocorrelation function and the auto-correlation function fitted to retrieve the measured probe diffusion coefficient.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for a SIFT study of reactions of positive and negative ions with polyfluoroalkyl (PFAS) molecules in dry and humid nitrogen at 393 K
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The rate coefficients and product ion branching ratio of the reactions of H3O+, NO+, O2+•, OH-,O2-•, O-•, NO2-, and NO3- with selected representative polyfluoroalkyl (PFAS) molecules: one fluorocarbon (perfluoro-2-methyl-2-pentene), five acids (pentafluoropropionic, heptafluorobutyric, nonafluorobutane-1-sulfonic, perfluorohexanoic, and perfluoro(2-methyl-3-oxahexanoic) acids) and two alcohols (nonafluoro-1-hexanol and tridecafluoro-1-octanol). The experiments were conducted using a selected ion flow tube (SIFT), the Voice200, in N2 at 393 K under dry and humid conditions. The raw data are organised into two main folders, each named according to the specific condition. Within these folders, data for each specific compound are consolidated into a single file. Each file contains:

w  A peak table with raw data spectra for all reagents reacting with the specific compound. 

w  Two Excel files: one detailing the branching ratios and the other containing the rate coefficients.

w  PDF.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Spectroelectrochemistry and sensing of GFE
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
New voltammetric and flow amperometric methods for the determination of guaifenesin (GFE) using a
perspective screen-printed sensor (SPE) with boron-doped diamond electrode (BDDE) were developed. The
electrochemical oxidation of GFE was studied on the surface of the oxygen-terminated BDDE of the sensor. The
GFE provided two irreversible anodic signals at a potential of 1.0 and 1.1 V (vs. Ag|AgCl|KCl sat.) in Britton-
Robinson buffer (pH 2), which was chosen as the supporting electrolyte for all measurements. First, a voltammetric
method based on differential pulse voltammetry was developed and a low detection limit (LOD = 41
nmol L- 1), a wide linear dynamic range (LDR = 0.1–155 μmol L- 1), and a good recovery in the analysis of model
and pharmaceutical samples (RSD <3.0 %) were obtained. In addition, this sensor demonstrated excellent
sensitivity and reproducibility in the analysis of biological samples (RSD <3.2 %), where the analysis took place
in a drop of serum (50 μL) without pretreatment and additional electrolyte. Subsequently, SP/BDDE was
incorporated into a flow-through 3D printed electrochemical cell and a flow injection analysis method with
electrochemical detection (FIA-ED) was developed, resulting in excellent analytical parameters (LOD = 86 nmol
L- 1, LDR = 0.1–50 μmol L- 1). Moreover, the mechanism of electrochemical oxidation of GFE was proposed based
on calculations of HOMO spatial distribution and spectroelectrochemical measurements focused on IR identification
of intermediates and products.
Cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, density functional calculations, IR spectroelectrochemistry

metadata.title: Data k rukopisu AuAg nanoklastry - podmínky zrání a dopad vybraných kovových kationtů na luminiscenci AuAg nanoklastrů
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Palackého v Olomouci

additional_titles.title: Data to manuscript AuAg nanoclusters - maturing conditions and the impact of selected metal cations on AuAg nanocluster luminescence

additional_descriptions.description: Gold-silver synergism has been well documented in many scientific works dealing with luminescent nanostructures that are exploitable in biomedical and environmental application. Frequently, the ratio of Au : Ag in synthetic mixtures was varied to influence the extent of Au-Ag synergism of the resulting luminescent gold-silver nanoclusters (LGSN). However, in our approach, a new step, maturing under differing conditions using the same Au : Ag ratio (5 : 1), has been investigated systematically for the very first time. As referent systems, monometallic gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and protein treated by the conditions of synthesis and maturing were prepared and investigated. The selected types of maturing conditions led to distinct changes in fluorescence characteristics and, consequently, Au-Ag synergism extent (evaluated as the ratio of fluorescence quantum yields (FQY) of GSNC vs. AuNCs). The best synergism was obtained for GSNC matured at 37°C for 2.5h. The stability of luminescent signal of these GSNC was tested in the presence of an excess (till 20 mM) of Cu (II) and/or Fe (III) ions (crucial cofactors in living systems). The same metallic ions concentration caused different extents of GSNC luminescence quenching, for which a plausible reasoning is suggested.

The data set is related to the characterization of luminescent AuAg nanoclusters being synthesized in the laboratory of Dept. of Experimental Physics, Faculty of Science, Palacký University Olomouc. The laboratory synthesis is followed by varying maturing conditions and subsequently by purification of the nanocomposites. Then, the characterization of gold-silver luminescent nanoclusters embedded in protein-matrix is performed by means of several experimental techniques available such as: UV-vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dischroism, dynamic light scattering, zeta potential measurements, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, high-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy, energy dispersive spectroscopy. Based on fluorescence quantum yield, synergism of AuAg is assessed. Finally, the impact of selected metal cations (copper and ferric ions) on AuAg nanocomposite luminescence is evaluated.

The sets of experimental data were recorded on several instruments within Palacký University Olomouc. In cases where possible (UV-vis, fluorescence, DLS, zeta potential, XPS, CD), the data are in csv format enabling any replotting.

metadata.title: Polycaprolactone nanofibers for construction of the alveolar-capillary interface model: Detailed data
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_titles.title: Cell culture and co-culture analysis
Cell co-culture scaffolds production
Nanofibers production and characterization

additional_descriptions.description: This data collection contains the datasets showing the preparation and characterization of polycaprolactone nanofibers for the proof‑of‑concept construction of the alveolar‑capillary interface. We include parameters of nanofibers manufacturing as well as their characterization. We prepared nanofibers from polycaprolactone, polylactic acid and polyamide. We used polycaprolactone nanofibers to model the alveolar-capillary interface of human lung: We electrospun the nanofibers onto supporting mesh and incorporated the whole structure into 3D-printed insert to create the nanofibrous cell co-culture scaffold. For reproducing the 3D-printing of 24-well plate co-culture insert, see also the GitHub repository https://github.com/Grindyd/Nanofiber-holder-insert/. We seeded the scaffold with capillary endothelial cells (HUVEC) and alveolar epithelial cells (ELEP) to mimic the alveolar-capillary interface. For reproducing our protocol for differentiation of ELEP (Expandable lung epithelium) from hESCs (Human embryonic stem cells) see our protocol in the publication, DOI: 10.1007/s13770-022-00458-0. Importantly, we include detailed data from cell culture and co-culture experiments leading to construction of the in vitro alveolar-capillary interface proof-of-concept model. Some conclusions based on these data have been summarized in this publication: https://doi.org/10.1002/jbm.a.37824.

Nanofibers production and characterization: We produced nanofibers by electrospinning method, using Nanospider technology. The nanofibers were electrospun onto supporting polyamide mesh. The characterization of nanofibrous structures provided in this dataset is based on advanced microscopic techniques (SEM).

Cell co-culture scaffolds production: The nanofibrous structures electrospun onto supporting polyamide mesh were mounted into 3D-printed polyamide insert and used for cell culture and co-culture. We provide detailed description of this arrangement and the methodology used to get it.

Cell culture and co-culture analysis: The analysis of cell culture and co-culture provided in this dataset is based on standard microscopic techniques (brightfield microscopy), advanced microscopic techniques (SEM), biochemical methods (MTT and CV assay).

Nanofibers production and characterization: We used three polymers - polycaprolactone, polyamide and polylactic acid. The polymers were dissolved to 7-9% solutions in acetic acid, formic acid, trifluoracetic acid and their mixtures. The electrospinning was performed using rotating wire electrode. Detailed technical information are incorporated into the dataset (in form of a table).

Cell co-culture scaffolds production: The scaffolds were prepared in the form fitting to standard 24-well culture plates. The scaffolds consist of nanofibers on supporting UHELON mesh mounted into 3D-printed inserts. For reproducing the 3D-printing of 24-well plate co-culture insert, use attached .stl files or see also the GitHub repository https://github.com/Grindyd/Nanofiber-holder-insert/. Detailed technical information are incorporated into the dataset (in form of a schematic pictures/descripted photos).

Cell culture and co-culture analysis: HUVEC and ELEP cells were seeded using seeding densities ranging from 5000 to 20000 cells/cm2. The cells were propagated in the culture (co-culture) on the scaffolds for maximum of six days. Detailed technical information are incorporated into the dataset (in the form of data description - name of files or readme files).

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Are Redox-Active Centers Bridged by Saturated Flexible Linkers Systematically Electrochemically Independent?
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The extent to which electrophores covalently
bridged by a saturated linker are electrochemically
independent was investigated considering the charge/
spin duality of the electron and functionality of the
electrophore as a spin carrier upon reduction. By
combining computational modeling with electrochemical
experiments, we investigated the mechanism by which
tethered electrophores react together within 4,4'-oligo-
[n]methylene-bipyridinium assemblies (with n=2 to 5).
We show that native dicationic electrophores (redox
state Z= +2) are folded prior to electron injection into
the system, allowing the emergence of supra-molecular
orbitals (supra-MOs) likely to support the process of the
reductive σ bond formation giving cyclomers. Indeed,
for Z= +2, London Dispersion (LD) forces contribute
to flatten the potential energy surface such that all-trans
and folded conformers are approximately isoenergetic.
Then, upon one-electron injection, for radical cations
(Z= +1), LD forces significantly stabilize the folded
conformers, except for the ethylene derivative deprived
of supra-MOs. For radical cations equipped with supra-
MOs, the unpaired electron is delocalized over both
heterocycles through space. Cyclomer completion (Z=
0) upon the second electron transfer occurs according to
the inversion of redox potentials. This mechanism
explains why intramolecular reactivity is favored and
why pyridinium electrophores are not independent.
Cyclic voltammetry and bulk electrolysis
Cyclic voltammograms for a series of 5 molecules plus some background measurements. Bulk electrolysis files for 2 molecules and simulations of 1 molecule included.

metadata.title: A dataset of hyperspectral Look-Up Tables for 3.5 million traits and structural combinations of Central European temperate broadleaf forests
metadata.publisher: Ústav výzkumu globální změny AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset comprises 3.5 million unique combinations of leaf biochemical properties and canopy structural characteristics of forest scenes, along with various configurations of solar geometry. It provides spectral data spanning wavelengths from 450 nm to 2300 nm, with a 2 nm resolution. The dataset is divided into two files: one detailing the average reflectance of all scene pixels and another focusing specifically on sunlit leaf pixels. Look-up tables (LUT) were created using version 5.10.0 of the Discrete Anisotropic Radiative Transfer model. The virtual forest scenes are based on 3D models of European beech trees, derived from Terrestrial Lidar Scanning, and adjusted for different leaf area indices and structural configurations to simulate natural forest diversity. Reflectance data were processed using Matlab and Python scripts, resulting in hyperspectral cubes, which were subsequently used to generate the LUT. This dataset is valuable for training machine learning models aimed at retrieving forest functional traits and for calibrating remote sensing algorithms. Its high spectral resolution and diverse trait combinations make it adaptable to different times, locations, and both hyper- and multispectral sensors, supporting future hyperspectral satellite missions.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
The Sec61/TRAP Translocon Scrambles Lipids - NBD Fluorescence Quenching assay
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: The inter leaflet transport of phospholipids is a crucial physiological process that takes place in the cell plasma membrane. This process can be facilitated by specific proteins – scramblases – without the consumption of ATP. Experimental data, provided here, describe the  lipid scrambling activity of the Sec61/TRAP translocon. Sec61/TRAP has previously been described as a protein complex that maintainins the membrane transition of the nascent amino acid chain. In our work, we reconstituted Sec61/TRAP into large unilamellar vesicles and used experiments based on NBD fluorescence quenching by sodium dithionite or bovine serum albumin to investigate the scrambling activity of Sec61/TRAP. Both complementary approaches demonstrated lipid scrambling. Blocking the Sec61 translocation cavity did not affect scrambling, supporting the existence of an alternative scrambling pathway in the translocon complex.

Sec61/TRAP was reconstituted in Large Unilamellar Vesicles, containing 0.4 % (mol) of NBD labelled lipid. The labelled lipid is supposed to be distributed equally in both membrane leaflets. Once the Sodium Dithionite is added to experimental mixture, it quenches all the sterically available NBD molecules. Thus, observed fluorescence decrease to ~ 50 % of initial intensity. However, if there is present a scramblase, which maintains fast translocation of phospholipids from one to the other leaflet, the NBD fluorescence decrease to 0, in an ideal case. Because Sodium Dithionite is a small molecule which can theoretically penetrate the membrane via any pores, we replicated the experiments also with bovine serum albumin. This protein can quench 50 % of NBD fluorescence, and due to its size, it cannot penetrate the membrane. Experiments were performed in presence of different inhibitors, showing that sterical block of Sec61 cavity does not influence the lipid scrambling – such a result support the relevance of TRAP scrambling activity.

Large Unilamellar Vesicles (LUVs), composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC and 0.4% (mol) of 16:0- 12:0 NBD PS; or 0.4% (mol) of 16:0-06:0 NBD PC (Avanti), were prepared by extrusion. Chloroform solutions of POPC and NBD labeled PC or PS were mixed in a round bottom test tube and dried under a mild flow of nitrogen for 10 minutes. After that, dry lipids were incubated under vacuum overnight. Next day, lipids were hydrated in 500 μL of SECb buffer (50 mM Hepes, 300 mM KOAc, 10 mM MgCl2, 1 mM DTT, pH 7.5) for 30 min at room temperature and vortexed for 1 min. Multilamellar liposomes, present in the solution after vortexing, were extruded through a 200 nm membrane.


For the protein reconstitution in the membrane, we followed a procedure introduced by Brunner and Schenck. Specifically, 75 μL of LUVs were mixed with 37 μL of the protein, 15 μL of Triton X-100 (10 % (v/w)), and 248 μL of the SECb buffer. Thus, the final total lipid concentration was 800 μM, and the final protein concentration was 109.5 nM. This mixture was incubated at room temperature for one hour with a gentle mixing. Then, 100 mg of Bio-Beads SM2 (BioRad) was added, and the mixture was incubated overnight and gently mixed at 277 K. In parallel, the negative control was prepared in the same way, containing 37 μL of the SECb buffer instead of protein solution. The size distribution of LUVs, containing reconstituted translocon, was controlled using DLS (Zetasizer Nano S, Malvern Panalytical).

 

The dithionite assay was performed at 96 well plate (Nunc MicroWell 96-Well Optical- Bottom, Thermo Scientific) using Infinite M Nano+ (Tecan) plate reader. Samples containing the reconstituted proteins and negative controls were measured in the same run. For the experiment, 17 μL of the reconstitution mixture was added to 183 μL of SECb buffer. Light with a wavelength of 460 nm was used for the excitation, whereas the emission was detected at 535 nm. After temperature equilibration, the fluorescence intensity was measured in 20 s intervals for at least 180 s to acquire the initial plateau. Then, 30 μL of 0.3 M sodium dithionate was added to all samples including the negative controls. The fluorescence decay curves (F (t)) were normalized so that their maximum intensity was equal to 1. The same setup of the plate reader and the same composition of the initial reaction mixtures, as described for the dithionite assay, was used for the BSA assay. After temperature equilibration and acquirement of an initial plateau, 30 μL of fatty acid-free BSA (25 mg/mL) was added. The fluorescence curves were fitted with Eq. (4), as for the dithionite assay.

Infinite M Nano+ plate reader writes measured values to a MS Excel sheet. Here are available Excel files from 4 experimental series, which differ in membrane compositions and inhibitor presence. Every file contains sheets, according to experiments with different quenching approach or inhibitor presence. "dit" means Sodium Dithionite quenching, BSA means quenching with BSA, A is for Apratoxin A presence and I is for Ipomoeasin F inhibiton.

PC,PC,A.xlsx – LUVs composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC, 0.4% (mol) of 16:0-06:0 NBD PC, Apratoxin A inhibition

PC,PC,I.xlsx – LUVs composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC, 0.4% (mol) of 16:0-06:0 NBD PC, Ipomoeassin F inhibiton

PC,PS,A.xlsx – LUVs composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC and 0.4% (mol) of 16:0- 12:0 NBD PS, Apratoxin A inhibition

PC,PS,I.xslx – composed of 99.6% (mol) POPC and 0.4% (mol) of 16:0- 12:0 NBD PS, Ipomoeassin F inhibiton

Produced sheet contains heading with all parameters of the measurement – excitation / emission wavelength, gain, interval time, etc. Raw data are available in a table below the parameters of measurement. In this table, first column is for number of cycle, second for time, third for temperature and following columns represent fluorescence intensity of particular wells. This data was normalized and averaged – green table in every sheet. Also, the time gap for pipetting during the experiment was subtracted to keep the experimental time continuous. Final version of processed data for each experiment is available at sheet "Summary".

metadata.title: Bacteriophage-related epigenetic natural and non-natural pyrimidine nucleotides and their influence on transcription with T7 RNA polymerase
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: We have designed and synthesized a series of uracil 2ʹ-deoxyribonucleosides and 5ʹ-O- triphosphates (dNTPs) bearing diverse modifications at position 5. These included rare natural nucleotides occurring in some bacteriophages, i.e. α-putrescinylthymine, α-glutaminylthymine, 5-dihydroxypentyluracil, and methylated or non-methylated 5-aminomethyluracil, and related non-natural 5-sulfanylmethyl- and 5-cyanomethyluracil. Most of the dNTPs bearing basic substituents were moderate to poor substrates for DNA polymerases, but still useful in primer extension synthesis of modified DNA. Together with previously reported 5-hydroxymethyl, 5-hydroxyethyl, 5-ethyl and 5-acetylpyrimidine nucleotides, they were used for synthesis of diverse DNA templates modified in the sense or antisense of the promoter region and some of them also for templates modified in both strands. A systematic study of the in vitro transcription with T7 RNA polymerase showed a moderate positive effect of most of the uracil modifications in the non-template (sense) strand and some either positive or negative influence of modifications in the template (antisense) strand. The most interesting modification was the non-natural 5-cyanomethyluracil which showed significant positive effect in in vitro transcription.

metadata.title: CAMx simulations on BVOC impact over central Europe
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description:
Biogenic Volatile Organic Compounds (BVOC) emissions play an important role in atmospheric chemistry and ozone pollution. To assess their impact on air-pollution, regional chemistry transport models have to be applied that account for the transport, chemical transformation and deposition of pollutants. Here we present the results of CAMx simulations to quantify the impact of BVOC emissions on ozone (O3), formaldehyde (HCHO), hydroxyl radical (OH) and peroxy radicals near surface concentrations for central Europe over a decade-long period from 2007 to 2016. 

These data are the basis for the analysis presented by:
Liaskoni, M., Huszár, P., Bartík, L., Prieto Perez, A. P., Karlický, J., and Šindelářová, K.: The long-term impact of BVOC emissions on urban ozone patterns over central Europe: contributions from urban and rural vegetation, EGUsphere [preprint], https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2024-2027, 2024.  (check for final published version)

Data are based CAMx simulations over the period of 2007-2016 over a 9 km x 9 km horizontal resolution regional central European domain (189x165 gridpoints). These simulations were driven by WRF decadal simulations and anthropogenic emissions were taken from the MACC-III dataset. BVOC emissions were calculated based on WRF meteorology with LAI and PFT data extracted from MODIS satellite observations.

Multiple simulations were carried out to calculate the impact of: 1) all BVOC emissions 2) impact of only the urban BVOC emissions.
a) allBVOC - simulation with all BVOC emissions
b) noBVOC - simulation with no BVOC emissions
c) nuBVOC - simulation with only nourban BVOC emissions
d) uBVOC - simulation with only urban BVOC emissions
e)2nuBVOC - sensitivity simulation with double urban BVOC emissions
f)0.5nuBVOC - sensitivity simulation with half urban BVOC emissions
Data are provided for the following chemical species: O3, NO2, HCHO, OH, HO2 and RO2

Each file stands for one simulation and one species and contain hourly near surface data. Format of data: netcdf.

metadata.title: 3DZD: Protein structural embeddings of AlphaFold DB v4
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_titles.title: 214+ million protein structures encoded into a vector representation (size=120) for efficient analysis

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB (v4) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings), representing proteins based on their tertiary structure (the three-dimensional arrangement of the protein's amino acid chain). These embeddings are created using the 3D Zernike Descriptor (3DZD) method and capture information about the protein's 3D structure. Each protein is represented as a fixed-length vector of size 120. This vector representation allows for efficient computational analysis and comparison of proteins, bypassing the need for complex 3D structure alignment algorithms. The embeddings can be used for various bioinformatics tasks, including protein function prediction, structural similarity searches, protein-protein interaction prediction, and evolutionary relationship analysis. While powerful, these embeddings may not capture all aspects of protein function and may have biases based on the training data and methods used. Researchers and developers can leverage these embeddings for rapid protein analysis, drug discovery, and other computational biology applications, making them a valuable resource in the field of bioinformatics. These embeddings were created to be used in an efficient similarity search engine -- AlphaFind.

The embedding method was first published in "Real-Time Structure Search and Structure Classification" for AlphaFold Protein Models. The embedding uses 3D Zernike descriptor (3DZD), an emerging technique to describe molecular surfaces. The 3DZD is a series expansion of a mathematical three-dimensional function, and thus, a tertiary structure is represented compactly by a vector of coefficients of terms in the series. 3DZD is invariant to the rotation of the represented target object. The embedding implements 3D Zernike moments as a vector of length 120, which is also the length of this embedding.

The archive contains protein embeddings for more than 214 million proteins from AlphaFold DB. Protein data are divided into 25339 batches of approximately 10000 proteins in each. All data are stored in a parquet format and can be opened as pandas DataFrame. The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. For more info and to get in touch, see https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark. The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.

metadata.title: 3DZD: Protein structural embeddings of ESM Atlas
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB v4 (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings). The embeddings encode proteins by their tertiary structure. Additional information is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark).

The embedding method was first published in the paper Real-Time Structure Search and Structure Classification for AlphaFold Protein Models. The embedding uses 3D Zernike descriptor (3DZD), an emerging technique to describe molecular surfaces. The 3DZD is a series expansion of a mathematical three-dimensional function, and thus, a tertiary structure is represented compactly by a vector of coefficients of terms in the series. 3DZD is invariant to the rotation of the represented target object. The embedding implements 3D Zernike moments as a vector of length 120, which is also the length of this embedding.

The archive contains protein embeddings for more than 700-million proteins from ESM Atlas. Protein data are divided into 700 batches of approximately 1M proteins in each. All data are stored in a parquet format and can be opened as pandas DataFrame. The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. Note: There are approximately 250k proteins missing from the ESM Atlas. This is a known issue (https://github.com/facebookresearch/esm/issues/405). The list of missing proteins is in missing_proteins.txt. For more info and to get in touch see https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark. The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Mass measurement and separation of large unilamellar vesicles in SELINA instrument
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
'Sample 1', 'Sample 2' and 'Sample 3' are folders with data for latex beads (300 nm diameter) and large unilamellar POPC vesicles 
(nominal 200 nm diameter), which were electrosprayed (100 mM ammonium acetate water, +1.2 kV, 1.5-2 mm distance) and 
measured by Selected Ice Nanoparticle Accelerator (SELINA). Charge detector was used to record time transient of the amplified 
induced charge when a particle moves through the metal 5 cm pick-up tube of the charge detector. These transients were 
recorded as the MATLAB compressed MAT files ('beads non-resolving', 'vesicles non-resolving', 'vesicles resolving' folders). The 
raw data were processed to obtain m/z, charge and mass from each valid transient signal and are collected in corresponding CSV
files: 'beads non-resolving.csv', 'vesicles non-resolving.csv', 'vesicles resolving.csv'. The excel file 'Application records' contains 
data from processed transient signals within the software online, used mainly for the SELINA instrument operation and 
monitoring during the experiments, see Table 1 for details in README. There are also Python code files with examples for data processing.

Data for 'Selected ion flow tube studies of the reactions of H3O+, NO+, O2+• and O-• ions with alkanes in He and N2 carrier gases at different temperatures'
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
The kinetics of the reactions of H3O+, NO+, O2+• and O-• with n-hexane, 3-methylpentane, 2,5-dimethylhexane and 2,3-dimethylheptane were studied experimentally under several selected ion flow tube (SIFT) conditions: in a Profile 3 instrument in He and N2 at 300 K and in the Voice200 instrument N2 at 300 and 393 K – at extraction lens voltage 7V and 4V.

The Voice200 data are organised in Excel files named according to the compounds. The file names also indicate the temperature and the extraction lens voltage. In each file, there are sheets for different reagent ions containing the count rates of ions recorded at different times and different mass to charge ratios (indicated as mz columns). The branching ratios and the rate coefficients are given on separate sheets.

 The Profile 3 data are organised in a single file for all compounds.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Data for Mechanical response of monolayer graphene via a multi-probe approach
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This is a dataset supporting a published paper "Mechanical response of monolayer graphene via a multi-probe approach". Push-to-pull (PTP) testing is employed to probe the uniaxial tensile response of freestanding monolayer graphene. Various analytical approaches are employed to estimate the elastic modulus of end-clamped graphene samples, combining in-situ Raman spectroscopy and scanning electronic microscope (SEM) measurements. The utilization of spatially resolved Raman-derived strains for assessing the elastic properties of monolayer graphene leads to results consistent with previous experimental and theoretical values of the elastic modulus (approximately 1 TPa). Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of (pristine and defective) freestanding graphene sheets uniaxially loaded under varying clamping conditions are performed to support the experimental observations. The computational results indicate that the mechanical responses of the sheets are affected by the type, the spatial profile, and the heterogeneity of the clamping. When uniaxial pulling of end-clamped graphene is applied by a substrate adhering to the graphene sheet through van der Waals forces (as in PTP testing), the elastic modulus may be highly underestimated due to often inhomogeneous stress distribution and slippage processes. The MD simulations predict that the elastic modulus of pristine monolayer graphene is approximately 1 TPa, whereas its fracture strength can reach values of up to 110 GPa. Overall, this study underscores the limitations of traditional analyses of PTP experiments (utilizing indentation readouts and SEM imaging) and proposes new potential avenues (involving Raman measurements) for future research on the elastic properties of 2D materials.
Raman spectroscopy, push-to-pull indentation force-distance measurements, molecular dynamics

Push-to-pull force-distance curves are provided in txt files (ASCII) for all indentation cycles for samples #7, #8, F3, F7, F8 (notation the same as in the article). Raman spectra at each indentation cycle are provided in txt files (ASCII). Lorentzian fits for all the Raman spectra (D, G, and 2D modes) are provided in Excel files, summarizing all data for each sample in one file (on separate sheets). Data for molecular dynamics (MD) comprises scripts (*.lmp) that contain command lines to run MD simulations in LAMMPS,  the output files for each simulation with different setups, and the file for the AIREBO potential with modified cutoffs.

metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
This is a dataset supporting a published paper Interface and Morphology Engineered Amorphous Si for Ultrafast Electrochemical Lithium Storage. Ultrafast high-capacity lithium-ion batteries are extremely desirable 
for portable electronic devices, where Si is the most promising 
alternative to the conventional graphite anode due to its very high 
theoretical capacity. However, the low electronic conductivity and poor 
Li-diffusivity limit its rate capability. Moreover, high volume 
expansion/contraction upon Li-intake/uptake causes severe pulverization 
of the electrode, leading to drastic capacity fading. Here, interface 
and morphology-engineered amorphous Si matrix is being reported 
utilizing a few-layer vertical graphene (VG) buffer layer to retain high
 capacity at both slow and fast (dis)charging rates. The flexible 
mechanical support of VG due to the van-der-Waals interaction between 
the graphene layers, the weak adhesion between Si and graphene, and the 
highly porous geometry mitigated stress, while the three-dimensional 
mass loading enhanced specific capacity. Additionally, the high 
electronic conductivity of VG boosted rate-capability, resulting in a 
reversible gravimetric capacity of ≈1270 mAh g<sup>−1</sup> (areal capacity of ≈37 µAh cm<sup>−2</sup>)
 even after 100 cycles at an ultrafast cycling rate of 20C, which 
provides a fascinating way for conductivity and stress management to 
obtain high-performance storage devices.

Optical microscopy, scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy characterization, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements, Raman spectroscopy measurements, cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
The data are provided in raw data formats and/or Excel spreadsheets, folders are named accordingly to the accompanying figures, both for the main article and supporting information.

metadata.title: PCA-reduced GraSR: Protein structural embeddings of AlphaFold DB
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_titles.title: 214 millions of proteins encoded into a vector representation (size=50)

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB v3 (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings). The embeddings encode proteins by their tertiary structure. Additional information is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark).

This embedding was computed as PCA-reduction (with 99.9% of retained variance) of an embedding method called GraSR. The GraSR embedding method was first published in the paper Fast Protein Structure Comparison through Effective Representation Learning with Contrastive Graph Neural Networks. It utilizes Deep Graph neural networks to represent the 3D coordinates of protein structures. GraSR has been cross-validated on the SCOPe dataset and tested independently on a subset of proteins from PDB. TM-score is used as a reference benchmark. The original embedding is an average from 5 pre-trained networks of the same architecture, this embedding is created from the first network only (marked as fold-0 in GraSR implementation). The original embedding had a size of 400; the new reduced version has a size of 50.

The archive contains protein embeddings for 214M million proteins from AFDB v3. Protein data are divided into 25,339 batches of approximately 10,000 proteins in each. All data are stored in pickle format and can be opened as pandas Data Frame. To find a specific protein, use batch-to-protein-index.csv, which lists the contents of each batch.


The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. We benchmarked this embedding, assessing the correlation between protein structural information and embedding similarity. The metric used to assess structural similarity was a structure alignment method TM-Align; the embedding distance was computed as Euclidean (or Cosine) distance between the vectors. These results are available at https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark . The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.

metadata.title: LMI-10: Protein structural embeddings of AlphaFold DB
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_titles.title: 214 millions of proteins encoded into a vector representation (size=45)

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB v3 (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings). The embeddings encode proteins by their tertiary structure. Additional information is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark).

The embedding method, first published in Learned Indexing in Proteins: Substituting Complex Distance Calculations with Embedding and Clustering Techniques, proposed dividing the protein sequence into N consecutive sections – the positions of the atoms within each section are averaged, and the section is subsequently treated as a single point in space. Then, the distances between each pair of these sections are calculated, creating an incidence matrix. In this matrix, the embedding method prunes all the values exceeding a cutoff and normalizes the rest. The matrix is symmetrical, and all the diagonal values are 0. The half of this matrix (omitting the main diagonal) is then reduced into a single row. The resulting dimensionality of this dataset is then (N^2 - N) / 2. For this data, N=10 was chosen hence the resulting dimensionality is 45.

The archive contains protein embeddings for 214M million proteins from AFDB v3. Protein data are divided into 25,339 batches of approximately 10,000 proteins in each. All data are stored in pickle format and can be opened as pandas Data Frame. To find a specific protein, use batch-to-protein-index.csv, which lists the contents of each batch.

The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. We benchmarked this embedding, assessing the correlation between protein structural information and embedding similarity. The metric used to assess structural similarity was a structure alignment method TM-Align; the embedding distance was computed as Euclidean (or Cosine) distance between the vectors. These results are available at https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark . The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.

metadata.title: 3D-af-Surfer: Protein structural embeddings of AlphaFold DB
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description: The dataset contains proteins from AlphaFold DB v3 (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/) encoded into one-dimensional vectors (embeddings). The embeddings encode proteins by their tertiary structure. Additional information is available at our GitHub (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark).

The embedding method was first published in the paper Real-Time Structure Search and Structure Classification for AlphaFold Protein Models. The embedding uses 3D Zernike descriptor (3DZD), an emerging technique to describe molecular surfaces. The 3DZD is a series expansion of a mathematical three-dimensional function, and thus, a tertiary structure is represented compactly by a vector of coefficients of terms in the series. 3DZD is invariant to the rotation of the represented target object. The embedding implements 3D Zernike moments as a vector of length 120, which is also the length of this embedding.

The archive contains protein embeddings for 214M million proteins from AFDB v3. Protein data are divided into 25,339 batches of approximately 10,000 proteins in each. All data are stored in pickle format and can be opened as pandas Data Frame. To find a specific protein, use batch-to-protein-index.csv, which lists the contents of each batch.


The embedding is based on the protein's tertiary structure and can be used in search engines and similarity search applications. We benchmarked this embedding, assessing the correlation between protein structural information and embedding similarity. The metric used to assess structural similarity was a structure alignment method TM-Align; the embedding distance was computed as Euclidean (or Cosine) distance between the vectors. These results are available at https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/ProteinEmbeddingBenchmark . The repository also contains resources needed to encode arbitrary protein into this embedding.

metadata.title: AlphaFind: Discover structure similarity across the entire known proteome – data and model
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description: This repository contains the data used to create AlphaFind (https://alphafind.fi.muni.cz/), a web-based search engine that allows for fast structure-based search of the entire set of AlphaFold DB protein structures. First, we include compact (dimensionality=45) vector embeddings for 214M protein structures from the AlphaFold database (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/, available for download at https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold/tree/main/afdb). We used these embeddings for fast protein similarity searching, by quickly pre-filtering the candidate answer (214M → 1M structures). This is made possible by the grouping of proteins into 2000 similar clusters (the form in which the data is provided) using a classification neural network (provided as well). These compact vector embeddings as well as the grouping can be freely used and distributed and given the vector are suitable for processing by several data processing tools. For reproducing the results, see also our GitHub repository (https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/AlphaFind) with open-sourced code.

The embedding method, first published in Learned Indexing in Proteins: Substituting Complex Distance Calculations with Embedding and Clustering Techniques proposed dividing the protein sequence into N consecutive sections – the positions of the atoms within each section are averaged, and the section is subsequently treated as a single point in space. Then, the distances between each pair of these sections are calculated, creating an incidence matrix. In this matrix, the embedding method prunes all the values exceeding a cutoff and normalizes the rest. The matrix is symmetrical and all the diagonal values are 0. The half of this matrix (omitting the main diagonal) is then reduced into a single row. The resulting dimensionality of this dataset is then (N^2 - N) / 2. For this data, N=10 was chosen, hence the resulting dimensionality is 45.

Apart from the embedding itself, this dataset is pre-grouped into 2000 groups by structural similarity as identified by the K-Means algorithm. The group labels provided by K-Means are then, in conjunction with the embeddings, used to train a classification fully-connected neural network, which is then asked to predict the category of each embedding vector. The result is a fast way of navigating a vast landscape of protein structures using a single "predict" operation of the neural network.

The dataset has two components: the embeddings — embeddings.tar.gz, size of the compressed archive is 7.1Gb and 22Gb upon decompression and the weights of the classification neural network used to find the most similar category given an input embedding vector - model.pt

Embeddings: Upon uncompression (e.g. tar -xvf embeddings.tar.gz), a folder with 2000 pickle files will be created, each representing one category identified by the neural network. Each pickle file can be opened using the pickle library in Python. Each file is a Pandas DataFrame with Index corresponding to the protein's UniProt ID.

The neural network weights, training losses and state were exported using PyTorch library. For instructions to load them see our code repository -- https://github.com/Coda-Research-Group/AlphaFind

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Novel silicon nanoparticles-based carbonized polypyrrole nanotube composites as anode materials for Li-ion batteries
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: The development of elastic nanostructured Si-based anodes holds promise for advancing lithium-ion batteries due to the high theoretical specific capacity exhibited by Si. Combining nanostructured Si with C proves to be a successful strategy in addressing the challenges tied to the substantial volume expansion of Si during lithiation. In this work, a novel Si-based anode is fashioned through a simple and universal strategy, integrating Si nanoparticles with 1D nano-carbonaceous fillers (CPPy-NT) featuring nanotubular morphology and N atoms, and a water-based binder (poly(acrylic acid)). CPPy-NT are derived by carbonizing pre-synthesized polypyrrole nanotubes (PPy-NT). A clear correlation is established among the carbonization temperature for CPPy-NT preparation, N content in CPPy-NT, electrical conductivity, and the electrochemical performance of the ensuing Si/CPPy-NT anode. For comparative analysis, the electrochemical properties of the Si-based anode employing Super P (commercial carbon black) or PPy-NT are contrasted with those of Si/CPPy-NT. Notably, the Si/CPPy-NT anode with CPPy-NT bearing the highest amount of graphitic N sites exhibits a substantial improvement in initial charge capacity (approximately 2200 mAh g−1) and enhanced cycling stability. These findings underscore the potential to elevate the electrochemical activity of the C filler by carefully optimizing morphology, conductivity, and the incorporation of an appropriate amount of graphitic N.
SEM, TEM, XPS, Raman spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, galvanostatic charge/discharge, conductivity, electrochemical impedance
See experimental part in https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2023.233976.
