metadata.title: Protein embeddings and clustering used in AlphaFind - a protein structure similarity search tool
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description:
This repository contains compact (d=45) protein structure vector embeddings for 214M protein structures from the AlphaFold database (https://alphafold.ebi.ac.uk/), version 3 (raw data ↗️ made available by Deepmind in July, 2022). We used these embeddings to significantly reduce the search time in our AlphaFind web-based similarity search tool, designed to allow for fast protein similarity searching, by quickly pre-filtering the candidate answer (214M → 1M structures). This is made possible by the grouping of proteins into 2000 similar clusters (also a part of this dataset) using a classification neural network.

These compact vector embeddings as well as the grouping can be freely used and distributed and given the vector are suitable for processing by several data processing tools.

The embedding method, first published in ↗️Learned Indexing in Proteins: Substituting Complex Distance Calculations with Embedding and Clustering Techniques proposed dividing the protein sequence into N consecutive sections – the positions of the atoms within each section are averaged, and the section is subsequently treated as a single point in space. Then, the distances between each pair of these sections were calculated, creating an incidence matrix. In this matrix, the embedding method prunes all the values exceeding a cutoff and normalizes the rest. The matrix is symmetrical and all the diagonal values are 0. The half of this matrix (omitting the main diagonal) is then reduced into a single row. The resulting dimensionality of this dataset is then (N^2 - N) / 2. For this dataset, N=10 was chosen, hence the resulting dimensionality is 45.

Apart from the embedding itself, this dataset is pre-grouped into 2000 groups by structural similarity as identified by the K-Means algorithm. The group labels provided by K-Means are then, in conjunction with the embeddings, used to train a classification fully-connected neural network, which is then asked to predict the category of each embedding vector. The result is a fast way of navigating in a vast landscape of protein structures using a single "predict" operation of the neural network.

The dataset has two components:

  1. the embeddings — embeddings.tar.gz | size is 7.1Gb (compressed), 22Gb (uncompressed)

  2. the weights of the classification neural network (Multi-layer perception) used to find the most similar category given an input embedding vector - model.pt | size: 1.2Mb

Embeddings:

Upon uncompression (e.g. tag -xvf embeddings.tar.gz), a folder with 2000 pickle files will be created, each representing one category identified by the MLP (see ↗️ Methods).

Each pickle file can be opened using the pickle library in Python, e.g.:

import pickle
objects = []
with (open("class-0.pkl", "rb")) as openfile:
    data = pickle.load(openfile)

After opening using pickle, the data object is a Pandas DataFrame

import pandas
display(data.head(1))

the ↗️ index, which is the protein's UniProt ID and the row protein's vector representation.

Neural network weights were exported using PyTorch library, to load them:

1. Create an "empty" neural network of the same shape:

layers = torch.nn.Sequential(
	          torch.nn.Linear(45, 256),
	          torch.nn.ReLU(),
	          torch.nn.Linear(256, 128),
	          torch.nn.ReLU(),
	          torch.nn.Linear(128, 2000),
	      )

2. Load the weights:

nn.load_state_dict(torch.load(model.pt, map_location=torch.device('cpu')))

Self-Biased High-Responsivity Photodetector Based on Bi2SeTe2 Topological Insulator
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: This is a dataset supporting a published article. In this work we introduce a self-biased photodetector employing the photothermoelectric effect within a topological insulator, Bi2SeTe2. This device exhibits competitive visible-range photoresponse and high detectivity. Its intrinsic self-bias eliminates external power requirements, positioning it for low-power and remote-sensing applications, highlighting the potential of the photothermoelectric mechanism in optoelectronics.
Sample preparation, optical and atomic force microscopy characterisation, optical spectroscopy measurements, electrical characterisation, photodetector characteristics.
The data is provided in Excel spreadsheets. Separate folders are provided both for the main article and supporting information and are named according to the accompanying figures.

metadata.title: FUME user cases
metadata.publisher: Univerzita Karlova

additional_descriptions.description: FUME 2.0 - "Flexible Universal processor for Modeling Emissions" provides a flexible emission preprocessing tool for importing, interpolating, speciating and time-disaggregating various emission inventories to user defined spatial and temporal frames for chemistry transport model utilization (typically hourly data on a regular grid). Here, two user test cases are provided for the users: 1. "Czech" case covering the area of Czech republic that uses European and Czech emission repositories and  2. a "Prague" case for very high resolution (urban scale, street level) modelling.

User test cases supplied along with the FUME 2.0 emission preprocessor provided as two file archives that encompass the configuration, input data and ready-to-use folder structure for easy user testing. Input data are formed as a set of different available datasets adapted for the purpose of FUME software. For details on particular datasets see references.

The test cases is based on UNIX shell scripts and ASCII based configuration files. The source code of FUME is not part of the test case and is called externally by the script. PostgreSQL database with access information has to be provided in the configuration files. Datasets included in the user test cases are:
[1] EMEP Gridded emissions in 0.1°x0.1° long-lat resolution, https://www.ceip.at/the-emep-grid/gridded-emissions
[2] CAMS  https://permalink.aeris-data.fr/CAMS-REG-ANT, Kuenen, J., Dellaert, S., Visschedijk, A., Jalkanen, J.-P., Super, I. and Denier van der Gon, H. Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service regional emissions version 5.1 business-as-usual 2020 (CAMS-REG-v5.1 BAU 2020) Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service [publisher] ECCAD [distributor], doi.org/10.24380/eptm-kn40, 2021
[3] REZZO dataset provided by the Czech Hydrometeorological Institute, www.chmi.cz
[4] ATEM Traffic Emissions dataset provided by ATEM (Studio of ecological models), www.atem.cz

metadata.title: ChOP-CT: Quantitative morphometrical analysis of the Hindbrain Choroid Plexus by X-ray micro-computed tomography
metadata.publisher: Vysoké učení technické v Brně

additional_descriptions.description: Despite the irreplaceability of the cerebrospinal fluid-secreting HbChP in the development and homeostasis of the entire central nervous system, the research of this tissue has been neglected for decades. One of the obstacles is the lack of tools that describe its complex shape in the context of the 4th brain ventricle. Here, we introduce an effective tool - ChOP-CT - for the noninvasive, X-ray micro-computed tomography-based, 3D visualization and subsequent quantitative spatial morphometrics of developing mouse HbChP. ChOP-CT can reliably quantify parameters like volume, surface, length, outgrowth angle or shape variations. We also provide evidence that ChOP-CT is suitable for the unbiased evaluation and detection of the HbChP alterations within various mutant embryos. Thanks to its versatility and quantitative nature, ChOP-CT can precisely facilitate comparative analysis of the HbChP in mouse models. This will ultimately accelerate the screening of the candidate genes responsible for the regulation of plexus formation and mechanisms involved in the onset of various HbChP-related diseases.

Before micro-CT measurements, embryos were fixed (by 4% PFA) and dehydrated (by ethanol grade: 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90% EtOH). Then, embryos were stained by the Lugol´s solution (1% I2 in 90% MeOH). After the ethanol-based washes, the embryos were immobilized in 15 ml Falcon conical centrifuge tubes containing 1% agarose gel.  For the localization of the HbChP within the brain within the micro-CT data, we employed the contrast-noise-ration formula by Kraemer et al., 2015. For the landmark analysis, we took advantage of the Generalized Procrustes Analysis by Pulak Purkait, 2020.

The micro-CT datasets used for the HbChP segmentations and subsequent morphometrics were measured using GE Phoenix v|tome|x L 240 (Waygate Technologies, Baker Hughes Digital Solutions GmbH). Segmentation was performed via the MATLAB-based ChOP-CT script available at https://github.com/CEITEC-CTLAB. Manual segmentations of the HbChP lateral parts were done in Avizo 9.5 software (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Computation of the mean models was performed in VGStudio MAX 2023. Morphometric parameters presented in the table were measured by the ChOP-CT script or done manually in VGStudio MAX 2023. Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 9.

metadata.title: J. Heyrovsky Institute Community
Electrostatic Gating of Monolayer Graphene by Concentrated Aqueous Electrolytes
metadata.publisher: Ústav fyzikální chemie J. Heyrovského AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: A dataset supporting a published article entitled Electrostatic Gating of Monolayer Graphene by Concentrated Aqueous Electrolytes on Arbitrary Substrates. In this article, we study electrostatic gating of monolayer graphene using different aqueous electrolyte solutions. We find that while the initial doping state of graphene is affected by the type and concentration of the electrolyte, the rate of the doping of graphene with applied voltage, measured using in-situ Raman spectroelectrochemistry, is nearly constant. Furthermore, we compare different gating systems and show that the gating efficiency depends strongly on the gate capacitance, dropping as low as 70% for common electrolytes and applied voltage, and as low as 6% for common dielectric gates

In-situ Raman spectroelectrochemical measurements of CVD graphene electrodes in a microdroplet electrochemical cell using a range of different aqueous electrolytes; data analyses, peak fitting, and simulations; evaluation of the literature data; figure graphics.

The data are provided in Excel spreadsheets, Origin project files, IgorPro project files. Folders containing specific data for the graphics, including CorelDraw and Powerpoint files, are named accordingly to the corresponding figures, both for the main article and supporting information.

additional_descriptions.description: A dataset supporting a published article entitled Large-Area Mechanically-Exfoliated Two-Dimensional Materials on Arbitrary Substrates. In this article, we present a method exploiting gold-assisted exfoliation to prepare large-area monolayers of various layered materials, followed by their transfer to arbitrary substrates, is presented. This technique is used to fabricate field-effect transistors and photodetectors to demonstrate the suitability its for large-area optoelectronic devices.

Sample preparation, optical and atomic force microscopy characterisation, optical spectroscopy measurements, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements, electrical characterisation, photodetector characteristics.
The data are provided in Excel spreadsheets, folders are named accordingly to the accompanying figures, both for the main article and supporting information.

metadata.title: Raw data for: The selection of a hydrophobic 7-phenylbutyl-7-deazaadeninemodified DNA aptamer with high binding affinity for the Heat Shock Protein 70
metadata.publisher: Ústav organické chemie a biochemie AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description:
Hydrophobic 7-phenylbutyl-7-deaadenine-modified DNA aptamers were selected against the Heat Shock Protein 70 via PEX and
magnetic bead-based SELEX. After 9 rounds of selection, the pool was sequenced and a number of candidates were identified.
Following initial screening, two modified aptamers were chemically synthesised in-house and their binding affinity analysed by
two methods, bio-layer interferometry and fluorescent-plate-based binding assay. The binding affinities of the modified
aptam,ers were compared with that of their natural counterparts. The resulting modified aptamers bound with higher affinity
(low nanomolar range) to the Hsp70 than their natural sequence (> 5 μM) and hence have potential for applications and further
development towards Hsp70 diagnostics or even therapeutics.

additional_descriptions.description: Dataset supporting a published article entitled Renewable Energy Transition Facilitated by Bitcoin. Statistical analyses of global energy and electricity production and consumption, electrify consumption by different regions, technologies, and sectors, and electricity consumption by bitcoin mining in different countries.
The analyses are carried out using Excel spreadsheets, folders are named accordingly to the use in the article figures.

metadata.title: Raw image data for article "Role of casein kinase 1 (CK1) in the amoeboid migration of B cell leukemic and lymphoma cells"
metadata.publisher: Masarykova univerzita

additional_descriptions.description: Raw image data from cell tracking experiments published in article "Role of casein kinase 1 (CK1) in the amoeboid migration of B cell leukemic and lymphoma cells" (https://doi.org/10.3389/fcell.2022.911966). We have tracked migration of 4 different cell lines originating from chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) in vitro under agarose confinement. We have tested the effects of biochemical and genetic attenuation of casein kinase 1 (CK1) signalling on the migration parameters. We have also tested positive stimulation by chemokines (CCL19 and CXCL12) together with VCAM1 coating in this setup to promote the migratory behaviour of the non-migratory cell lines. Finally, we attempted to quantify polarized localization of endocytic vesicles in MEC1 cells.

These datasets were used for quantification of cell migration and morphology under confinement. A more detailed protocol for this assay have been published in the original article: https://doi.org/10.3389/fcell.2022.911966. In short, we have injected the cells under a layer of low melting agarose supplemented with culture medium and fetal bovine serum, to observe changes in cell morphology and migration in real time. The migration of the cells was tracked according to the position of their nuclei, which were stained using Hoechst 33342. For this reason, the cells were imaged in two channels in parallel: blue fluoroscence channel (used for actual tracking) and brightfield (visual reference). The images were acquired at 10s interval for the total of 60 frames. The image analysis and tracking pipeline is described in detail in the original article. For the analysis of vesicle distribution, we stained the cells using CellBrite Steady 650 (Biotium) a live-cell plasma membrane stain according to manufacturer's instructions and imaged in red fluorescence channel at 10s interval for the total of 40 frames. The protocols used for image analysis of this data is described in detail in the original article.

The image datasets used for cell tracking were acquired using widefield fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX83 inverted widefield microscope, U-HGLGPS light source, QImaging Retiga 2000R Mono camera) equipped with live imaging chamber (Okolab) set for standard culture conditions (37 °C, 5 % CO2). The samples were imaged using 10x magnification (CPLFLN PH objective, Olympus) in phase contrast and blue fluorescence channel in parallel (1 frame per 10 s), for the total of 60 frames. The image datasets for quantification of endocytic polarity were acquired using Leica DMI6000B inverted microscope in widefield mode equipped with EL6000 light source (Leica) and ORCA-Flash4.0 V3 Digital CMOS camera C13440-20CU (Hamamatsu) equipped with on-stage incubator (UNO-T-H-CO2, Okolab). The samples were imaged using 40x magnification (HC PL FLUOTAR L 40x/0.60 DRY objective, Leica Microsystems) at 10s interval for the total of 40 frames. Additional imaging parameters can be accessed from the metadata of the individual datasets. The data are provided as the original raw files (.vsi and .lif) exported from the imaging software, however the files including metadata can be opened using FIJI software (https://imagej.net/software/fiji/). In order to open the .vsi files successfully, the files must not be renamed and they need to be located in the same folder as their source folder of the same name (containing corresponding .ets file).

metadata.title: CT sken párátka
metadata.publisher: Vysoké učení technické v Brně

additional_titles.title: Toothpick CT scan

additional_descriptions.description:
Surová projekční data CT skenu dřevěného párátka s vysokým rozlišením. 
Sken byl proveden pomocí přístroje Rigaku nano3DX s kvazi-paralelním 
rentgenovým svazkem. Délka hrany pixelu v projekčních datech je 0.54 
mikrometru.

Raw projection data of a high-resolution CT scan of a wooden toothpick. The data were scanned on the Rigaku nano3DX with a quasi-parallel x-ray beam. The pixel edge length in the projection data is 0.54 micrometers.

The sample was scanned natively, without any contrast agents. The dataset is in absorption contrast. The sample is larger than the detector, so the central part of the toothpick was scanned as a region of interest.

The scan was performed on a Rigaku nano3DX X-ray microscope, using a quasi-parallel geometry.

X-ray source:
- target material: Copper,
- tube voltage: 40 kV,
- tube current: 30 mA.

Detector:
- projection size: 1648 (W) by 1250 (H) pixels (after 2×2 binning),
- pixel pitch of the detector: 0.54 micrometers (after 2×2 binning),
- output image datatype: little-endian unsigned 16-bit integer.

Geometry:
- Source-object distance: 275 mm,
- Object-detector distance: 6 mm,
- Scan range: 180°,
- Projection number: 802 (first projection scanned at 180°, and the next scanned in the 0°-180° range, inclusive),
- axis-of-rotation shift: +5.0 pixels.

Note:
The x-ray beam used in the scan is quasi-parallel, which means it has a very small amount of divergence. This results in only a slight cone-beam magnification of 1.02×. This quasi-parallel setup allows for conducting 180° scans without significant loss of information about the sample.

metadata.publisher: Ústav fotoniky a elektroniky AV ČR

additional_descriptions.description: Molecular dynamics simulations trajectories of microtubule in an intense electric field. Four electric field directions, two different field strengths, two different temperatures, and three different ion concentrations were explored. The data can serve further to researchers in the field of bionanotechnology, bioelectromagnetics and biophysics.

CUDA accelerated version of AMBER software with ff14SB force field in combination with the TIP3P water model for solvation was used to generate molecular dynamics trajectories. See more details in the linked full paper.

metadata.title: A set of peptides with B3LYP/6-31G*/NPA charges
metadata.publisher: CEITEC

additional_descriptions.description: This dataset contains a set of 60 di- and tripeptides with associated quantum mechanical charges.

All 3D conformers were downloaded as SDF files from the PubChem database, where they were generated by OEOmega. All structures were additionally manually prepared in Chimera as zwitterions (charges are assigned at pH 7) by removing hydrogen from the C-terminal α-carboxyl group and adding hydrogens to the N-terminal α-amino group. Peptides were chosen to contain all possible proteinogenic amino acids and to cover the range of integer charges, from -2, over neutral to +2.

The charges are of type B3LYP/6-31G*/NPA and were computed using Gaussian 09.

additional_descriptions.description: Měření průměrné teploty v Praze (hlavní město České republiky) během dubna, května a června 2018.
Measuring the average temperature in Prague (capital city of the Czech Republic) during April, May and June 2018
Teplota byla měřena každou hodinu a z hodnot byla vypočítána průměrná denní teplota.
